desperate:
The appointment was in forty minutes and Renata had already driven past the office building three times. Her hands were at ten and two on the wheel, knuckles pale, the radio off because she couldn't process sound and thought at the same time anymore. She'd been on the waitlist for eight months. Eight months of calling her sister at midnight, of sitting in her car in the work parking lot until she could arrange her face into something functional, of Googling "how to feel like a real person again" at 2 AM. When the clinic had finally called with an opening, she'd said yes before the receptionist finished the sentence.

She hadn't recognized the name until she Googled him. Dr. Daniel Marsh. The photo on the practice website was professional, reassuring—a man with a patient expression and wire-rimmed glasses. She'd stared at the photo for a long time before it clicked. Danny Marsh. Who used to wait for her by the water fountains in sixth grade. Who had a nickname for her that she could still hear in a specific pitch, a specific register, whenever she walked into a room full of strangers.

She pulled into the parking lot and killed the engine. She sat there. She pulled the paper intake form from her bag, where she'd already filled in her emergency contact, her insurance information, a summary of symptoms she'd written and rewritten four times so it didn't sound too alarming but also didn't undersell the past year. The paper was soft at the folds from being handled so much. She smoothed it against the dashboard.

The thing was, she had nowhere else to go. Her GP had given her a list of three providers accepting new patients. The first had a nine-month wait. The second didn't take her insurance. Dr. Marsh's practice had the opening. She'd already used one sick day coming here, and she had two left for the year. She looked at the building entrance. A woman came out holding a tote bag, looking ordinary, unchanged. Renata watched her get into a silver car and leave.

She got out. Her legs carried her to the door in a way that felt only loosely connected to any decision she'd made. In the waiting room, a small fountain burbled on a corner table. The receptionist smiled and asked her to take a seat. Renata sat with her intake form on her knees and her coat still buttoned and stared at the fountain and tried to remember how to breathe the way the app had taught her.

When the door opened and he appeared, she saw the recognition move across his face a half-second before he controlled it. He was the first to speak. "Renata." Not a question. He said her name carefully, like he was aware of its weight. He looked older than the photo. His expression gave her nothing to read. She stood up. Her intake form fell to the floor and she left it there, which she would think about later, how she hadn't bent down to pick it up, how she'd just looked at him across the waiting room and lifted her chin.

I found out the way you find out most things now, which is that someone posts it somewhere and eventually it reaches you whether you want it to or not. Cassie Holt, sharing a link in the neighborhood Facebook group of all places—our shared, excruciating hometown—writing *so proud of this guy!!* above a news article about a new counseling center opening downtown. There was his face. There was his name. Marcus Webb, Licensed Professional Counselor, specializing in adolescent trauma.

I read the phrase "adolescent trauma" four times in a row.

I have a sticky note on my bathroom mirror that my last therapist told me to put there. It says, in my own handwriting: *you are allowed to take up space.* I've been standing in front of that note every morning for six months since Dr. Avery moved her practice three states away, trying to find a replacement who isn't either a eight-month wait or completely out of my price range. I have a spreadsheet. It has seventeen names on it and notation in red text next to fifteen of them that says things like *not accepting new patients* or *out of network, $200/session.* I update it the way some people check the weather.

I clicked on the article about Marcus's new practice. I don't know what I expected to feel. I sat at my kitchen table at 7 AM with my coffee going cold and read about his sliding scale fees, his welcoming environment, his conviction that everyone deserves access to mental health support. I looked at the listed specialties. I looked at the phone number at the bottom of the page.

Here is what Marcus Webb did: he told everyone in our grade that I stuffed my bra with tissues. I did not. He said it so many times and with such confidence that I'm still not entirely sure people believed me when I denied it. I went through the better part of seventh grade answering to a name he gave me that I'm not going to write down. I know this is not the worst thing that has ever happened to a child. I know the word "trauma" is not mine to claim for this. But I also know that I still feel my face change when I walk into a room where I don't know anyone, still feel the threat of being named and reduced, still have that specific ancient flinch living somewhere under my sternum.

His practice had two slots open for new patients. I know this because

The voicemail said "effective immediately" and listed a forwarding address for correspondence. Maya played it three times, standing in the hallway outside Dr. Okafor's office, her back sliding down the wall until she was sitting on the floor with her knees pulled to her chest. The door had a paper taped to it—a printed notice about the transition of advising responsibilities. She'd eaten lunch with him two weeks ago. He'd asked about her dissertation chapter. He'd said *next time we talk, bring your outline*.

She called the department administrator twice, then sent an email asking if there was a going-away gathering she'd somehow missed. There hadn't been one. She typed a message to Dr. Okafor's university address and watched it bounce back immediately, his auto-reply informing her that he was no longer accepting correspondence through that account. She found his name in her phone and stared at his number for a long time, thumb hovering. She pressed call. It rang seven times before a recording told her the number had been disconnected. She sat very still with the phone pressed hard against her ear, listening to the silence on the line long after the recording ended.

Later she pulled up every email he'd ever sent her, going back four years, scrolling to the oldest ones from when she'd first joined the program. *Your instincts are strong. Trust them.* She read that line five times. She was supposed to defend her dissertation in six months. She had nobody else who understood what the work meant, what it cost, where it was trying to go. She closed the laptop and opened it again immediately. She opened a new document and started typing a letter she had no way to send.

I found out from someone else. That's the part I keep coming back to. Not from Professor Halliday himself, not from an email, not even from the department website—I found out because Tomas mentioned it casually over coffee, the way you'd mention that a restaurant had closed down. "Did you hear Halliday retired? Kind of sudden, right?" And I said yes, I'd heard, because I didn't know what else to do with my face.

I went back to my car and sat there for forty minutes. My hands wouldn't stop moving. I kept picking up my phone, setting it down, picking it up again. I checked his faculty page, which already had the word *emeritus* appended to his title like a small, quiet door closing. I thought about every conversation we'd had over the last three years, combing back through them for something I'd said, something wrong, some reason he might have chosen to simply disappear without a word to me specifically. I found his home address in an old letter he'd mailed me, the one with the recommendation he'd written in longhand on his personal stationery. I sat in the parking lot and looked at it. I'm not proud of how long I looked at it.

I have an audition in two weeks—the one he spent a year helping me prepare for. Every note I'd worked through with him sits in a folder on my music stand at home. I've been going to the practice room at six in the morning because I can't sleep, running the same passage until my fingers ache, listening for his voice in my head telling me where I'm going wrong. It's getting fainter. I can't let it get fainter. I went back to the practice room at eleven last night and played until someone knocked on the wall from the next room, and I stopped and sat in the dark with the violin in my lap and my chest doing something I couldn't name.

The book had been sitting on the display table at the front of the Barnes & Noble for three days before Miriam finally worked up the nerve to open it. Her own face stared back from the cover — a photo taken at her sister's wedding, cropped so her sister was gone. *Miriam Voss: A Life in Pieces.* She didn't recognize the author's name. She stood there with her coat still buttoned, holding the book with both hands, reading the back cover copy three times as the holiday shoppers moved around her like water around a stone.

She called her agent from the parking garage, pacing in tight circles between two concrete pillars. Her voice echoed. She said the title. She said the author's name. She described the cover photograph. Her agent put her on hold for four minutes and thirty-seven seconds — she counted — and came back sounding careful in a way that meant he had already known. "When were you going to tell me," she said, and it didn't come out as a question.

That night she sat on her kitchen floor with the book open across her lap, reading passages about her childhood, her father's drinking, the miscarriage she had never told anyone about except her doctor and one close friend who had since moved abroad. Whoever had written this had spoken to people. Had gone looking. The acknowledgments listed seventeen names, and she recognized four of them, and the recognition felt like touching something hot. She closed the book. She opened it again. She read the miscarriage paragraph a second time, a third time, and her breathing went shallow and strange.

She drove to her lawyer's office before it opened and waited in the parking lot with the engine running for forty minutes. When the receptionist unlocked the front door and found Miriam already standing on the mat, Miriam held up the book without saying anything. Her hands were perfectly steady. Everything else about her was not.

I found out on a Tuesday, the same way I find out everything I'm not supposed to know — late, by accident, through someone else. My college roommate texted me a link with three question marks and no other context. I clicked it on my lunch break, standing outside the sandwich shop with my food going cold in my hand, and read the Amazon listing twice before the words assembled themselves into meaning. Title. Author. Description. Publication date: eight months ago. Eight months.

I went back inside and sat down and did not eat the sandwich. I opened the book preview and read as far as it would let me, which was forty-three pages, and in those forty-three pages I found my mother's full name, my estranged brother's full name, the name of the town I grew up in, the name of the church, the specific year I stopped attending it, and a version of a story about my grandfather that was ninety percent accurate and ten percent wrong in a way that made the true parts feel stolen.

I called the publisher's general line and was transferred three times and put on hold twice and eventually spoke to a woman in the legal department who had the particular tone of someone reading from a prepared statement. "The author conducted extensive interviews," she told me. "The book is framed as an unauthorized biography." I wrote the word *unauthorized* on my napkin. I underlined it. I tore the napkin into small, even pieces while she kept talking.

That evening I posted nothing. I texted no one except my roommate, who asked if I was okay, and I sent back a thumbs up because I couldn't figure out what the honest answer looked like in emoji form. I pulled up the author's name and spent two hours reading everything he'd ever published, cataloguing every detail, building something in my head that felt like a case and also felt like a scream that hadn't found its way out yet. My coffee went cold. I made more. The cursor blinked in the search bar and I kept typing, kept clicking, kept pulling at the thread, because stopping felt like something I simply could not afford to do.

She found the manuscript by accident, wedged between the mattress and the headboard while she was changing the sheets. Three hundred and forty-two pages, printed and bound with a binder clip, her own name—slightly altered, *Marisol* instead of Maribel—on the first page. She read standing up, still holding the fitted sheet, for two hours. By page sixty she was sitting on the bare mattress. By page one hundred she was on the floor.

The woman in the novel was a liar. Not in small ways. In architectural ways, the kind of lying that had load-bearing walls. Dominic had written scenes she recognized—the argument at the lake house, the night she'd miscarried, the morning she'd found out about her mother's diagnosis—but he'd reconstructed them from the inside of her head in a way that made her stomach press against her ribs. He'd given her thoughts she'd never said aloud. Motivations she barely recognized and recognized completely.

She put the manuscript back exactly where she'd found it. She smoothed the new sheets over the mattress with particular care, tucking the corners the way her mother had taught her, military precision. Then she went to the kitchen and started dinner. She peeled four potatoes instead of two. She set the table. She folded the napkins into triangles. Dominic came home at six-fifteen and she asked him about his day and listened to his answer and refilled his water glass without being asked.

That night she lay beside him in the dark and stared at the ceiling while he slept, running through possibilities. She could leave. She could say nothing. She could ask him directly. She could read the last fifty pages she hadn't gotten to yet. She turned over all of these options methodically, the way you press on a bruise to map its edges, and when the clock read 3 AM she was still at it, her eyes wide open, her breath measured and careful and completely controlled.

She got up and went to the living room and called her sister in a different time zone. When her sister answered, groggy, she opened her mouth and what came out wasn't a sentence or even a question. It was a sound she'd never made before, low and pressurized, like something that had been stored under too much weight for too long finally being released.

I wasn't supposed to be on his laptop at all. My phone had died and I needed to look up the train schedule, just that, nothing more. But the document was already open, maximized on the screen, and I saw my name in the third paragraph before I could look away.

I missed the train. I missed the next one too. I sat at the kitchen table with my dead phone in my hand and read one hundred and eighteen pages of Garrett's novel, the one he'd told me was about a fictional couple in 1970s Buenos Aires, the one I'd never asked to read because he'd said it wasn't ready. The fictional couple lived in present-day Portland. The woman's name was Clara. My middle name is Clara. She had my laugh, my sleep habits, my specific and apparently notable way of leaving cabinet doors open. She had a version of my childhood I'd shared with Garrett in fragments, late at night, the kind of fragments you only offer to people you trust with the assembly.

When I heard his key in the door I closed the laptop. I went to the sink and ran the cold water and put my wrists under it and stood there.

"You're home early," he said.

"Missed the train."

He went to change his shoes. I heard him in the bedroom, the specific sequence of sounds I knew so well I could track his location through walls. My wrists were still under the water, which had gone from cold to lukewarm. I turned it off and dried my hands on the dish towel.

I needed him to tell me himself. I understood this with a sudden physical clarity, the way you understand certain things only when standing still. I needed there to be a version of this where he chose to say it—*I've been writing about us, I've been writing about you, I should have told you*—because if he said it himself then I'd know he understood what he'd taken and what it meant. I'd know he grasped the specific weight of it.

"Garrett," I called, and my voice came out completely level.

"Yeah?"

I picked up a pen from the counter. Set it down. Picked it up again.

"Come in here when you get a chance."

The cardboard boxes had been up there since before the closing, the realtor had mentioned it offhandedly, promised they'd be cleared out. They hadn't been. Maya found them on a Sunday in November, three weeks after the bank had sent the second notice, the one with the red text across the top. She'd gone up to the attic looking for the old copper piping she'd heard could be sold for scrap. Instead she found twelve boxes, taped and labeled in someone else's handwriting. Her hands moved fast, tearing through tape, pulling out contents and cataloguing them the way she used to catalogue inventory at the store she no longer had. Kitchenware, old clothing, paperback novels. Her jaw tightened. She kept going. Fourth box: a camera in a hard case. She held it up to the single bulb, turned it over. It was a film camera, but the case itself, the brushed aluminum, the brand. Her phone came out before she'd even set it down, fingers moving across the screen to an auction site. The listing she found made her sit down hard on a dusty trunk.

The trunk was locked. She found a flathead screwdriver and worked at the latch for eleven minutes straight, not stopping when it bit into her palm. Inside: a quilt, handstitched, the kind that appeared in listings on antique sites for four hundred dollars and sold in twenty minutes. Under it, a set of cast iron cookware still in its original box, never opened. Under that, a tin container she almost set aside. She opened it and found currency inside, foreign bills she couldn't identify, and below those, a folded document in a language she didn't recognize, and below that, a photograph of a family standing in front of a house that took her a long moment to place. It was her house. Her hands were not quite steady as she smoothed the photograph against her knee, studying the faces of strangers who had lived inside these walls and left all of this behind without explanation. She sat with it for a while. Then she opened her phone again and started making a list.

I found the first one by accident. I was measuring the attic for insulation quotes, holding the tape out with one hand and writing numbers on my forearm in marker because I'd forgotten a notebook, when I stepped around a support beam and nearly kicked a ceramic urn about the size of a shoebox. I put down the tape measure. I stood there. I picked it up. It was sealed, and it was heavy, and some part of my brain that I'd been arguing with for three weeks went very quiet.

My brother had stopped returning calls. The surgeon's office wanted a decision by Friday. I had been sleeping on the couch because lying in the bedroom, in the bed, made the ceiling feel too close. There were $6,000 and 72 hours standing between me and a conversation I wasn't ready to have. And here in my own attic, in the home I had bought six months ago in a neighborhood I didn't yet know, were things that didn't belong to me. I counted, eventually. Three urns, two plastic storage tubs sealed with tape that had gone brittle, a wardrobe box with a woman's name written on the side in a way that suggested someone had written it while crying, and a wooden crate with foreign postage stamps still stuck to its lid. I opened the crate last. Inside, individually wrapped in old newspaper, were pieces of silver. Actual silver, the kind you look up and find pages of results for. My mouth went dry. I sat down on the attic floor with my back against the beam, holding a silver candlestick in both hands, listening to the house settle around me in the cold.

The app was still open on his phone. Duolingo, forty-seven day streak, and the language selected was Tagalog. Marisol stood in the kitchen holding Marco's phone with both hands, not moving. She'd picked it up to check the time while he was in the shower. The water was still running. She had maybe four minutes.

She scrolled through the lesson history. Greetings. Family words. Then phrases she recognized from her own childhood: *Huwag mo akong iwan.* Don't leave me. *Mahal kita.* I love you. *Saan ka pupunta?* Where are you going? The lessons were dated back eight months, which meant he'd started the same week she'd told him she was thinking about moving back to Manila.

The shower turned off. Marisol set the phone face-down on the counter exactly where she'd found it, then gripped the edge of the sink. Her knuckles went white. She heard him moving around in the bathroom, the familiar sounds—the medicine cabinet, the towel rack—and her throat closed.

He came into the kitchen in a t-shirt and damp hair, smiling at her. She pointed at the phone. She didn't plan to. Her arm just extended, finger aimed, and she watched his face change as he understood.

"I was going to tell you," Marco said.

"Eight months." Her voice came out wrong, stretched thin. "Eight months and you said nothing."

"I wanted to be good enough first." He reached for her hand. "I wanted you to hear me say something to your grandmother and have it actually work. I wanted—" He stopped. "I didn't want you to leave and feel like you were choosing between two different lives. I thought if I could just—"

Marisol pressed her fingers over her mouth. She was not going to cry in the kitchen at seven in the morning. She was not.

"Say something," she said. "In Tagalog. Right now, say something."

Marco's pronunciation was imperfect and a little slow. But she understood every word.

I found the notebook under a stack of his architecture journals, which I wasn't snooping through—I was looking for a pen. That's the thing I keep telling myself, as if it matters, as if it changes what I saw written there in Tomáš's careful block letters: columns of Czech vocabulary, phrases with their English translations penciled beside them, pages and pages of his handwriting practicing words I grew up with.

*Zůstaň.* Stay.

*Prosím.* Please.

*Neopouštěj mě.* Don't leave me.

I've been sitting on the bedroom floor for twenty minutes. My back is against the bed and the notebook is open in my lap and I can't put it down. We've been fighting for three months about whether I should take the Prague fellowship. I keep saying he doesn't understand what it would mean to me to go back, to work in my own country again, and he keeps saying he supports me but his face does something terrible when I bring up departure dates.

He's been learning my language in secret. He's been sitting in this apartment practicing how to ask me not to go, in words he thought would reach me better, and he said nothing, and I said nothing, and we've both been drowning in this two feet apart.

I hear his key in the door.

My heart is going so fast I can feel it in my wrists. I look down at the page. *Jak dlouho ještě čekat?* How much longer to wait?

I don't close the notebook before he walks in.

The notification sat at the top of her screen like a splinter she couldn't stop pressing. Maya Kowalski. The name alone made Renata's throat close up the way it used to in fourth grade when she'd hear footsteps behind her in the hallway. She accepted the request before she'd consciously decided to, her finger moving on its own, and then sat very still staring at what she'd done.

She was three months behind on rent and had spent the last two weeks emailing every former contact she had, looking for freelance accounting work, any accounting work. Maya Kowalski was now a regional director at Hartwell Financial. Renata had seen it on the profile before she'd even registered the face in the photo.

She typed a message. Deleted it. Typed another. The version she finally sent was four sentences long and had taken forty-five minutes to write, and she read it back to herself afterward and felt her face go hot. *It's been such a long time. I saw you're at Hartwell now—I'm actually looking for contract work in financial auditing. Would love to reconnect and hear what you're up to.* The exclamation point at the end looked obscene to her now. She added it anyway.

Maya had told everyone in their class that Renata smelled like old soup. She'd organized a petition—an actual handwritten petition—to have Renata moved to a different lunch table. Renata could still picture the notebook paper, the signatures in purple gel pen. She was now watching Maya's profile photo for signs of the same person and finding just a polished woman in a blazer, smiling at a company event.

The reply came at 11 PM. *Renata! Of course I remember you. We actually have a contractor position opening up—I'll have someone reach out.* Three sentences. An emoji. A period at the end of everything, even the emoji. Renata read it eight times, moving her lips slightly on the last few reads. Then she went to the bathroom and ran cold water over her wrists for a long time, watching it spiral down the drain, not sure what she was feeling or whether it had a name she wanted to use.

I found the request on a Tuesday, which is my worst day because Tuesdays are when the weekly status meeting happens and I've now missed four of them in a row and my manager has stopped sending follow-up emails, which is somehow worse than if she kept sending them. I was lying on the couch still in my work clothes from the day before when I picked up my phone and there he was. Derek Paulson. Round face, good jaw now, holding a fish he'd caught somewhere green and sunny.

I put the phone face-down on my chest and stared at the ceiling for a while. Derek Paulson used to steal my lunch money and then tell the teacher I'd lost it. He did it so consistently that the teacher eventually told my mother I had a problem keeping track of my belongings. I was eight. My mother bought me a wallet with a zipper.

I accepted the request. I'm not going to explain that part because I don't fully understand it myself, except to say that I'd been lying on that couch for six hours and I was already on my phone and my thumb just moved.

Here is what I know about Derek Paulson now: he runs a landscaping company with thirty employees, he has two kids with a woman named Bridget, he coaches Little League, and his company does corporate contracts. I know this because I spent the next hour reading every post he'd made going back two years, and then I sent him a message that said I'd seen he did corporate grounds maintenance and that I worked in facilities procurement. The second part is technically true. I do work in facilities procurement. I have not opened my laptop in eleven days and my inbox has 340 unread messages and my keycard still works, as far as I know.

He responded with a phone number and a voice message that I've listened to six times. His voice is ordinary. It doesn't sound like anything. I've written out what I want to say when I call him back, bullet points on a sticky note, and I keep moving the sticky note to different surfaces around the apartment—the kitchen counter, the bathroom mirror, next to the couch—as if the right location will make the call easier to start.

Maya had called in sick to work three days running, spending those days cross-referencing LinkedIn profiles, archived company newsletters, and the screenshots she'd saved of every conversation where Dani had mentioned her supposed role as a senior architect at Harwell & Cross. The firm's actual employee directory was public. Dani's name wasn't in it. Hadn't been in it, as far back as the Wayback Machine would take her. Maya had a notebook now, filled with handwriting that had grown progressively less legible as the pages went on.

She showed up at Dani's apartment without calling ahead. When the door opened, she couldn't get the words out in the right order. "The Riverside project," she finally said, holding up her phone with the archived press release. "I read the whole thing. Every name. Every contractor. Every subcontractor." Dani looked at the phone, then at Maya's face, and something behind her eyes went very quiet.

The truth arrived in pieces. Dani was a draftsperson, not an architect. Had been for eleven years. The firm existed; her position did not. She'd been promoted in conversation long before she'd been promoted in reality, and then it had just kept going, snowballing, until Maya's loan application had listed Dani as a professional reference with a title that meant nothing.

Maya sat down on the hallway floor without being invited in. Her loan was already in processing. She had quit her job based on Dani's assurances that the reference would hold up, that the money would come through, that everything was solid. She pressed her back against the wall and stared at the water stain on the ceiling across from her and said, very quietly, "How long did you think this could go on."

I found out on a Tuesday, which seems like the wrong day for something like this. Tuesdays are supposed to be nothing days.

I'd been at the unemployment office for two hours before I even checked my phone. My severance had run out the week before, and the woman behind the desk kept asking me to list my professional references in order of relevance, and when I typed in Marcus's name and his title—Senior Compliance Officer, Drexel Financial Group—she looked at her screen for a long moment and then looked at me with an expression I couldn't immediately read. She turned the monitor toward me. The Drexel Financial Group website listed its entire compliance team. There was no Marcus Webb anywhere on it.

I called him from the parking lot. My voice came out flat and measured in a way that didn't match what was happening in my chest. "Tell me what you actually do," I said, when he picked up. There was a pause I will not forget for a long time. "Tell me your actual title." He started with excuses. I cut through them. I had nowhere to be now. I had nothing but time and a dead reference and a list of bills I'd been holding off because I was sure, so sure, that the check from my last employer's transition package would be released once HR confirmed my references. I'd staked everything on that confirmation.

He works in data entry. Has for six years. He told me this in a voice that expected forgiveness, and I stood in that parking lot with the sun doing nothing useful and listened to him explain himself and felt the ground underneath me become a thing I could no longer fully trust.

The email from Professor Hartley arrived at 11:47 PM, and Maya read it six times before her legs stopped working properly. She sat down on the floor of her dorm room, back against the bed frame, because the chair felt too far away. Academic integrity hearing. Monday morning. Bring documentation. She had documentation — drafts, browser history, the library database receipts with timestamps — but the hearing was in thirty-six hours and the department office was closed and she didn't know if documentation like that would even count as documentation. She pulled up her laptop and started a new document, typing everything she remembered about writing the paper: the date she'd started, the sources she'd found first, the paragraph she'd restructured four times because the argument wasn't landing right. She typed until 4 AM. She deleted it all and started over.

Her mother picked up on the first ring when Maya called at seven, which meant she'd been awake already, which meant someone had told her. "The scholarship," Maya said, and couldn't finish the sentence. Her mother was quiet for a long moment. "We'll figure it out." Maya pressed her forehead against the cold window glass and looked out at the campus quad, at the building where she'd spent the last three years doing everything exactly right. She had a 3.9. She tutored other students in the subject she was now accused of cheating in. The irony of it sat in her chest like something she'd swallowed wrong.

She showed up to Professor Hartley's office hours two hours early and waited in the hallway, sitting cross-legged on the floor with her printed drafts organized in a manila folder, her annotated bibliography paper-clipped to the front. When two other students arrived for actual office hours and looked at her curiously, she smiled and said nothing. She'd written out what she wanted to say on the back of a receipt — key points, in order, so she wouldn't lose the thread if her voice started shaking. The receipt was damp by the time the office door opened. She smoothed it against her knee and stood up.

I keep starting sentences and not finishing them. That's what I notice first — that I'll begin to explain the situation to someone and then stop halfway through because I can hear how it sounds. *I didn't plagiarize anything. The similarity is a coincidence. I've never even read that article.* All of it true. None of it, apparently, enough.

The academic integrity board meets in a room on the fourth floor of Whitmore Hall that I've walked past hundreds of times without ever looking at. I looked it up on Google Maps last night. I zoomed in on the satellite view like that would tell me something useful about what happens inside it. I have three character references from professors, a seventeen-page paper trail, and the login records from every academic database I accessed while writing the essay. I also have the original article — the one I supposedly copied — which I printed out and highlighted in yellow everywhere it overlaps with my work, then highlighted in green everywhere it doesn't, which is most of it. I've been sleeping about two hours a night. My roommate asked if I was sick and I said yes, because it was the fastest answer.

What nobody tells you is that being innocent doesn't feel like anything clean or certain. It feels like standing in a room where the walls are slightly closer than they were yesterday, making a list of every decision you made correctly and wondering if the list will be long enough. My financial aid review is scheduled for the week after the hearing. I know the order of operations. I know exactly what falls first and what falls after. I catch myself doing the math in the dining hall, in the shower, in the three minutes between hitting snooze and the alarm going off again — the math of what I lose if the board decides the evidence doesn't matter. I stop doing the math because I need to finish the character reference request email to Dr. Okafor, who hasn't responded yet, and I need to respond before she does because I want to make sure I've explained the timeline clearly, and if I'm going to explain the timeline clearly I should probably reprint the annotated—

I start the email over. I've started it over four times. The fifth version is better. I send it before I can read it again.

The eviction notice was still on the kitchen table when Margaret got to school that morning, the corner of it visible under her coffee mug like a tongue sticking out at her. She'd already called the district office twice about her delayed paycheck and left a voicemail with her landlord that she'd immediately regretted for how her voice had sounded—too high, too fast, too much. She was forty-three years old and had seventeen dollars in her checking account. She kept her classroom door closed during lunch now because she couldn't afford to go to the diner with the other teachers anymore and didn't want to explain why.

It was Tyler Meadows who knocked on the door, which she almost didn't answer. He'd been in her tenth-grade English class four years ago—a quiet boy who filled the margins of his essays with small drawings of birds. She hadn't thought about him in months. He was holding a manila envelope and he said her name in the doorway like he wasn't sure she'd remember him, and she had to work to keep her face from doing something embarrassing.

Inside the envelope was a card, a folded letter, and a check. She read the letter standing up because sitting down felt like it would make it too real. He'd sold three paintings—actual paintings, in an actual gallery—and one of them, he wrote, was a piece he'd made based on a prompt she'd given the class in 2019: *Write about a place that changed you.* He'd turned it into a canvas. The buyer had paid two thousand dollars for it. Half was hers, he said. He'd been trying to find her address for weeks.

She sat down then. She put her palm flat on her desk and stared at the number on the check. Tyler was still standing in the doorway, turning his car keys over and over in his hand, saying something about how the prompt had always stayed with him, how he thought she should know. Her throat had closed in a way that made answering difficult. She nodded several times instead of speaking, and when she finally managed to thank him, she was already looking back down at the check, reading the number again as though it might be different the second time, or the third.

I found the package on my desk between first and second period, no note on the outside, just my name in handwriting I didn't recognize. I almost didn't open it right away. I was in the middle of recalculating whether I could push the car insurance payment another two weeks, running the numbers in my head for probably the eighth time that morning, like a different answer was going to surface if I just kept trying. My husband and I had stopped eating dinner together because neither of us could talk about the medical bills without it turning into something else entirely.

Inside the package was a leather journal—expensive, the kind with the stitched binding that lies flat when you open it—and a folded card. The handwriting inside belonged to Priya Anand, who had sat in the third row of my sophomore composition class six years ago and argued passionately about semicolons with a conviction I still thought about sometimes. She was a published author now, apparently. Her first novel had just come out. She'd written in the card that a journal entry assignment I'd made her do every week had started the whole thing, that she'd been looking for a way to say thank you, and that she hoped I was well.

I stood there holding the journal while my second period students filed in around me. One of them asked if I was okay. I told him yes and set the journal in my bag and started writing the day's agenda on the board. My handwriting came out smaller than usual—I noticed that—cramped and careful, like I was trying to take up less space than normal. After class I took the journal back out and held it for a long time before I looked up Priya's book online, and then I sat in my empty classroom and read the acknowledgments page, which thanked, among others, a tenth-grade English teacher who made her write in a journal every single week, no excuses. I closed my laptop. I opened it again. I read the page a second time.

The interview was at two o'clock. Maya had known about it for three weeks, and she had spent most of those three weeks watching Daniel. Watching the way he laughed with the regional director at the coffee machine. Watching him arrive twelve minutes earlier than everyone else, which meant he was arriving nine minutes earlier than her. She had started setting her alarm for 5:45 AM. She had started laughing louder.

The folder in Maya's lap contained thirty-one pages. A revised proposal for the eastern distribution restructuring, a breakdown of her client retention numbers going back four years, three letters of recommendation she had personally requested and then coached, gently, carefully, over several phone calls. Her handwriting covered the margins in blue ink. She had been up until 1 AM adding a color-coded appendix that nobody had asked for.

Daniel emerged from the conference room at 2:47 PM with his jacket still buttoned and his face giving nothing away. Maya watched him walk back to his desk. He sat down. He put on his headphones. She realized she had been gripping the arms of her chair hard enough that her knuckles had gone white, and she made herself let go, finger by finger, before they called her name.

In the interview, when the regional director asked where she saw herself in five years, Maya opened her mouth and something entirely unplanned came out: "Here. In this role. I've thought about very little else for the past two years." There was a brief silence. She heard herself fill it. "What I mean is—I've been preparing. Specifically and deliberately. I can show you." She was already reaching for the folder.

I stopped sleeping around the time they announced they were considering both of us. That's not quite accurate—I still went to bed, still set my alarm, still went through the whole performance of it. But I'd lie there doing the math. Marcus had been here eight months longer than me. Marcus had played golf with the VP in September. I'd never played golf in my life. At 3 AM I googled "how quickly can an adult learn golf" and then I closed the tab and stared at the ceiling and opened it again.

I started sending emails at strange hours. Not to anyone important, not at first—just to establish the timestamp, to make sure it showed up in the system that I'd been working. I CC'd myself on things that didn't require it. I began keeping a log of every task I completed, formatted neatly in a shared document that my manager could access if she ever thought to look. She probably never looked.

The thing I'm least proud of is the lunch. Marcus suggested we get sandwiches together, the way we always had on Fridays, and I said yes, and I sat across from him and asked careful questions about his weekend and laughed in the right places. Meanwhile I was turning over everything he said, checking it for useful information, feeling my own smile from the outside like something I was operating remotely. He's not a bad person. I kept thinking that while I smiled. He's genuinely not a bad person.

I was in the bathroom washing my hands before the final decision meeting when I noticed I'd been running the water scalding hot without registering it. My hands were red to the wrist. I turned the tap off and stood there looking at them under the fluorescent light, and I had this sudden, very clear thought: I don't know when this started feeling like survival. I straightened my collar. I went back out.

Marcus had been a starting quarterback for eleven years. Now Coach Reyes was sitting across from him in a beige office that smelled like old carpet, using words like "transition" and "opportunity" and "better fit for the team." Running back. They wanted him to play running back. Marcus nodded slowly, the way you nod when the words aren't fully landing yet. He'd thrown for over three thousand yards last season. His whole identity—the thing his father had driven him four hours to practice for every Saturday morning since he was nine—lived in his arm. That night he went back to the empty field alone, no one watching, and threw passes to no one for two hours straight. His shoulder ached. He threw anyway. He called his college coach at midnight, the one who'd recruited him specifically for his throwing mechanics. "Just tell me I'm still that guy," he said, and immediately regretted it. The silence on the other end lasted too long.

He showed up the next morning before anyone else, cleats still wet from the dew-covered grass, and asked the equipment manager to open the film room. He watched footage of himself for three hours straight—not the bad games, the good ones, the great ones—pausing and rewinding the same throws over and over. When the other players started arriving, he was still sitting there in the dark, his untouched coffee cold on the desk beside him.

I've been a setter my whole career. Six years on this team, two championships, three all-conference selections. So when Coach pulled me aside after Thursday's practice and said she was moving me to libero, I laughed. Actually laughed, out loud, in her face. She didn't laugh back. I called my agent from the parking lot, pacing back and forth between the same two painted lines until I'd worn a path in my attention. He said there wasn't much I could do contractually. I hung up and called my mom, then hung up before she answered because I didn't know what I would say.

The thing nobody tells you is how physical it feels, news like that. My chest kept doing something strange all weekend, this tight fluttering like a trapped bird. I couldn't eat. I reorganized my equipment bag four times. I texted three former teammates who'd been through position changes, asking questions I already knew the answers to, just needing someone to stay on the line with me. By Sunday night I was researching libero statistics across the whole league, filling a notebook with numbers that made no particular argument, that proved nothing, but that my hand wouldn't stop writing down.

The letter had been sitting on Marina's kitchen table for six hours. She'd read it eleven times. She knew this because she'd made a small tally mark on the back of an envelope each time, a habit from her years of trying to track her own spiraling thoughts. The handwriting was Donna's—unmistakably Donna's, that looping cursive she'd once spent a whole afternoon trying to imitate at age fourteen. *I've wanted to write this for so long. I am so deeply sorry for what I did to you that summer.* Marina had the phone in her hand before she realized she'd picked it up. She put it down. She picked it up again. Donna's number was still in her contacts, unchanged, the same area code from their hometown. Outside the window, the neighbor's sprinkler system kicked on with a mechanical hiss. Marina watched the arcs of water catch the evening light and thought about how she'd told her therapist last month that she was fine, that the past was the past, that she had genuinely moved on. Her thumb hovered over the call button. She pressed it.

The call rang four times and went to voicemail. Marina set the phone face-down on the table and then immediately flipped it back over. She pulled up her email and started typing Donna's name into the search bar, looking for any thread between them, any prior contact, any trail she might have missed. There was nothing after 2009. She opened a new email, typed *I got your letter* in the subject line, and then sat there for a long time with her fingers resting on the keys without pressing them. Her tea went cold. She didn't notice. She opened a drawer and took out the letter again, as if the words might have rearranged themselves into something that required less of her.

I found the letter wedged between a power bill and a pizza coupon, which felt like exactly the kind of thing that would happen to me. I recognized the return address before I recognized the name—the street in Galveston where I'd spent the worst six months of my life at twenty-three. I stood at the mailbox and read it right there in the drizzle, not moving even when a car pulled into the driveway and my upstairs neighbor had to say excuse me twice before I stepped aside.

I called my sister first. She didn't pick up. I called her again and stood in the kitchen with the refrigerator open, not taking anything out, just letting the cold air run over my hand. I'd spent four years trying to get this man to understand what he'd done. Four years of unreturned emails, of a formal complaint that went nowhere, of a meeting with HR that left me feeling smaller than when I'd walked in. I'd stopped talking about it somewhere around year two because I could see people's eyes glaze over, could feel them quietly deciding I needed to let it go. And I had. I'd let it go so completely that I'd built a whole version of myself around the letting go.

The letter was two pages. It named specific things. It used the word *wrong* without any qualifiers attached to it. I read it again sitting on the kitchen floor with my back against the cabinets, and when my sister finally called back I answered and then couldn't remember what I'd been planning to say. "He wrote me a letter," I told her. "He actually—he wrote me a letter." My voice sounded strange, thin and frayed at the edges, like something that had been pulled in too many directions for too long and was only now being released. I didn't know if that was better or worse. I genuinely did not know.

The phone was still warm from her daughter's hand when Renata found it under the couch cushion. She'd only been looking for the TV remote. The screen lit up at her touch, and there it was—an account under a name she didn't recognize, profile picture cropped so the face wasn't quite visible, but the jaw, the earrings, the particular way her daughter tucked her hair behind one ear. Renata's chest went tight. She sat on the floor right there, not making it to the couch, scrolling through posts that went back fourteen months. Fourteen months. Her daughter had been fourteen when this started. Some of the comments made her vision blur at the edges.

She took screenshots of everything until her thumb ached. She emailed them to herself, then forwarded the email, then saved the screenshots again to a separate folder she created and labeled and relabeled. She wasn't sure what she was building a case for or who would ever need to see it. She just couldn't stop. She made a list on the back of a grocery receipt—therapist, school counselor, the non-emergency police line, a number she'd once seen on a pamphlet about online safety. She wrote so hard the pen tore through the paper at the bottom.

Her daughter came downstairs asking about dinner, and Renata looked up at her from the floor, surrounded by the grocery receipt and her own phone and her daughter's phone, and tried to arrange her face into something that wouldn't shatter everything immediately. "In a minute," she said. Her voice came out wrong—too even, too careful, like someone walking on ice they already knew was cracked. Her daughter paused on the bottom step, and in that pause Renata gripped her own phone so hard the case made a small popping sound.

I found it because I was trying to reset her streaming password—that's all. A completely ordinary Tuesday evening, and then suddenly I'm sitting at the kitchen table at midnight with the laptop screen the only light in the room, reading comments my fifteen-year-old son has been leaving for strangers, comments strangers have been leaving for him, for a year and a half. My coffee went cold two hours ago and I haven't moved except to scroll.

What I keep doing, which makes no sense, is checking how many followers the account has. I've refreshed that number six times now. As if the number going up or down will tell me what to do next, will hand me a script for the conversation I have to have with him tomorrow, will explain how a kid I eat breakfast with every morning has been living this parallel life that I know nothing about. I made a list of questions and then crossed them all out. I started a text to my ex-husband and deleted it. I walked to my son's bedroom door and stood outside it listening to him breathe in his sleep, my hand flat against the wood, not knocking.

The account isn't the worst thing I've ever seen. I have to keep reminding myself of that, because my body isn't getting the message—my hands won't stay still, and there's a pressure behind my sternum like something compressed and waiting. Some of the posts are just teenager nonsense, performance and posturing and the usual sharp-edged humor. But some of them are not. Some of them are a version of my son I've never met, talking about things he has apparently decided he cannot say to me, and I am sitting here in the dark trying to figure out how I became someone he felt he had to hide from.

The drilling started at 7 AM on a Monday and hadn't stopped in three weeks. Mara had already called the city noise hotline twice, left four notes in her neighbor's mailbox, and stood in front of his door for ten minutes one evening before losing her nerve. None of it mattered now. The walls of her apartment shook in a rhythm that made it impossible to sleep, impossible to think, impossible to do the work that was keeping her one month ahead of eviction. She was a transcriptionist. She needed silence the way other people needed oxygen. She opened her laptop and stared at the audio file she'd been paid to complete by Friday. The drilling started up again, the vibration traveling through her desk chair, up her spine. She put her headphones in, turned the volume up until her ears hurt, and tried to type. She deleted the same sentence four times. By midnight she was sitting on her bathroom floor with the door closed and a pillow pressed against her ears, her laptop balanced on the edge of the tub, whispering transcriptions to herself just to keep track of where she was.

I haven't slept more than two hours at a stretch since Phil next door tore out his kitchen. That was five weeks ago. At first it was just the hammering—I told myself I could handle hammering. Then came the contractors, arriving before dawn in trucks that idled outside my bedroom window for twenty minutes at a time. Now there's some kind of grinding tool that runs for hours without pause, a sound that gets inside my back teeth. I have a court date in eleven days. I'm a court reporter. My entire career runs on my ability to focus, to catch every syllable without flinching, and I have been sitting at my practice keyboard for the past hour hitting the same key over and over because my hands won't stop shaking. I went over to Phil's twice. The second time I brought cookies, which I know is absurd, but I couldn't think of what else to do with my hands while I stood there rehearsing what I'd say. He was friendly. He showed me the new layout. He said six more weeks, maybe eight. I smiled and nodded and walked back to my apartment and sat down on the kitchen floor and didn't move for a long time.

The eviction notice was still folded in Margaret's back pocket, the paper worn soft at the creases from how many times she'd unfolded and refolded it over the past three days. Sixty days. That's what the landlord had given her before she had to be out of the house she'd lived in for eleven years. She was standing at her neighbor Paul's door before she'd fully decided to walk over, her knuckles already raised to knock. When he answered, she smiled too quickly, too wide. "I have a strange request," she said, her voice coming out higher than intended. She cleared her throat. "I want to put in a vegetable garden. Along the south fence, on your side — the light's better there." Paul scratched the back of his neck. "On my property?" She'd rehearsed this. She had a whole reasonable explanation ready about shared fence lines and seasonal arrangements and she'd maintain it herself entirely. Instead she just stood there with her hands clasped in front of her, pressing her own fingers hard enough to whiten the knuckles. "I need to grow food," she finally said. The rehearsed speech was gone. "I genuinely need to."

I counted the seeds twice before I went next door. Eighteen tomato seeds, a paper envelope of pole beans, two small pots of basil seedlings I'd been keeping on the windowsill under a grow lamp since February. My apartment had no outdoor space, no balcony, nothing. I'd been eating one real meal a day for six weeks and I couldn't tell anyone that, couldn't say it out loud to my sister or my old college friends who posted photos of restaurants online. But I knew that if I could get things in the ground by May I'd have food by August. I rang the bell and waited, rubbing my thumb across the packet of seeds until the paper started to go fuzzy at the edge. When Darnell opened the door I pointed toward the strip of unused land along his back fence, the weedy patch he'd never done anything with in the four years we'd been neighbors. "I want to ask you something," I said. My mouth had gone very dry. "That section back there — I've been thinking about it for a while." I hadn't slept the night before. I could feel that fact sitting plainly on my face, visible as a bruise. He looked at me for a moment, then looked at the seed packet in my hand, then back at me. "Come inside," he said. "Tell me what you need."

The eviction notice had been taped to Maya's door at 6 AM, which meant the landlord had wanted her to see it before she left for work. Three months behind. She'd been eating rice and canned beans for two weeks, had sold her laptop and was borrowing her coworker's during lunch breaks just to keep applying for second jobs. She was sitting on her front stoop going through the notice for the fourth time when she noticed the FedEx truck idling in front of Mr. Calloway's house next door. The driver unloaded seven packages, then came back with four more. Maya watched without meaning to. She'd always assumed Calloway was like her—retired on a modest fixed income, making do. But then his front door opened and she caught a glimpse of the interior she'd never seen before: hardwood floors that gleamed, a painting on the foyer wall that looked like it belonged in a gallery, a suit jacket hanging on a coat rack that had a particular kind of drape she associated with money. Calloway signed for the packages without looking up. Maya folded the eviction notice into smaller and smaller squares until the paper started to tear.

She knocked on his door that evening with a plate of cookies she'd baked from the last of her pantry staples, her jaw tight the whole walk over. When he answered, she smiled and said she'd made too many, which wasn't true. Her hands stayed very still at her sides while he thanked her, while he made small talk about the weather. She kept finding her eyes drifting past him into the house—the bookshelves, the side table with a lamp that was clearly antique, a framed photograph of him shaking hands with someone she almost recognized. "Is everything alright, dear?" Calloway asked, and she realized she'd gone quiet mid-conversation. "Fine," she said, her voice coming out smaller than she'd intended. "Just tired." She walked back to her apartment and sat on the kitchen floor and stared at the eviction notice she'd left on the counter, its corners still creased from folding.

She started noticing things she hadn't before: the car in his garage when the door was left open one morning, black and low to the ground. The way the landscaping company came every Thursday without him ever seeming to call them. The name on one of the FedEx packages she'd seen by accident when the wind shifted it on his porch—a law firm she recognized from downtown, one that handled estates. That night she wrote a note asking if he knew of any work she could do for him, yard work, errands, anything, and then she sat holding it for forty minutes before she finally put it in his mailbox at midnight, her heart going so fast she could feel it in her throat, her breath fogging in the cold air.

I've been pawning things in the wrong order. I know that now. Should have kept the good camera and sold the coffee maker first, should have thought longer before I let the camera go, because now I have nothing worth selling and still three hundred dollars short of what I owe my sister, who is running out of patience in ways she doesn't say directly but I can hear in the pauses when we talk on the phone. I'm standing at the kitchen window eating crackers for dinner, not even putting them on a plate, just holding the sleeve, when I notice the light on in the basement over at Gerald's place. He's lived next door for eleven years and I've never once seen the basement light on.

The next morning I'm putting out my trash when his garage door rolls up automatically, which I've never seen it do before—he usually opens it by hand—and inside, behind his old Civic, there are shelves floor to ceiling packed with labeled boxes, and leaning against the wall is what I'm almost certain is a painting in a crate, the kind of crate with padding and reinforced corners that you get from a professional shipper. Gerald comes out and sees me staring and just says good morning, same as always. I say good morning back. My voice is level. I'm proud of that.

I start paying attention in a way that makes me a little ashamed of myself. I notice the financial newspaper in his recycling, folded to the markets page. I notice that when he mentions his "old house upstate" he says it the way people do when they mean a second property rather than a former residence. I notice the watch. I'd never looked at his watch before. That evening I knock and ask if I can borrow a cup of sugar, which I don't need, and while he goes to get it I stand in his doorway and see the framed lithograph in the hallway, signed in pencil at the bottom right corner, and something goes cold and quick through my chest. I thank him for the sugar and go home and I open my laptop and I pull up the number for the bank and I just sit there staring at it, my thumb hovering over the screen, trying to figure out what I'm actually going to say.

The box of her things sat on Maya's new desk in the acquisitions department, still unpacked three days in. She kept leaving it sealed, as if not settling in might mean she could still go back. Every morning she took the long route to her new floor, the one that passed by the design studio where she used to work, and she'd slow her pace near the glass partition, watching her former team hunched over their light tables. On the fourth day she sent her old manager, Dennis, the fifth unanswered email about the Hartley rebrand—the project she'd spent eleven months developing, the one she'd promised a client she'd personally see through. She knew how it looked. She'd written and deleted the subject line four times before settling on "Quick question re: Hartley" as though it were nothing, as though her hands weren't shaking when she hit send. At lunch she sat in the stairwell between floors rather than the cafeteria, eating nothing, her untouched sandwich balanced on her knee, rehearsing what she might say to HR if she requested a formal review of the transfer decision. By Friday she'd requested that meeting three times. The third request had a word count her colleague later described, gently, as "a lot."

I've started setting my alarm for 5 AM just so I can get to the office before anyone else arrives and sit at my old workstation in the analytics wing. I tell myself I'm collecting my things. There aren't really any things left to collect. The new role in client services starts Monday and the desk they've assigned me is on a different floor entirely, near the windows that look out over the parking structure instead of the river. I put in a request to keep leading the Meridian account—I'm the only one who knows where the data gaps are, the only one who built those forecasting models from scratch. The request came back denied with two lines of boilerplate. So I submitted it again, reworded, with a four-page attachment outlining transition risks. When my manager called me into her office about it, I sat very still with my hands flat on my knees and spoke slowly because I didn't trust what my voice would do otherwise. She said the decision had come from above her. I nodded. I thanked her. I went back to my desk and immediately began drafting an email to the person above her, then the person above that, stopping only because my vision had gone slightly blurry and I realized I hadn't eaten since the day before.

Maya found out by accident—a text meant for someone else, forwarded to her by mistake, with the subject line "Re: canceling Maya's party." She read it three times standing in the cereal aisle of the grocery store, her basket handle cutting into her palm. She didn't put it down. She stood there reading it a fourth time, her thumbnail pressing white crescents into the plastic handle. The store's fluorescent hum felt suddenly too loud, the brightness too flat and indifferent. She bought nothing and walked to her car and sat in it for twenty-two minutes before starting the engine.

By evening, she had called her sister four times. Voicemail, every time. She wasn't supposed to know about the party—that was the whole thing, you weren't supposed to know—so she couldn't say the actual words. She left messages about nothing. "Just calling to check in." "Thought we could do dinner this week." "Are you around? Call me back." She stared at her phone between each attempt, turning it over and over on the kitchen counter, the screen winking on every time it caught the light.

She opened her calendar. Her birthday was Saturday. She had nothing on Saturday. She had nothing on the whole weekend and had planned it that way intentionally, had turned down two invitations, had even lied to a coworker about being busy—all because she'd been told to keep herself free. She'd been so careful. She'd done everything right. Her throat tightened until swallowing took effort.

At midnight, she started making a list. Venues that were open on short notice. Catering minimums. Whether the party supply store near her apartment was open on Fridays. She wrote in handwriting that got smaller and more cramped as the page filled up. She crossed things out. She added asterisks. She told herself this was practical, that this was just contingency planning, that she wasn't doing what she was obviously doing. The pen scratched hard against the paper. The list was two pages long before she realized she'd been crying for several minutes without noticing.

I've known since Tuesday. That's four days of carrying it around—four days of answering "nothing much" when people ask what I've got planned for my birthday, four days of smiling at Deb from accounting when she says "big week coming up" with that little eyebrow raise that I now understand was about the party and not about the Henderson report.

The party was cancelled because of money, from what I can piece together. Somebody's card declined. Somebody else had a work thing come up. That's what the email thread said—the one that landed in my inbox because Jess forgot to check who was in the CC field. I've read it so many times the words have lost their shape. I almost replied. I got as far as typing "Hey, I—" before I closed the window and went to the bathroom and turned the cold tap all the way up and held my wrists under it until I felt something other than what I was feeling.

The thing is, I don't want a party. I want what the party means. I want the evidence of it—the proof that there was planning, coordination, a group of people who looked at a calendar and agreed that I was worth the effort of showing up for. I am aware this is not a great thing to admit. I'm admitting it anyway.

Yesterday I asked my roommate if she wanted to do something Saturday. Casual, totally offhand, while I was unloading the dishwasher and had my back to her. She said she thought she had something but she'd check. I put a mug away. I put another one away. I stood there with the cabinet open longer than any cabinet has ever needed to be open, waiting, and she said "yeah, sorry, I actually do have plans." I closed the cabinet very gently and said that was totally fine.

I've been cleaning the apartment for three days. Not light cleaning—real cleaning, the kind where you move the furniture and get behind things. My hands stay busy and my head stays quiet enough. The grout in the bathroom is now immaculate. I keep finding more things that need scrubbing.

The email had been sent to the wrong distribution list—that was how Priya found out. She read it three times standing in the break room, her coffee going cold in her hand, the words "acquisition finalized pending regulatory approval" rearranging themselves each time like they might eventually mean something different. She had forty-three days of unused vacation. She had a non-compete clause that covered her entire industry within a 200-mile radius. She had a daughter starting orthodontic treatment in January.

By noon she had sent LinkedIn connection requests to everyone she'd ever worked with, including people she actively disliked. By two o'clock she'd downloaded the employee directory and was cross-referencing names against the acquiring company's org chart, looking for gaps, for openings, for any indication that her role—her specific, particular role—might survive the merger. She kept pulling up the email and rereading the CC list, trying to determine who already knew.

She called her old manager, Daniel, who had left the company two years ago for reasons he'd never fully explained. He picked up on the second ring. She heard herself say "I need advice" and then she couldn't organize the words after that, just talked in circles about her non-compete and the orthodontist and her unused vacation, while Daniel stayed very quiet on the other end. "Priya," he finally said. "Breathe. Have you actually been let go yet?" She hadn't. She hadn't even been spoken to. She was standing in a bathroom stall at her still-functional job, gripping her phone with both hands.

That evening she sat at the kitchen table with a legal pad, and made a column of assets and a column of debts, and looked at the gap between them for a long time. Then she turned the legal pad over so she couldn't see the numbers, and sat there anyway, very still, while her daughter did homework nearby and the refrigerator hummed and outside a car alarm went off and no one came to silence it.

I found out because I walked into the conference room without knocking, which I never do, which I should have done this one time. The CFO was on a video call and behind him on the whiteboard someone had written the name of our largest competitor and circled it twice. He minimized the screen immediately but we both knew what I'd seen. He said "close the door, please" and I did, from the outside.

I've been here nine years. I've been here longer than our current CEO. I trained the person who currently manages the department I used to manage before my role was reorganized last spring, which I told myself at the time was not a warning sign.

I spent the rest of that afternoon walking the long way between every meeting, taking routes that let me pass the CFO's office, watching for closed doors, for unfamiliar faces, for anyone carrying paperwork in a manila folder. I noted who was called into conversations I wasn't invited to. At one point I counted the same colleague being summoned twice in two hours and had to go sit in my car in the parking garage and eat the granola bar I keep in my glove compartment for reasons I've never had to examine before.

That night I stayed at my desk until the cleaning crew came through. I told myself I was catching up on work but what I was actually doing was opening and closing the company's job listings page, looking for my own position, checking whether they'd already posted it. My reflection in the dark window behind my monitor looked like someone I vaguely recognized. I refreshed the page. I refreshed it again.

The will had been read three days ago, and Priya still hadn't eaten a real meal. She'd been subsisting on coffee and the kind of crackers you find forgotten in the back of a cupboard, sitting at her mother's kitchen table with every financial document she could locate spread out in overlapping layers. The inheritance her brother Rohan had described — the investment accounts, the property portfolio, the carefully maintained savings — bore no resemblance to what the lawyer had handed her. Her portion was a single envelope containing the deed to a storage unit in a town she'd never heard of and a handwritten note that said *you always knew what to do with difficult things*. She'd read it forty times. She didn't know what it meant. She'd called Rohan twice and both times he'd said, gently, that maybe their mother had simply made her wishes clear. Priya hung up the second time without saying goodbye. Now she was driving to the storage unit at six in the morning, hands tight on the wheel, having told no one where she was going. There were forty-seven miles between her and whatever her mother had decided she deserved, and she drove every one of them without the radio on.

I found the second will on a Tuesday, tucked inside a first edition my father kept on the highest shelf of his study — the one he'd told me, specifically, never to touch. I remember standing on the rolling ladder with the folded paper in my hands, reading my own name repeatedly as if it might stop meaning what it meant. My sister Elena had already met with the estate attorney. She'd already begun talking to realtors about the house. According to the document I was holding — dated four years later than the one Elena had — the house was mine outright, along with a trust our father had apparently established without mentioning it to anyone. I called the attorney's office and was put on hold. I called back and left a voicemail. I sat down on the study floor with my back against the shelves and dialed Elena's number, then ended the call before it connected. My throat felt like something was lodged in it. What I kept turning over wasn't the money, or even the house — it was the image of my father climbing that ladder, sliding this paper into a book he knew I would eventually open, arranging a confrontation he'd never have to witness himself. I called the attorney again. This time I didn't let them put me on hold.

The eviction notice was taped to Marcus's door when he got home from his third overnight shift that week. Thirty days. He read it four times standing in the hallway, then folded it into smaller and smaller squares until it was the size of a postage stamp. He hadn't been sleeping anyway. The baby had colic—had for eleven weeks—and nothing worked, nothing, not the bouncing or the white noise machine or the four different formulas they'd cycled through while watching their bank account drain. His wife had cried herself into exhaustion on the couch again, the baby finally quiet against her chest. When the knock came at 8 AM, Marcus already knew who it was before he opened the door.

Gerald from 4B stood in the hallway in his bathrobe, arms crossed, jaw set. He'd complained twice before, once through the super and once through a note slid under the door. Marcus opened his mouth, then closed it. He looked back at his wife's sleeping face, at the small fist curled under the baby's chin. He turned back to Gerald and the words that came out were quieter than he intended, flattened somehow. "She's sick. We're trying. We're doing everything we can." Gerald said something about his right to quiet enjoyment of his unit. Marcus gripped the door frame. His thumbnail was bleeding where he'd been picking at it.

He went online that night and filed an appeal with the housing board, hands moving too fast across the keyboard, backspacing through errors. He found a forum for parents of colicky infants and read through forty pages of posts at 3 AM with the kind of focus that felt less like reading and more like searching for something to hold onto. He printed out a three-paragraph letter explaining the medical situation and slid it under Gerald's door at 6 AM, then stood in the hallway for a moment after, forehead nearly touching the wood, listening to the silence on the other side.

I've started leaving for the library at seven in the morning just to avoid running into her in the stairwell. I know how that sounds. But if Sandra catches me before I've had coffee, before I've had a chance to rehearse what I want to say, I'll just stand there blinking while she talks about decibel levels and lease agreements, and I'll nod along to things I shouldn't agree to. It happened last time. I nodded so much I practically validated her complaint about my Tuesday evening piano practice, which I do between seven and eight PM, which I checked against the building's quiet hours policy before I ever even moved the keyboard in.

The piano is the thing. That's what she doesn't understand, what I haven't been able to make her understand in three separate conversations and one typed-out message I slipped under her door. I started playing again after my brother died. I'm not explaining that to Sandra. I'm not putting that on the table for her to weigh against her sleep schedule. But my fingers go to the keys now the way they used to go to cigarettes—involuntarily, because something in me needs the motion, the sound, the way a chord fills a room. I've bought a sustain pedal dampener. I've put rubber feet under the stand. I have looked up the specific decibel output of a digital keyboard at medium volume and I carry that number around in my head like a talisman.

Last Thursday she knocked while I was mid-phrase. I sat completely still. I watched my own hands hovering above the keys, not moving, and counted to sixty, then to one hundred twenty, then to one hundred eighty. She knocked again at the two-minute mark, three short hard raps. I set my palms flat on my thighs and stared at the wall until I heard her footsteps retreating down the hall.

The notice was taped to her door at 7:43 in the morning. Maya read it four times on the doorstep, then folded it, then unfolded it and read it again. Thirty days. She went inside and sat at the kitchen table and did not move for eleven minutes. Then she opened her laptop and began typing emails — to her landlord, to a tenant rights organization she found through a Google search, to her sister in Phoenix who hadn't returned her last three calls. She wrote to her landlord twice more after that, changing the tone each time, starting formal, then friendly, then formal again. By noon she had printed out copies of every rent receipt she'd kept over four years and arranged them in chronological order on the floor. She photographed them with her phone. She photographed the notice. She photographed the water damage on the ceiling that she'd been reporting since February, in case it was relevant, in case anything was relevant. Her hands moved faster than her thoughts.

I keep the notice in my jacket pocket now, folded into eighths, because leaving it at home means I might lose it, and I can't lose it, I need it for the housing office and the legal aid appointment and whatever comes after that. I've had the appointment rescheduled twice — once because they were understaffed, once because I showed up on the wrong day, which I still don't entirely understand, I had it written down. At night I lie on top of the covers fully dressed, staring at the popcorn ceiling I've memorized over six years, doing arithmetic I already know the answers to. My daughter's school is four blocks from this apartment. The next closest school is two bus transfers away and I don't have a car. I told my landlord this on the phone last Tuesday, standing outside the building so she wouldn't hear me through the walls. My voice stayed level the whole time. I was very calm. I was very, very calm.
