When he hung up without saying anything, I stood on the sidewalk for a while with the phone pressed against my sternum, watching cars pass, until someone asked if I was alright and I realized I'd been there for twenty minutes.

Maya refreshes the journal's website for the eleventh time in an hour. The article is right there — her structure, her argument, her eighteen months of fieldwork in coastal Ecuador — and the byline reads Dr. Phillip Harrow. She knows Phillip Harrow. He was her thesis advisor. She had emailed him the draft in confidence, asking for feedback before submission. Her stomach contracts like something is being wrung out of it.

She pulls up every email thread between them and screenshots each one with shaking hands, her phone nearly slipping twice. Then she opens her laptop and starts a folder labeled EVIDENCE, which feels melodramatic until she remembers that it isn't. She has the original document with its metadata. She has the email timestamps. She has four drafts saved in chronological order across three different cloud services, each one with her name in the author field.

The department chair's office hours end in forty minutes. Maya is already in her car before she realizes she left her keys on the kitchen counter, goes back inside, knocks her water glass off the counter reaching for them, and does not stop to clean up the water spreading across the tile.

She practices what she'll say at every red light. By the time she parks, the speech has grown from two sentences to nine, then back down to three, then she abandons it entirely and walks through the building's glass doors with her folder pressed flat against her chest as though something inside it might escape.

I found it by accident. I was doing what I always do on Sunday mornings — coffee going cold beside me, aimlessly clicking through the new publications in our field — when I saw the title. *Reconfiguring Grief: Narrative Identity in Post-Conflict Oral Histories.* I had written every word of that title on a legal pad in my kitchen fourteen months ago, and I had written the 9,000-word article that followed it, and the name above it was not mine.

I call the journal's editorial office and get a voicemail. I call back immediately and get the same voicemail. I hang up and call the main university switchboard asking to be connected to the editor's direct line and am told she is unavailable. I ask for her email address. I am given a general inbox address I already have. I say, very carefully, that this is a time-sensitive matter regarding a publication ethics concern, and the person on the phone tells me to hold and then the line goes silent for four minutes before disconnecting.

I have been awake since finding the article at 7 AM. It is now past midnight and I am sitting on my bathroom floor with my laptop because the bathroom has the best cell signal in the apartment, and I am composing the third version of a formal complaint letter, and the first two are open in adjacent tabs, and none of them feel like enough. My hands have not warmed up since this morning. I keep rereading the opening paragraph — my paragraph, the one I revised forty times, the one I know the way I know my own handwriting — and something in my chest does the same thing it did the first time: a hard, sudden drop, like a floor giving way.

She refreshed the grade portal four times in sixty seconds, each time hoping the number would be different. A 58. On the thesis paper she'd spent eleven weeks writing, the one she'd skipped her cousin's wedding for, the one her advisor had called "promising" in October. Maya's financial aid required a 3.0. She was already at a 2.8. She got up from her desk, sat back down, got up again. She found herself standing in the hallway outside her professor's office at 7:45 AM, forty-five minutes before office hours began, back pressed against the wall, rehearsing sentences under her breath. When Professor Harding finally arrived and unlocked the door, Maya followed her in before being invited. "I need to understand every single point that was deducted," she said, pulling out a printed copy of the rubric she'd annotated in three colors. "I can rewrite it. I know resubmissions aren't standard but I looked up the academic appeals process and I have the forms already filled out." She set the folder on the desk. Her hands didn't stop moving.

I got a 61. I keep writing that number down, crossing it out, writing it again, as if the repetition might make it feel real or make it feel less real—I can't decide which. The exam was worth forty percent of my grade. I have the textbook open in front of me right now and I've been cross-referencing every question I remember getting wrong, and the answers are right there, they were always right there, which is somehow worse. I called my dad and then hung up before the second ring because I didn't know what I was going to say. I emailed Professor Castellano asking whether there was any extra credit, any makeup option, any possibility at all, and then I pulled up his rate-my-professor page and read every review twice looking for evidence that his grading was unfair, that this had happened to others, that there was something I could use. My roommate knocked and asked if I wanted dinner and I said I wasn't hungry, which is true in the way that true things can still be incomplete. I have a spreadsheet open in another tab, calculating the exact scores I'd need on every remaining assignment to pass this course. The numbers are not impossible. I've highlighted them anyway.

The email had arrived at 6:47 AM, and Priya had read it eleven times since then. *Administrative error. Misallocation of funds. The scholarship was intended for another recipient.* She sat in the financial aid office now, forty minutes before it opened, first in a line that didn't exist yet. She had brought everything: her acceptance letter, her financial aid package, three semesters of transcripts, a folder of recommendation letters she'd never been asked to provide. The folder had a label she'd printed and laminated. She wasn't sure when she'd done that.

When the advisor finally unlocked the door, Priya was already standing. She laid the documents on the desk in a sequence she'd rehearsed, pointing to figures, dates, her name typed in black ink on official letterhead. "There has to be a record of the original award," she said. Her voice came out very level, which surprised her, because her right knee had been bouncing since she sat down and she couldn't make it stop. "I've been enrolled for three semesters. I've maintained a 3.9. Whatever error happened, it happened in your office, not mine."

The advisor, a tired man named Gerald who had probably seen everything, looked at the documents and then looked at her. "I understand your concern," he said, and she felt something close very quickly behind her eyes. *Concern.* She pressed her palms flat on the desk. "My tuition is due in nineteen days," she said. "My mother works two jobs. I have a younger brother. I left a full-time position to be here." She heard herself beginning to list things and couldn't stop, the way you can't stop a tipped glass. "I have a 3.9. I have never missed a deadline. I was told this money was mine."

She walked out of the office an hour later with a case number and a timeline of four to six weeks. The hallway outside was filling with students changing classes, backpacks and laughter and people who presumably had somewhere to be. Priya stood against the wall with her laminated folder pressed to her chest and stared at the middle distance, breathing slowly and deliberately, like someone who has just been told to stay very still.

I found out on a Tuesday, which felt wrong somehow. Bad news should at least have the decency to arrive on a Friday. The scholarship committee chair called me personally, which I initially took as a good sign—people don't call you personally to tell you something fine. She was apologetic in the particular way of someone whose apology is covering something much larger than it appears, and by the time she'd finished explaining, I was sitting on my bathroom floor without any memory of having gotten there.

Here's what I did in the seventy-two hours after that call: I filed a formal appeal through the registrar's website at 11 PM. I emailed my academic advisor, my department chair, and the Dean of Students. I wrote a second email to the Dean of Students with a subject line that said *URGENT - TIME SENSITIVE - PLEASE READ* and then sat staring at my sent folder as if I could will a response into existence. I ate maybe once. I called my dad and told him nothing, just asked if he was home, asked how things were, stayed on the phone for forty minutes talking about the weather in a voice I barely recognized.

The other student—the one it was *meant* for—I looked her up. I'm not proud of it. Her name was on the department website. She was a third-year with publications already, a thesis advisor who'd written her recommendation in language that made mine look like a grocery list. I closed the tab and opened it again three times. Then I went for a run at midnight in thirty-degree weather without a jacket, just to make my body feel something it could actually solve.

By Thursday I had a meeting scheduled with the committee chair in person, a printed timeline of every communication I'd received about the award going back fourteen months, and a three-page letter that I'd written and rewritten until the original document was buried under forty-seven saved versions. I walked into that meeting with every piece of paper I owned and both hands shaking so slightly that probably no one could see it but me.

The bouquet had been sitting on her kitchen counter for six hours. Mara had photographed it from every angle, uploaded the images to three different flower identification apps, and cross-referenced the results with a list she'd made of every person who might possibly send her something. The list had seventeen names on it, each one annotated with a likelihood percentage and her reasoning. Daniel was at 34% because of the yellow tulips, which she'd mentioned once, offhandedly, in a conversation two years ago that she'd since replayed so many times it had worn grooves into her memory. She picked up her phone, put it down, picked it up again. She had drafted and deleted eleven texts. Some were casual. Some were not casual at all. She opened her email and began composing a message to the florist whose name was printed on the paper wrapping, intending to ask politely who had placed the order. By the third draft, she was no longer asking politely. She was explaining, in detail, why it was critical that she know. She deleted that one too and sat on the kitchen floor with her back against the cabinets, staring up at the flowers as though they might eventually crack under the pressure of her attention and simply tell her.

I held the stems under warm water and cut the ends at an angle the way my mother taught me, because keeping my hands busy was the only thing that was working. Someone had left them on my doorstep. No card. No note. Not even a ribbon with a shop's name on it, just brown paper tied with twine and fourteen white peonies that smelled like something I couldn't name but recognized anyway. I'd called my sister first. Then my two closest friends. Then, humiliatingly, an ex I hadn't spoken to in two years, who didn't pick up, which was probably for the best. My upstairs neighbor heard me in the hallway and I stopped him with a hand on his elbow and asked, too quickly, whether he'd seen anyone at my door that morning. He looked at my face in a way that made me aware of what my face must be doing. I went back inside and sat at the table across from the vase I'd put them in. The apartment was very quiet. Outside, a car alarm went off and then stopped. I got up and checked my phone. I sat back down. I got up and checked the hallway again, looked both ways, stood there for a moment in my socks on the cold floor, and then closed the door very carefully, as though making noise might disturb something that hadn't yet decided what it was.

The profile picture was definitely Jamie. Same crooked nose, same gap between the front teeth, even in the tiny thumbnail. Lena had spent forty minutes crafting the Facebook message before sending it, then deleted the app from her phone so she'd stop checking. That lasted six hours. When the reply came, it was four sentences. Friendly. Polite. The kind of message you'd send to a stranger who had the wrong number. *I'm sorry, I don't think we've met? I grew up in Portland, not Tucson. Are you sure you have the right person?*

She pulled out the shoebox from the top shelf of her closet, the one she hadn't opened since the move. Inside: a birthday card in Jamie's handwriting, a photo of the two of them at a water park, their arms slung around each other, squinting into the sun. Lena was missing her two front teeth. Jamie had a bandage on one knee. She photographed the picture and attached it to a reply, then added the card. Then she typed out a list of memories—the tree fort, the summer Jamie's dog got out and they spent three days searching the neighborhood, the pact they'd made on the last day of fifth grade. She read the message over once before sending it, her thumbnail pressed hard into her palm, leaving a white crescent in the skin.

The reply took two days. *That's so strange—I honestly don't recognize myself in that photo, or any of this. I'm not trying to be hurtful. I just genuinely don't remember.* Lena read it standing in her kitchen at 6 AM, still in yesterday's clothes. She'd been awake for most of those two days. There was a half-eaten bowl of cereal on the counter from whenever she'd last tried to eat, the milk gone yellowish. She put her phone face-down on the counter, then immediately picked it back up. She opened a new message. She started typing. She needed Jamie to know about the time they got lost in the woods behind the school for four hours and Lena had cried and Jamie had held her hand the whole time and told her they'd get out, they'd definitely get out.

---

I found him at a hardware store, of all places. Thirty-two years, and Danny Pak was standing in the paint aisle holding two shades of beige up to the fluorescent light, squinting at them like it was the most important decision in the world. My cart stopped rolling. I just stood there in the middle of the aisle with my hand on the handle and my chest doing something I couldn't explain. He looked older—obviously—but the way he tilted his head to one side when he was thinking, that was exactly the same as when we were nine.

I said his name twice before he turned around. The second time my voice came out louder than I meant it to, and a woman nearby looked over. He turned, and his face was polite and blank in a way that I felt in my back teeth. *Hi there,* he said, the way you'd greet a coworker you barely knew. I said my name. I said we'd grown up on the same street. I said he'd taught me to ride a bike in my driveway, that I'd been at his house the night his parents told him they were getting divorced, that we'd been inseparable until my family moved to Cincinnati when we were eleven. His expression didn't change. He was trying, I could see him trying—his eyes moving over my face like he was searching a room for something he'd misplaced. *I'm so sorry,* he said finally. *I don't—I really don't remember. I'm sorry.*

I drove to my mother's house afterward. She still had the same landline number, the same door that sticks in humid weather. I asked her to find the old photo albums, the ones from the street we'd lived on. She brought them out and I sat at her kitchen table and photographed every single page with Danny in it—birthday parties, Halloween, the Fourth of July with both our families in the backyard—my hands moving fast, like someone might take the albums away. She watched me from the doorway with her arms crossed. She said my name once, gently, the way she used to when I was small and had worked myself up over something. I didn't look up. I kept going through the pages. I was building something, a case, a proof, though I couldn't have said who it was for.

The hole had appeared where Marcus had been digging up the dead rosebush, and the metal tin inside it had no business being there. He wiped the mud off the lid with his thumb, hands not quite steady. Inside: a folded piece of paper, a photograph, and a small brass key. The paper had his handwriting on it—unmistakably his, the particular way he formed his sevens—but the words made no sense. *For when you need proof. You know what to do.* He read it four times standing in the cold backyard, the wet soil soaking through the knees of his jeans.

He'd been awake for most of the previous week, cycling through the same sequence: check his email for the DNA results, call the lab's automated line, hang up before the message finished. His daughter was in a custody dispute he had no legal standing in, a child he'd raised for six years suddenly someone else's jurisdiction because of a biological test he hadn't known was coming. He needed evidence of something—presence, continuity, a life lived in the same place for long enough to matter. He wasn't sure what kind of evidence. He wasn't sure what kind would even work.

The photograph was of him and Lily in the backyard, her maybe three years old, both of them crouched over something in the dirt. He turned it over. *She helped you bury this. She wanted to.* His throat closed around whatever he'd been about to say. He looked at the brass key for a long time, turning it in his fingers, then went inside to find what it opened, moving through every drawer and cabinet in the house with the photograph pressed flat against his palm.

I found it on a Tuesday, when I was already running on four hours of sleep and the particular kind of stillness that comes after you've cried until there's nothing left to wring out. I'd been clearing the storage unit—the one I kept paying for out of some reflex I couldn't name, even after the divorce, even after I moved into the studio that barely fit a bed—when my boot hit something solid under an old moving blanket. A shoebox wrapped in plastic wrap and duct tape. My name on the outside in my own handwriting.

I didn't remember wrapping it. I didn't remember writing my own name like a label, like a thing to be stored and retrieved. Inside was a USB drive, a folded letter, and a photograph of a woman I didn't recognize at first, and then did: my mother, maybe thirty years old, before she got sick. I hadn't seen a photograph of her that young in years. I'd thought they were all gone.

I read the letter standing up because my legs wouldn't agree to let me sit. Past-me had written it in second person, which felt intrusive in a way I hadn't expected—*you will have forgotten this, you always forget the things that cost you something*—and it listed, in careful bullet points, everything I had that was worth keeping. The list was longer than I would have predicted. My hands had started shaking before I reached the end of it, and I had to set the letter down and press both palms flat against the cold concrete floor and breathe, just breathe, until the shaking moved through me and came out the other side.

Maya had been refreshing the Instagram post for six hours. The tattoo—her tattoo, the one she'd spent four months painting, the heron standing in shallow water with its reflection breaking apart into geometric shapes—was inked across some stranger's entire back, and the post had 47,000 likes. No credit. No "inspired by." Nothing. She'd already sent twelve direct messages to the tattoo artist, each one escalating in tone from polite to something she wasn't proud of. None delivered. She pulled up the studio's website, found a contact form, and filled it out three times using different email addresses because the first two got no response within the hour.

The painting had been the piece that got her dropped from her gallery. She'd sold the original for four hundred dollars when she needed rent money, but had retained the rights—she'd been careful about that, painfully careful, the one professional thing she'd done correctly in a year of mistakes. She still had the contract somewhere in her email, a PDF she now forwarded to herself, to her sister, to two separate cloud drives, and printed out at the library down the street even though she had no idea what she would actually do with a printed copy.

She called the tattoo studio at 9 AM when they opened. A young man answered and she spoke very slowly and very quietly, the way she did when she was trying not to cry in public. She explained the situation. He said the artist was booked and couldn't come to the phone. Maya asked when the artist would be free. He said he didn't know. She asked if she could leave a number. He said sure. She gave it, then gave it again, then spelled her name twice, pressing the pen so hard into the notepad beside her that it tore through to the page below.

By noon she had opened a new tab with a copyright lawyer's contact page. She stared at the consultation fee—three hundred and fifty dollars—and closed the tab. She opened it again. The number didn't change. She had sixty-two dollars in checking and a Venmo balance of eleven. She closed the tab a second time and sat very still at her desk, her hands flat on the surface, her coffee going cold, while outside her window a bus went by and someone on the street laughed at something and the world continued as though nothing at all had shifted.

I found it on a Tuesday, which is a cruel day to have your world tipped sideways. A friend sent me the link with a string of question marks, no words, just the link and the marks, and I knew before I even clicked that it was going to be something I couldn't unfind. The tattoo covered the person's entire forearm—my drawing, my ink lines, the intricate moth I'd posted as a work-in-progress eighteen months ago and then quietly taken down when I decided to submit it to a print competition. They'd reproduced it perfectly. Every crosshatch. Every feather division on the wings. The tattoo artist had even replicated the small smudge near the lower left corner that I'd always meant to fix.

I have a particular thing I do when I don't know what else to do, which is clean. I scrubbed the bathroom grout with a toothbrush for forty minutes after I read that my father was sick. I reorganized every drawer in my studio the night my last relationship ended. That Tuesday I cleaned the kitchen floor on my hands and knees, then moved all my reference books from one shelf to another and back again, then stood in the middle of my studio holding a jar of brushes and couldn't remember what I'd meant to do with it.

The print competition was in six weeks. I had entered that moth drawing specifically—it was the centerpiece of a series I'd been developing, the piece that tied everything else together. The submission guidelines were explicit: work must not have been publicly reproduced in any commercial capacity. I didn't know if a tattoo counted. I didn't know if I could prove the timeline. I had the dated file on my laptop but I'd deleted the original post and I couldn't be certain the metadata would hold up to whatever scrutiny "holding up" might involve. I emailed the competition organizers at 11 PM, writing and rewriting the opening sentence nine times before sending something that probably still sounded unhinged.

My phone was in my hand constantly for the next three days. I carried it from room to room, set it face-up on the bathroom shelf while I showered, checked it at 3 AM and at 4 AM and once at 4:17 because I'd only been asleep for eleven minutes and it felt like longer. I kept returning to the photo of the tattoo, zooming in on the smudge in the corner. My smudge. I had made that mark with my own hand, a mistake I'd been too tired to correct, and now it was living permanently in someone else's skin without my name anywhere near it, without any trace that I had existed at all.

The tow truck was already three blocks away when Renata got outside, still in her socks. She ran after it anyway. Her lungs burned in the January air and she kept waving her arms even after it turned at the intersection and disappeared, as if the driver might check his mirrors and see her standing in the middle of the road with her hands raised above her head. She stood there for a full minute before going back inside to find the number for the towing company. Her hands shook so badly that she misdialed twice.

The woman on the phone explained, in the measured tone of someone who had this conversation many times a day, that the vehicle had been reported by a third party. Renata's voice climbed without her meaning it to. "It's my driveway. My name is on the deed. I have the title right here." She was already pulling open kitchen drawers, scattering takeout menus and batteries and rubber bands across the floor, looking for documents she wasn't even sure she had on hand. "I can fax it. Can I fax it? Do you have a fax?" She hadn't used a fax machine in a decade.

The car had her sister's dialysis equipment in the trunk. She'd loaded it the night before so she wouldn't have to rush in the morning. Renata sat down on the kitchen floor among the scattered drawer contents and calculated: the impound lot was fourteen miles away, the first fee was $175, the daily storage rate started accumulating at midnight, the dialysis center opened at seven, the appointment was at eight. She pulled her knees to her chest and pressed her forehead against them. Then she stood up, picked up the phone again, and started dialing.

I watched them load my car onto the flatbed from the upstairs window and couldn't process what I was seeing fast enough to stop it. By the time I got downstairs and out the front door I was only in time to see the truck pulling away with my Honda strapped to the back like something hunted and caught. I stood in my own driveway in the spot where it had been and stared at the oil stain on the concrete as though that might explain something.

The city inspector I reached after forty minutes on hold told me there was a lien on the property from a previous owner. A lien that had apparently been there when I bought the house eight months ago and that my closing attorney had somehow missed. The words kept arriving in my ear but stopped making sense partway through. Previous owner. Unresolved notice. Standard procedure. I asked him to repeat himself twice, each time pressing the phone so hard against my ear that it ached.

I called my attorney next and got her voicemail, then called back and got voicemail again, then called the main office line and asked to speak to anyone. I was pacing a tight oval in my kitchen without realizing it until I caught my own reflection in the dark screen of the microwave: phone jammed against my jaw, free hand pulling at the collar of my shirt, circles under my eyes from the night shift I'd just come home from. The car had the cashier's check in the glovebox. The check for my mother's mortgage payment, which was due today, which she could not miss, not after last month, not with what the bank had already told her. I stopped pacing. I pulled up the rideshare app. My hands did the thing they needed to do while the rest of me stood very still, somewhere far behind my own eyes.

Maya refreshed the university's homepage for the eleventh time that hour, as if the announcement might quietly disappear between visits. The email had arrived at 6:47 AM: *Hargrove College will cease operations at the end of the current academic semester.* She was three credits short of her degree. Three. She'd spent four and a half years at this school, working double shifts at the diner every weekend to pay tuition, skipping her cousin's wedding to finish her thesis draft. Her hands wouldn't stop moving—opening tabs, closing them, pulling up transfer credit policies at other universities and reading the same paragraph four times without retaining a word.

She called the registrar's office before it opened and stood in the hallway outside the door until someone arrived to unlock it. The woman behind the desk had clearly fielded a hundred versions of this conversation already that morning, her expression worn smooth with exhaustion. Maya spread her transcript across the counter. "These credits," she said, pointing, her fingernail tapping the paper hard enough to leave a dent. "Someone at another school has to accept these. Someone has to." Her voice came out strange, clipped and too high, like she was conserving something.

That night she sat on her dorm room floor surrounded by every document she owned—financial aid letters, course syllabi, professor recommendation drafts, receipts from the bookstore—and sorted them into piles with the concentrated focus of someone defusing something. Her roommate, passing through to get water, paused in the doorway. "You should sleep," she offered. Maya looked up. There were creases along her palms from gripping papers, and she hadn't eaten since morning. "I'll sleep when I have a plan," she said, and turned back to the floor.

I found out the same way everyone else did—a push notification from the campus news app, sandwiched between a weather update and a dining hall menu change. *Whitmore University announces closure effective June 30th.* I read it three times on the bus and then missed my stop by six blocks.

I am two semesters into a nursing program that does not exist anywhere else in this city at a price I can actually afford. My clinical hours don't transfer cleanly, I've looked it up, and the nearest comparable program has a two-year waitlist. I know this because I spent the bus ride back calling their admissions line, and then calling again when I got a voicemail, and then calling a third time and asking to be transferred to anyone who could speak with me right now, today, not next week. The woman I eventually reached was kind, which somehow made it worse. I thanked her and hung up and sat on a bench outside the bus station and watched pigeons for a while because I needed something to do with my eyes.

My mother asked me at dinner how my day was, and I told her fine. I watched her talk about her coworker's retirement party, and I nodded, and I cut my food into smaller and smaller pieces without eating much of it. After she went to bed I opened my laptop and started a spreadsheet: every nursing school within commuting distance, their tuition, their waitlists, their credit transfer policies. By 2 AM I had seventeen rows and had emailed eleven of them. I kept checking my inbox. I kept adding more columns.

The cookbook had been sitting in the display window of the bookstore for three weeks before Marlena finally made herself go inside and buy it. *Harvest & Hearth: A Southern Kitchen* by someone named DelphineArceneaux, who had a whole glossy author photo and a quote from a Food Network host on the back cover. Marlena stood at her kitchen counter and turned to page 47 with hands that wouldn't quite stay still. There it was. The fig and bourbon preserve with the black pepper finish — her grandmother's recipe, the one Marlena had typed up and emailed to her former business partner, Colette, four years ago when they were still trying to launch the catering company together. The measurements were identical. The instruction to let the mixture "rest until it smells like a memory" was word for word, a phrase Marlena had spent an afternoon composing.

She made coffee and didn't drink it. She sat at the table and opened her laptop and searched Delphine Arceneaux and found a website, a mailing list, a booking page for private cooking classes. She searched for Colette Marsh and found nothing, which somehow made it worse. She clicked back to Delphine Arceneaux's contact page and typed a message and deleted it and typed it again. Outside, her neighbor was mowing the lawn, the sound cutting in and out in long, even passes, and Marlena became aware that she was gripping the edge of the table hard enough that her knuckles had gone pale.

She called her sister first, because her sister was a paralegal and knew words like *intellectual property* and *documentation*. Her voice came out higher than she intended, tumbling over itself. "I have the email," Marlena said. "I have the original email with a timestamp, I have the handwritten card where Gran wrote it out for me, I have the version I submitted to the state fair in 2018 where I placed second, I have—" Her sister said her name, slowly, twice. "I hear you," her sister said. "Start from the beginning." Marlena pressed her thumbnail into her palm and started from the beginning.

She found an attorney who offered a free thirty-minute consultation and booked the earliest available slot, which was eleven days away. She printed every piece of documentation she owned and put it in a manila folder, then took it out and reorganized it chronologically, then alphabetically by document type, then chronologically again. She made a spreadsheet with dates. She added a column for sources. She added another column she labeled *notes* and filled it with observations she wasn't sure were legally relevant but couldn't stop herself from recording. The folder grew thicker. She kept the cookbook on the counter, open to page 47, because every time she closed it she felt like she was forgetting something important.

I found out on a Tuesday in October, sitting in the waiting room of my dentist's office, flipping through a glossy food magazine because my phone was at four percent and I didn't want to waste the battery. The recipe was on page 112, under a heading that read *Chef Tomás Reyes shares the secret behind his restaurant's most-requested dish*. The secret was my mother's spiced lamb stew. I know it was my mother's because she learned it from her mother, who brought it from Oaxaca in 1971, and I know I gave it to Tomás because I dated Tomás for two years and cooked for him constantly and he asked me once to write it down so he could try it himself. I had a memory, right then, of his handwriting on a Post-it note on my refrigerator that said *you're going to make me fat* with a little drawing of a smiling face.

The hygienist called my name and I walked in and sat in the chair and let her clean my teeth and I didn't say anything because what was I going to say. On the way home I stopped at a red light and pulled up the restaurant's Instagram on my phone and there was a photo of the stew in a clay bowl with fourteen hundred likes and a comment from someone who had driven four hours specifically to try it. My jaw was aching from being open at the dentist and I sat through three green lights before the car behind me honked.

I texted my cousin Bea because she knows Tomás, or knew him, and I needed someone to tell me I wasn't misremembering. *The ancho and the cinnamon together*, I wrote. *The way you finish it with the orange peel*. She called me back in two minutes. "I know," she said, before I'd said anything else. "I saw it last month. I didn't know how to tell you." I walked from room to room in my apartment while we talked, picking things up and putting them down. My mother's clay pot was on the shelf in the kitchen where I keep things I don't use often but can't put away. I took it down and held it while Bea talked about lawyers and documentation and how hard these cases were to prove. The pot was cold and heavier than I remembered. I kept turning it over in my hands, looking at the place near the base where my mother had scratched her initials into the clay before it dried.

The neighbor had meant it as a kindness, probably—stopping Maya on the driveway to explain why the previous owners had sold so quickly, why the price had been so low, why the whole street had gone quiet when the moving truck arrived. Maya had nodded through it, smiled, carried the last box inside. That was three hours ago. Now she was on her hands and knees in the master bedroom with a flashlight, pressing her fingers along the baseboards, looking for she didn't know what exactly. A stain. A mark. Something the cleaning crew had missed. She'd already pulled up the corner of the carpet near the closet. Her nails were split from it.

She called her real estate agent at 9 PM, then at 9:15, then sent a text in all capitals asking about disclosure laws, about what the agent had known and when. She'd already opened six browser tabs on her laptop. The search history read like a breakdown: *can you sell a house without disclosing murder*, *how to test for blood under flooring*, *crime scene cleaning companies*, *how long does trauma stay in walls*. That last one she'd typed and deleted and typed again.

Her husband found her rearranging the living room furniture at midnight, pushing the couch away from the east wall, scraping it across the hardwood they'd been so proud of at the showing. "Maya." His voice was careful in the way it got when he was frightened of her answer. She didn't stop pushing. "I need it away from that wall," she said. "I just need it away from there." He stood in the doorway in his socks and watched her, and she could feel his eyes on the back of her neck, and she kept moving the couch anyway, inch by inch, until her shoulders ached and the room looked completely wrong and she still didn't feel any better.

I found out on a Tuesday, which feels important somehow—a forgettable day, a day that shouldn't carry weight, and now it always will. My sister forwarded me the article without comment, just the link, and I sat in my own kitchen reading about what had happened here fourteen years before I ever made an offer. I read it three times. Then I got up and washed every dish in the sink, even the ones that were already clean.

The bank owns most of this house, technically. I know that. I signed the papers understanding that. What I didn't understand was that I'd stretched everything I had to get here—my savings, a loan from my mother that we both pretended was a gift, a second job I took for eight months just to shore up the down payment—and now I'm standing in the hallway touching the walls like I'm trying to read something written beneath the paint. I've called the bank twice this week asking about options, my voice going higher each time, more clipped, covering the shaking. The loan officer sounded confused. I don't blame him.

I've started sleeping with every light on. Not because I believe in anything, I want to be clear about that—I don't, I'm not that person—but the electric bill has doubled and I find myself at 3 AM standing at the window that faces the backyard, watching the motion-sensor light I installed last weekend trip on and off in the wind. I could sell. The thought arrives and I grip the windowsill. To sell I would have to disclose, and to disclose I would have to say the words out loud to a stranger across a table, and I would get back less than I put in, far less, and my mother's money would be gone and I would have nowhere to go that felt like mine. The motion light trips again. I don't move from the window.

The class list arrived in Marion's email at 6:47 AM, and she read the teacher's name three times before setting her phone face-down on the kitchen counter. Hendricks. Room 14. She stood at the sink running water over her hands even though they weren't dirty, letting it go cold, then colder. Her daughter Bea was upstairs eating cereal and swinging her legs off the chair and humming something from a cartoon, completely unaware. Marion picked up the phone again. She put it down again. She opened the school district's staff directory and found his photo, and there he was, twenty years older but with the same small eyes and the same way of tilting his head slightly in photos, like he was always waiting for something. Her coffee went cold on the counter untouched.

She called the school at 8:01, one minute after the office opened. She had prepared what to say. She asked about the process for requesting a different classroom placement, keeping her voice level, speaking slowly. The secretary explained that placements were final unless there were documented academic or medical reasons. Marion said she understood. She hung up and immediately began drafting an email to the principal, then deleted it, then started again. By the third draft she was listing things she had never written down before in her life, things she hadn't said out loud since she was nine years old, her cursor blinking at the end of each sentence like it was waiting for her to stop.

She didn't send it. She couldn't send it. She sat in her car in the driveway for twenty minutes after dropping Bea at her grandmother's, pressing her thumbnail into her palm until the half-moon mark stayed. She thought about what her therapist had said four years ago—that she had done the work, that she didn't have to keep carrying it. She thought about how Bea had packed her new backpack herself, with her name written on the tag in her own handwriting, loopy and proud. Marion started the car. She pulled out of the driveway. At the first red light she picked up her phone and called her sister, and when her sister answered, Marion said, "I need you to listen to me. I need you to not say anything yet. Just listen," and the light turned green and she didn't move, and the car behind her honked once, softly, like even it understood.

I found out the way you find out everything these days—a group chat. One of the other parents posted a photo of the classroom door, little paper apples with the kids' names on them, and there was Jonah's, third from the left. And underneath the apples, a construction paper sign that said MR. PETROV in big block letters, and I had to set my phone on my knee because my hands started doing something I couldn't control. I know that name. I know that name the way you know the smell of a place you've been trying to forget.

I told myself I was going to handle it calmly. I made a list. I wrote down exactly what I remembered and exactly what I was going to say and I rehearsed it in the bathroom mirror while Jonah watched cartoons in the next room. I sounded reasonable. I sounded like someone who had it together. Then I drove to the school and sat in the parking lot for forty minutes and ate an entire sleeve of crackers I found in the glove compartment without tasting a single one.

The vice principal was kind, in the way that people are kind when they think you might be overreacting. She used phrases like "personnel matter" and "formal complaint process" and "documentation." I nodded. I said "I understand" after each one, which was a lie, because what I understood was that nothing was going to be quick and Jonah started school in eleven days. On the drive home I called my mother, who I haven't called in two years for reasons that are related, and when she picked up I just said his name. She was quiet for a long time. Then she said, "I know." And something about that—just those two words—made me pull over because I couldn't see the road properly anymore.

The grant money runs out in six weeks. I've been sleeping four hours a night, maybe less, surviving on gas station coffee and the particular kind of tunnel vision that comes from knowing your entire doctoral career hinges on a single paper. My hands won't stop shaking when I type. I've started keeping a notepad on my nightstand because I wake up at 3 AM convinced I've forgotten something critical, some variable, some citation. So when Dr. Reeves slides her laptop across the conference table toward me at what was supposed to be a routine departmental meeting, and I see the methodology section—my methodology, the exact sequence I spent fourteen months developing—I don't speak for a very long time. I just look at the screen. Then I look at her. Then I look back at the screen, because surely I am misreading something. My coffee cup is still in my hand, tilted at an angle, and I watch a thin brown stream hit the carpet before I right it. "When did you start this?" I ask, and my voice comes out stripped of everything, just the bare question hanging there. Dr. Reeves is already pulling the laptop back, already talking about parallel development, about collaboration opportunities, but I am doing the math in my head about what it means if she publishes first, what it means for my funding renewal, for my position, for the five years I have poured into this room.

Pavel found the GitHub repository at 11:47 on a Tuesday night, and for a full minute he just sat there with his face lit blue by the monitor. Someone named Taryn Cole had committed her first build nine months ago—three months before Pavel had even started his project, which meant nothing except everything. His own software was better. He knew it was better. He had the testing data to prove it. But she had nine months on him, a clean public record of development, and—he clicked through her profile—two industry mentors listed as collaborators. Pavel opened his own repository in a second tab and held both side by side. The architecture was nearly identical. Not plagiarism, nothing like that, just two people arriving at the same solution by parallel roads, which his advisor had always said was a sign of good thinking and which felt, right now, like a fist closing around his throat. He began typing an email to Taryn Cole, deleted it, typed it again. He'd been rejected from four jobs this year, all of them citing the unfinished project as the sticking point, and he had told himself—had genuinely believed—that finishing it would turn everything around. He pressed his knuckles hard against his sternum. The clock on his screen read 12:03. He pulled up her LinkedIn, then her university profile, then a conference presentation she'd given in March that he watched on mute while eating crackers over his keyboard. He watched it twice.

Maya found out on a Tuesday, through a department email she almost deleted. She read it three times standing in the cereal aisle of the grocery store, her basket cutting into the crook of her elbow until she finally set it down on the floor. Professor Alderman was accepting a position at Edinburgh. Effective end of semester. She left the basket where it was and walked out.

By midnight she had drafted and deleted eleven emails. The twelfth one she sent. It was not her best work. It asked, among other things, whether she could relocate to Scotland to continue her research under his supervision, whether he could petition the department to delay his departure by one academic year, and whether there was any precedent for a remote advising arrangement across international institutions. She included her CV as an attachment, as if he had forgotten who she was.

She showed up to his office hours the next morning before he'd unlocked his door, sitting cross-legged on the floor of the hallway with her laptop open and four years of annotated research spread in a semicircle around her. When he arrived with his coffee and saw her, he stopped walking. She got to her feet too quickly and knocked half the pages loose. "I have a plan," she said, her voice coming out higher than she intended. "I have several, actually. I printed them." She bent to gather the scattered pages, and when she straightened up her eyes were very bright and she was pressing her lips together hard.

I heard it from another grad student, which is somehow worse than hearing it officially. Kowalski's leaving. Taking some endowed chair position at Northwestern, which is, objectively, great for him. I know that. I understand that intellectually. I sat with that understanding for about forty-five seconds before I started making a list of every faculty member in the department who could theoretically replace him, rating them on a scale I invented on the spot.

There are currently seven browser tabs open on my laptop. One is the Northwestern faculty page, because I spent twenty minutes trying to figure out if there's any way to make the commute work before I caught myself and closed it and then immediately reopened it. One is the university's policy on mid-program advisor transitions. One is a Reddit thread from 2019 titled "advisor left mid-dissertation, what now" that I have refreshed four times even though the content hasn't changed. I keep reading the replies where things worked out okay. I don't let my eyes stop on the other ones.

My coffee has gone cold. I made it two hours ago and haven't touched it. I've been chewing the drawstring of my hoodie, something I haven't done since I was nine years old waiting for my parents to come home from the hospital. I have three years of work and one published chapter and a methodology that only Kowalski fully understands, built on a theoretical framework we developed together in office hours over eighteen months. The cursor is blinking in a new email draft. I've typed "Dear Professor Kowalski" and nothing else, because I do not yet know how to say what I need to say without it sounding like exactly what it is.

Maya refreshed the scholarship portal for the eleventh time that hour, even though the decision letter was already open in another tab. The words hadn't changed. She closed the laptop, opened it again, and read them once more as if she'd somehow misunderstood plain English. Her roommate had stopped asking if she was okay somewhere around the third hour. The financial aid office closed at five. It was four forty-seven. She grabbed her backpack and ran.

The woman behind the desk had the patient, practiced expression of someone who delivered bad news professionally. Maya spread her documents across the counter without being asked—her transcript, her recommendation letters, a printed copy of her application, a handwritten list of questions she'd composed on the walk over. Her GPA was a 3.9. She'd volunteered two hundred hours at the community clinic. She'd written four drafts of the personal essay. She pushed the papers forward as if assembling a legal case. "There has to be something I can resubmit," she said. "There has to be an appeal process."

Back in her dorm room, she did the math again on a yellow legal pad. Tuition. Room and board. The work-study hours she was already maxing out. The number at the bottom of the page didn't care how many times she redrew the columns. She called her mother, hung up before the second ring, then called back. Her mother answered on the first word with the tone of someone who already knew something was wrong. Maya opened her mouth and found she had nothing to say that wouldn't cost her mother something.

She was still at her desk at two in the morning, drafting an email to the scholarship committee that had already ballooned to six paragraphs. She deleted it and started again. The campus outside her window had gone completely quiet. Somewhere in her search history were fourteen tabs: emergency grants, private scholarships with rolling deadlines, tuition payment plans, what happens if you withdraw mid-semester, how long a leave of absence can last before you lose your credits. She hadn't closed a single one.

I read the email standing outside the library, and for a second I actually thought I'd clicked on the wrong message. I read it again. Then I walked to the bike rack, sat down on the concrete next to it, and didn't move for a while. A girl I vaguely knew from my statistics class asked if I was okay, and I told her I was fine in a voice that clearly communicated I was not, and she kept walking, which was the right thing to do.

The scholarship was the whole plan. Not a backup plan, not one piece of a larger strategy—the whole thing. I'd deferred my start date by a year to strengthen my application. I'd taken an extra job at the tutoring center specifically to show financial need paired with academic commitment. My advisor had written what she described as the strongest recommendation letter of her career. I had arranged my entire life around a single point of entry, and the door had just been locked from the inside.

I called the financial aid office and got put on hold for twenty-two minutes. When someone finally picked up, I asked every question I could think of, and then I asked them again in different words, and then I asked if there was someone else I could speak to, a supervisor, a committee member, anyone who could tell me something more specific than "the selection process is highly competitive." My hand was pressed so hard against my thigh that I left a mark through my jeans.

That night I couldn't eat. I sat at my kitchen table with a bowl of cereal going soft in the milk and wrote out a list of every person I could contact, every alternative funding source I hadn't yet tried, every professor who might know someone on a selection board somewhere. My handwriting got smaller and more cramped as the page filled up, the letters pressing into each other like I was trying to fit more in than the paper could hold. I wrote until I ran out of names, and then I flipped the page over and kept going.

The electric bill was three months overdue, and the shutoff notice was folded in Renata's back pocket, creased from how many times she'd unfolded and refolded it on the bus ride over. She'd already sold her laptop, her mother's old camera, the vintage watch her father had left her. She pushed through the door of the pawn shop with her grandmother's pearl earrings in a zip-lock bag, the last thing she owned that was worth anything. The fluorescent lights made everything look slightly wrong. She was heading toward the counter when she stopped.

It was sitting in the second display case, between a tarnished cuff bracelet and a man's signet ring. The oval garnet in the yellow gold setting. The tiny cluster of seed pearls around the band. She knew the ring the way she knew her own face—she had watched her grandmother twist it when she was nervous, had held it briefly at the estate sale before her aunt pocketed it, claiming seniority. Renata pressed her fingertips to the glass. Her throat closed.

She flagged down the clerk before she'd made any conscious decision to do so. "That ring. The garnet one. When did it come in?" Her voice came out smaller than she intended. The clerk shrugged, pulling out a ledger. About six weeks ago, he said. Came in with a lot of estate pieces. Renata nodded slowly, already calculating: $340 on the tag, minus what the pearls would bring, minus the shutoff fee, minus—the numbers collapsed in on themselves. She pressed her palm flat against the case.

"Can I hold it?" The clerk unlocked the case and placed it in her hand. It was lighter than she remembered. She turned it over and found the tiny engraving on the inside of the band, the initials she knew would be there: *M.A.K.* Marta Alena Kovač. Her grandmother's maiden name. Renata closed her fingers around it. She stood there for a long moment, perfectly still, while the clerk waited and the fluorescent lights hummed and the electric bill crinkled softly in her back pocket every time she breathed.

I've pawned things before. I know the smell of these places, the particular quality of the silence, the way the guy behind the counter looks at you like he's already appraised your worth and found you manageable. I came in with my grandfather's old fishing reel and a box of silver-plate cutlery I'd been hauling between apartments for six years without ever using. I needed enough to cover my daughter's medication co-pay and the first week of the motel we were moving into on Friday, after the eviction. I had a number in my head. I was keeping my eyes on the number.

I was not keeping my eyes on the jewelry case, which is why it took me almost ten minutes to notice it. Then I did notice it, and I forgot the number entirely.

The ring is impossible to mistake. The setting is this strange, almost art nouveau shape, like two leaves curving around the stone, and the stone itself is an alexandrite—purple in fluorescent light, green in sunlight—and there is not another ring like it in the world because my grandfather had it made. I know this because my grandmother told me the story of it so many times I can recite it in her accent, with her pauses. She was buried in that ring. She was buried in it, and I watched them close the casket.

My hands were shaking by the time I got the clerk's attention, badly enough that I had to set the fishing reel down. I asked where the ring had come from. He said he couldn't share customer information. I told him the woman who owned it died four years ago, and that she was buried wearing it. He looked at me differently then. I could hear myself talking too fast, pitching forward over the counter, describing the alexandrite, the leaf setting, explaining what alexandrite does in different light as if I needed to prove to him that I knew it, that it was mine to know. He took it out of the case and I held it up toward the window and it went green, just like it always did, green as river water. My daughter was sitting in the car. I had the number in my head. I stood there turning the ring in the light and could not make myself put it down.

The folder had been sitting on the kitchen table for three days before Miriam finally opened it. She'd requested her complete medical history after the new oncologist said something that didn't line up—a reference to a biopsy she'd never had, a notation about a condition she'd never been diagnosed with. Now she spread the papers across the table and started cross-referencing, using a yellow highlighter that ran dry halfway through. She called the hospital's records department four times before someone picked up. "I need to speak to a supervisor," she said, her voice very quiet and very even in the way it got when she was holding something together with both hands. The hold music played for eleven minutes. She counted.

The supervisor confirmed it: somewhere in the system, her file had been merged with another patient's. Wrong treatments flagged, wrong history attached, wrong risk factors noted. For two years, her doctors had been reading the wrong story. Miriam set the phone down without ending the call and stood at the window looking at her neighbor's sprinkler going back and forth across the lawn. Her follow-up appointment was in four days. The appointment where they would decide her treatment plan. She picked the phone back up. "I need every corrected document reissued and flagged urgent before Friday morning," she said. "I understand that's a short timeline. I'm going to need you to make it work."

She slept two hours that night. By 6 AM she had drafted a written complaint, a list of every physician who had accessed the incorrect file, and a timeline of every decision that might have been affected. Her handwriting, usually looping and unhurried, had gone tight and cramped, letters pressing hard into the paper. She made coffee she didn't drink. She called her sister and hung up before it connected, not ready to say it out loud yet, not ready for the conversation to become real in that way. She sat back down at the table and opened her laptop and started again from the top.

---

I found out on a Tuesday, which feels significant in a way I can't explain. The nurse had this careful, neutral expression, the kind people practice. She told me there had been an administrative error—another patient with a similar name, admitted the same week I was, seven years ago. Some of their records had been folded into mine. I nodded slowly, the way you nod when you're not actually processing language anymore but your body is still performing comprehension.

I drove home and sat in my car in the parking garage for forty minutes. Seven years. I'd made decisions based on those records. I'd told every new doctor that I had a family history I might not actually have. I'd taken a medication for three years to manage a risk factor that might have belonged to someone else entirely. My hands were doing something strange on the steering wheel, gripping and releasing, gripping and releasing, like they were trying to wring something out of the air.

The thing nobody tells you is that the anger comes later. At first there's just this frantic, clawing need to know the true version—to find out what's actually mine and what isn't, which parts of my own history are real. I called my GP from the parking garage. I called the hospital's patient advocacy line. I left a voicemail for the specialist I'd seen last spring, my words coming faster than I intended, tripping over each other. I heard myself say, "I just need someone to tell me what is actually true about my own body," and then I had to stop talking for a moment.

I made a spreadsheet that night. Every diagnosis, every medication, every test result, color-coded by whether I could independently verify it or not. The yellow column—unverified, possibly not mine—was longer than I could look at without my vision going a little strange at the edges. I kept adding rows. I kept going back to check ones I'd already checked. Outside, the neighborhood went quiet, and then quieter, and I was still at the table when the sky started to lighten.

Maya refreshed her follower list for the seventh time that morning, sitting cross-legged on her bed at 6 AM. There it was again: @MrCalloway_Lit. She hadn't imagined it. Her English teacher had followed her private account three days ago, and she hadn't slept a full night since. She opened her drafts folder—four different messages she'd started writing to the school counselor, each one deleted before she got to the second paragraph.

She arrived at school forty minutes early and waited outside the main office until the vice principal's car pulled into the lot. Mrs. Okafor barely had her coat off before Maya was in the doorway. "I need to talk to someone," she said, her voice coming out thinner than she intended. "It's about a teacher." She had her phone in her hand, screen already open to the evidence, her thumbnail pressing so hard into the case it had left a crescent-shaped dent.

The vice principal listened carefully, asked her to sit, asked her to start from the beginning. Maya talked fast, her knee bouncing under the desk. She'd written notes on her hand because she was afraid she'd forget something important, and she kept glancing down at the smeared ink to make sure she covered everything. When Mrs. Okafor said, "We take this seriously, I want you to know that," Maya let out a breath she'd been holding so long her ribs ached. But she still checked her phone twice before she left the office, just to make sure the follow request was still visible. Just to make sure she hadn't fabricated it. Just to make sure someone finally, finally knew.

I found out on a Tuesday, which is the kind of mundane detail that somehow makes the whole thing worse. I was lying on my couch going through my followers list out of boredom and I stopped on a name I recognized: Ms. Peralta. Twenty-six years old, second-year teacher, grades my lab reports with little encouraging stickers on them. Following my account. An account where I'd posted, three weeks ago, a photo of myself crying in a McDonald's parking lot with the caption "thriving" as a joke for my friends.

I sat up so fast I knocked my water bottle off the coffee table and didn't pick it up. I just stared at my phone with my mouth open, water spreading across the floor.

I screenshotted it and sent it to four different friends with no message, just the image. Then I lay back down on the couch, held my phone over my face, and went through my entire grid, post by post, the way a person inventories a house after a break-in. The crying photo. A video of me doing a bad impression of my dad. A series of stories I'd archived about not wanting to go to school the week after my grandfather died. I couldn't breathe right. My chest kept doing this thing where it felt too small for whatever was inside it.

By midnight I'd made my account private, changed my username, and written a text to my older sister asking if I could call her. She called back immediately, which meant my voice must have sounded exactly as strange as I thought it did.

The cardboard boxes had been stacked in her mother's closet for thirty years, and Maya only found them because she was looking for something to sell. The estate sale was in four days. The mortgage company had stopped leaving voicemails and started sending certified letters, which felt like a different category of bad. She pulled the first box down and the flap fell open on its own, releasing the smell of old paper and dried glue. A macaroni self-portrait stared up at her, her seven-year-old name written in her mother's handwriting on the back with the date. Below it, a book report on Charlotte's Web. Below that, a math test with a gold star sticker still bright after decades. Her mother had kept all of it. Every single thing Maya had ever handed in.

She sat down on the closet floor without meaning to. There were four more boxes. She opened the second one and found her middle school years, a geography project about Brazil, a poem she'd written about autumn that she had no memory of writing, her own handwriting looking foreign and young. The certified letter from the bank was still in her back pocket. She could feel its edge pressing into her hip. She kept pulling papers out anyway, laying them in careful piles around her on the carpet, reading her own forgotten words, running her thumb across her mother's neat labels. Outside, a neighbor's lawnmower started up. Maya didn't move. She had an appointment with a bankruptcy attorney in two hours and she hadn't eaten since yesterday morning and she couldn't stop reading a book report she'd written in fourth grade about a spider who saved a pig's life.

I've been calling the grief counselor back for three weeks now and hanging up before it rings. What I keep telling myself is that I don't have time, which is partially true. What's also true is that I came home to pack up my mother's house and instead I've been sitting in the middle of her bedroom floor for six hours surrounded by my entire academic history, which she apparently treated like archival material. Every worksheet. Every creative writing assignment. Every science fair rubric with the teacher's comments. I found a finger-painting from kindergarten in a plastic sleeve.

My hands won't stop shaking, which has been happening a lot lately and which I've been ignoring. I have a flight back in thirty-six hours. I haven't listed the house yet. I haven't called the lawyer about the will. I told my boss I'd only need a week and that was three weeks ago and I've been responding to his emails with progressively shorter sentences. I pick up another paper. Third grade spelling test, one hundred percent, a small smiley face drawn by my teacher in red pen. My mother had written the date in pencil in the corner, so lightly I almost miss it, the way she did with everything she wanted to preserve without changing.

I press my back against the side of her bed and pull my knees to my chest. The to-do list on my phone has forty-seven items on it. There is a box of her things I need to donate and a box I need to throw away and a box I need to keep and I haven't been able to sort anything into any of them. I hold a paper up, a fifth-grade essay about what I wanted to be when I grew up. A marine biologist. I'd completely forgotten. I read it twice. Then I read it a third time, very slowly, like the answer to something is buried in there if I can just get to it.

My daughter hasn't slept in three days. I know this because I haven't slept in three days either, lying awake listening to the sounds coming from the garage—the whir of the lathe, the occasional clatter of a chisel hitting concrete. Woodworking was mine. Thirty years mine. I started when she was still in diapers, building birdhouses and small shelves, eventually graduating to furniture that people actually paid for. I taught her the basics when she was twelve, thinking it would be something we shared on weekends. That was eight months ago.

The pediatric sleep specialist's voicemail is full. I've called four times this week. I wrote the number on a Post-it that's been stuck to my palm so long it's absorbed into the creases of my skin. My daughter is fifteen and has lost nine pounds since September, and when I stand in the doorway of the garage watching her sand a cabinet door at two in the morning, her shoulder blades moving like wings under her shirt, I see something in her eyes that I recognize and cannot name.

She showed me the portfolio last Tuesday. Sixty-three pieces in eight months—I counted them after she went to bed, going page by page, my coffee going cold beside me. The work is extraordinary. A rocking chair with hand-carved joinery. A jewelry box with dovetail corners so tight you could hold them up to light and see nothing through the seam. My best work at fifteen was a lopsided step stool that wobbled if you breathed on it. I sat with the portfolio open in my lap for a long time.

"You have to eat something before you go out there," I told her this morning, sliding a plate in front of her. She picked up a piece of toast and held it, nodding, her eyes already somewhere else—some blueprint running behind them. She set the toast down untouched when she thought I wasn't looking. I picked it up and held it out to her again. We stood like that across the kitchen island, not speaking, the toast between us, until her eyes finally focused and she took it from my hand and actually ate it. It felt like I'd won something, though I couldn't have said what.

The school counselor had used the word "intervention" twice during the phone call, and Gerald had been replaying those two syllables in his head ever since, pacing the kitchen with his phone pressed hard against his sternum. His son Owen was eleven years old and had, over the past year, accumulated more vinyl records than Gerald had gathered across four decades of collecting. Owen had also stopped attending lunch at school, spending the period instead in the music room with a turntable the teacher had allowed him to bring in, cataloging pressings and release dates in a notebook he carried everywhere.

The record store owner, Pete, called Gerald that same evening. Pete was a careful man, deliberate with words, not given to overstatement. He told Gerald that Owen had been coming in every day after school and that last week he'd noticed the boy eating a granola bar for dinner while flipping through crates. Pete said it gently, the way you tell someone their tire is flat.

Gerald drove to his ex-wife's apartment, where Owen had been staying, and sat in the car for several minutes before going in. Through the window he could see the light on in Owen's room. He could picture exactly what was in there: the stacks sorted by genre, then by year, then by country of pressing. The inner sleeves removed and stored separately. The handwritten index cards in the wooden box Gerald himself had made for his own collection years ago and given to Owen last Christmas, thinking it would be a sweet gesture, thinking it would be something they'd do together casually, on quiet Sunday afternoons.

Owen answered the door before Gerald could knock. The boy's eyes were bright and immediately he began talking—something about a rare Blue Note pressing he'd found that afternoon, the matrix numbers, the condition of the dead wax. Gerald let him talk. He stood in the hallway and let his son's voice wash over him, watching the color in Owen's cheeks, the urgency in his hands as he gestured. Then Gerald noticed the calluses on Owen's fingertips from the record edges. Noticed the shadows beneath his eyes. Noticed that the dinner his mother had left on the counter was still wrapped in plastic.

"Come eat with me," Gerald said, when Owen finally paused for breath.

"I want to show you the pressing first."

"Bring it to the table."

Owen looked at him, seeming to sense something in his father's voice that stopped him. He tucked the record carefully under his arm and followed Gerald into the kitchen without another word. Gerald unwrapped the dinner and divided it onto two plates with hands that were not entirely steady.

The coffee shop was supposed to be a shortcut. Maya had ducked in to use the bathroom before her eviction hearing, the one where she'd have to explain to a judge why three months of rent had gone unpaid while she chased a record deal that kept almost happening. She was already running late, already calculating which of her remaining possessions could be sold, when she heard it. Her chord progression. Her bridge. The specific drop she'd spent six weeks getting right. A young woman with a ukulele sat in the corner, eyes closed, performing to maybe twelve people with paper cups. Maya stood completely still in the middle of the floor while someone behind her asked her to move. She didn't move. She pulled out her phone with hands that wouldn't quite cooperate, trying to record it, then trying to find the original voice memo she'd sent to her producer fourteen months ago. The timestamp. She needed the timestamp. She knocked over a tip jar reaching past someone at the counter and didn't stop to apologize. The performer opened her eyes and smiled at the smattering of applause. Maya was already calling her producer, walking in tight circles near the door, the eviction hearing now seventeen minutes away and entirely forgotten.

I recognized it before the second bar was finished. That opening riff—the one I wrote at four in the morning in my car outside a Walgreens because I couldn't afford the electricity to stay up in my apartment—coming out of a speaker on a television mounted above a hospital waiting room. My mother was somewhere behind two sets of double doors having a procedure that the doctor had used too many careful words to describe, and I hadn't slept in thirty-one hours, and now there was my song underneath some commercial for insurance or medication or something with a blue sky in the advertisement, I couldn't process that part. I stood up. Sat down. Stood up again. The man beside me asked if I needed water. I pointed at the screen like that would explain anything. I had emails somewhere in my phone, drafts I'd sent to labels, a copyright registration I'd paid sixty dollars for when sixty dollars was rent money. My hands were going through my bag before I knew what I was looking for. The song lasted maybe forty seconds in the commercial. I watched it twice more on my phone, then called my sister, then hung up before it connected because what would I even say first—the song, or our mother, or the fact that I was sitting on an orange plastic chair watching my entire unpaid future play out in a thirty-second loop on a television that was also showing a ticker about rain.

The coffee in Maya's cup had gone cold an hour ago. She kept turning her phone over on the table, screen-down, then screen-up, then screen-down again. The woman across from her—Priya, her name was Priya, she'd introduced herself with a handshake that neither of them had really committed to—was doing the same thing with a paper napkin, folding and unfolding it into smaller and smaller squares. They'd met through a mutual friend who'd finally, finally said something, and now here they were in this too-bright café with Daniel's texts still glowing on both their screens, the same endearments in the same order, sent twelve minutes apart.

"He told me his mother was sick," Maya said, not because it was the most important thing, but because her mouth needed to be doing something. "That's why he couldn't come to my sister's wedding. His mother. Who I now know lives in Portland and plays tennis three times a week because you showed me her Instagram." She laughed, and the sound came out wrong, too high and too short. She pressed her fingers flat against the table to stop them from shaking.

Priya had driven four hours to be here. Maya hadn't asked her to—she'd just shown up, texted from the parking lot, said *I'm outside if that's okay.* It wasn't okay, exactly, but Maya had walked out anyway, because what was the alternative? Sitting alone with the information? She'd already tried that for six days. She hadn't been able to eat a full meal. She'd called her mother twice and hung up before the second ring. She'd gone to Daniel's apartment building and sat outside it in her car for forty minutes before driving away without getting out, hating herself the entire way home, and then going back the next morning.

"I need to understand the timeline," Maya said, pulling out the notes app on her phone where she'd already built a color-coded calendar. "I need to understand exactly how long, and exactly how—" She stopped. Priya was staring at the calendar with an expression Maya recognized because she'd seen it in her bathroom mirror that morning. "Just tell me when he said he loved you for the first time," Maya said. Her voice was very quiet and very even. "Just tell me that one thing."

I found her through his Instagram. A tag he'd forgotten to untag, a comment thread I probably should have stopped reading after the second reply. Her name was Simone and she had warm eyes in her profile photo and a bio that said *dog mom, bad cook, trying my best,* and I sat with my phone in my hands for a long time before I typed out a message I deleted and retyped eleven times.

She called me instead of messaging back, which I wasn't prepared for. I had to walk into my bathroom and run the faucet so my roommates wouldn't hear me. We talked for two hours the first time, then three hours two nights later, reconstructing a shared calendar out of canceled plans and convenient excuses and the specific lie about the work conference in Austin that we'd both been given in the same week of the same month. By the end of the second call I had four pages of handwritten notes and a headache that started at the base of my skull and didn't leave for days.

The thing I kept coming back to wasn't even the betrayal—or it wasn't only that. It was the gap. The not-knowing. Marcus had looked at me the way people look at things they mean, or he'd done something so close to it that I still couldn't find the seam. I went back through two years of text messages in a single night, crouched on my kitchen floor with my back against the cabinet, reading and re-reading as if the words might rearrange themselves into something that made sense. My eyes were burning by 3 AM. I made coffee I didn't drink.

Simone wanted to confront him together. She'd already drafted a message, had it sitting in her notes app ready to go, and she read it to me over the phone with this terrible steadiness in her voice that told me she'd read it to herself many times already. I said I needed to think about it. Then I said yes. Then I said I didn't know. I told her I'd call her back by Sunday and it was already Thursday and I hadn't called. Instead I'd driven past his apartment twice, not stopping, just driving past, watching the lit window on the second floor, my hands at ten and two on the wheel, going around the block and coming back like I was caught in something with no clear exit.

She had rehearsed this moment for six months. Not this exact moment—not Jamie standing forty feet away in a slate-gray suit, laughing at something a bridesmaid said—but some version of it, the version where she was composed and glowing and clearly thriving.
