When the first sledgehammer blow landed somewhere deep inside the Delacroix walls, the vibration moved through her floorboards and up through the legs of her chair, and she pressed both hands flat against the table as if to hold the house in place, her breath coming in slow, careful measurements.

The knock at the door came just as I was finishing my coffee, and somehow I already knew it was Mr. Hargrove before I even reached the handle. He'd been eyeing that sunny strip along our shared fence line for weeks—I'd caught him measuring it with his eyes, the way a man does when he's working up to something. I opened the door and he smiled that big, open smile of his, and I felt my stomach drop straight through the floor. He launched into his pitch before I could say a word: raised beds, tomatoes, maybe some squash. Shared vegetables. Good for the neighborhood. I nodded along, but my hand was gripping the door frame so hard my knuckles had gone pale. The thing is, that strip of yard is the one place my dog runs her laps. The one place I'd planned—for three years now, quietly planned—to put in a small patio, maybe a fire pit, something for myself. I opened my mouth and heard a thin, reedy version of my voice say, "That sounds—yeah, that could work," while every muscle in my chest pulled tight. Mr. Hargrove beamed. I stood there in the doorway long after he'd walked away, watching the empty yard, wondering how I had managed to say yes when every part of me had been screaming to say no.

Della stood at her kitchen window and watched her neighbor, Carol, cross the yard with a casserole dish and a wide smile, and Della's stomach began doing something she could only describe as clenching. She set down the dish towel she'd been wringing between her hands. Carol was nice. Carol was perfectly, relentlessly nice. And that was the problem. When Carol asked for something—anything—with that particular tilt of her head, Della had never once in four years managed to refuse her. She moved to the back door and stepped out, and the late-afternoon sun felt too bright. Carol launched into it right away: she'd lost the plot at the community garden, long story, but she'd noticed Della's back corner was mostly just weeds and she was wondering— Della's throat had gone dry. She swallowed. "The back corner," she repeated, stalling, buying herself seconds she didn't know what to do with. Her heel bounced once, twice, against the wooden deck. That corner was nothing. Weeds, exactly like Carol said. There was absolutely no reasonable objection to raise, and she knew it, and knowing it somehow made the pressure behind her eyes worse. She pressed her fingers against her collarbone, a habit she'd never been able to explain. Carol was still talking—crop rotation, she was saying, nothing invasive—and Della found herself nodding in small, jerky increments, her smile stretched a little too wide to be quite natural.

I've lived next to Mr. Hensley for six years and never thought twice about him. Retired schoolteacher, beat-up Corolla in the driveway, the kind of guy who reuses grocery bags and argues about coupon expiration dates at the checkout line. So when the moving crew showed up at his house this morning and started carrying out furniture—furniture I recognized from catalogs I'd never be able to afford—I stood at my kitchen window with my coffee mug halfway to my lips and just stared. A Nakashima dining table. I know what those cost because I spent an embarrassing amount of time coveting one online last year. Then came what looked unmistakably like a Basquiat painting, wrapped in professional archival padding. My coffee went cold while I stood there. That evening I invented a reason to walk over—borrowed-sugar territory, the oldest trick—and when he opened the door I caught a glimpse of the interior. The walls were bare now, rectangles of unfaded paint marking where things had hung for years. "Just redecorating," he said pleasantly. I laughed and it came out two notes too high. My hand was trembling slightly as I held out the empty cup, and I had to concentrate to keep it still.

Delia had always assumed the Okafor house was just like hers—three bedrooms, drafty in winter, the kind of place where you noticed every repair you couldn't quite afford. She'd borrowed Adaeze Okafor's lawnmower twice, shared complaints about the neighborhood's crumbling sidewalks, and eaten her jollof rice at the block party last summer. They were equals in the small, comfortable way neighbors become equals. Then the letter arrived at Delia's door by mistake, the return address a private wealth management firm in Zurich, Adaeze's name typed cleanly above a figure—visible through the envelope's thin paper where the afternoon sun hit it just right—that made Delia's stomach drop like a stone. She sat down on her porch steps. She didn't remember sitting down. Her fingers went cold around the envelope's edges, and she realized she'd been holding her breath only when her chest began to ache. The mailbox was only forty feet away. She'd walked it a hundred times. She stood up, and her legs felt strange beneath her, like she was relearning something basic. By the time she reached Adaeze's door, she'd rehearsed four different opening lines, and when Adaeze answered with a warm smile, every single one of them disappeared, and Delia held out the letter without a single word, her jaw tight, her eyes not quite meeting her neighbor's face.

The elevator doors opened onto the fourteenth floor, and Marcus stood there a beat too long before stepping out. Marketing. The word on the frosted glass partition looked foreign, even though he'd walked past this floor a hundred times on his way to Accounting. His new lanyard badge felt stiff around his neck—he kept touching it, straightening it, as if it might suddenly look more like it belonged. Down the hall, a cluster of people laughed about something, their voices bright and easy with each other. Marcus adjusted the strap of his bag on his shoulder and told himself to move. His mouth had gone completely dry. He spotted a woman who must have been his new supervisor, walking toward him with an outstretched hand, and for one horrible moment he couldn't remember his own name.

I've already reorganized the contents of my desk drawer twice this morning, and it's barely eight o'clock. Everything I packed into that cardboard box feels wrong—too much, too little, like I can't figure out who I'm supposed to be on the third floor. Supply chain. I don't know anyone in supply chain. My stomach keeps doing this slow, rolling thing every time I think about walking into a room where I'm the new person, where I'll have to learn where the printer is and which mugs in the break room belong to people who will mind. I pick up my stapler, put it back down. Pick it up again. My colleague Dev pops his head in to say goodbye and I try to make my smile look normal, but he tilts his head slightly and asks if I'm okay. "Totally," I say, and my voice comes out about half an octave higher than usual.

I keep checking my phone even though I told myself I'd stop. Maya's texted me three times today already—*just making sure you're coming tonight*, then *don't be late*, then *okay seriously, 7pm, don't forget*—and each one made my stomach do something unpleasant. The thing is, I overheard her on the phone last week. I wasn't supposed to. I'd come back for my forgotten umbrella and the door was cracked and I heard my name and the word *party* before she noticed me and went very quiet. I've been carrying that knowledge around like a stone in my pocket ever since. Tonight is my birthday. I stand in front of my mirror and practice a face that looks surprised, opening my eyes wide, dropping my jaw. It looks completely fake. I try again. My hands won't stop fidgeting with the hem of my shirt. I'm halfway out the door when my phone buzzes one more time—Maya again—and the message reads: *change of plans, just dinner, the usual place*. I stand in my doorway for a long moment, reading it twice, three times. Just dinner. The usual place. I press my back teeth together and stare at those four words until my vision blurs slightly at the edges. Then I type back a single *okay* and walk to my car, where I sit without starting the engine for longer than I should.

Daniel had already changed his shirt twice. The first one had felt too formal, the second too casual, and now he stood in the third with his collar slightly askew, which he didn't notice because he was watching the street through the front window. He'd been told 6:30—come at 6:30, his friend Greg had said, very specifically, with the kind of deliberate casualness that was itself a giveaway. Daniel had been counting down to it for days, which meant he'd also had days to imagine everything that could go wrong. He pressed his fingertips to the windowpane. The glass was cold. At 6:14, his phone rang, and before Greg even finished his first sentence—*so, about tonight*—Daniel's stomach had already dropped somewhere below the floor. Greg's voice had that particular quality to it, apologetic and rushed, explaining that the venue had double-booked, that people had canceled, that it just wasn't going to happen the way they'd planned. "We'll reschedule," Greg said. "It'll be better, honestly." Daniel made sounds of understanding in the right places. He said it was completely fine. After he hung up, he stood very still in the center of his living room, dressed in his third-best shirt, with nowhere he needed to be, and the silence of the apartment settled around him like a held breath he couldn't quite release.

The email wasn't meant for me. I could tell that much from the subject line alone: "Re: Acquisition Timeline - CONFIDENTIAL." My cursor hovered over it for a full ten seconds before I clicked. Someone had accidentally CC'd the entire marketing department instead of just the executive team. I read it three times, each pass making the words feel less real. Binding offer. Due diligence. Transition of staff. My coffee went cold beside me while I sat there. When my colleague Dana swung by my desk to ask about the Henderson account, I heard my own voice answer her in some flat, distant register that didn't quite sound like mine. After she left, I realized I'd been gripping the edge of my desk hard enough that my knuckles had gone white. I stood up to get some water, sat back down, stood up again. The office around me kept humming along — printers printing, phones ringing, someone laughing in the kitchen — and I couldn't understand how any of that was still happening.

Miriam had been holding the printed memo for so long that the paper had grown soft at the edges where her fingers kept folding and unfolding the same corner. She'd found it face-down on the shared printer, left behind by someone who'd forgotten to collect it, and now she stood in the break room unable to make herself put it down. Strategic acquisition. Pending board approval. All employees to be notified in due course. Her stomach had clenched itself into something small and hard. She set the memo on the counter and immediately picked it back up. Fourteen years. She'd given this company fourteen years. When her manager, Gil, walked in to refill his mug, Miriam turned toward the window and pretended to check her phone, not trusting what her face might be doing. "You alright?" Gil asked, because apparently she wasn't as composed as she'd hoped. "Fine," she said, her voice coming out a half-step too high. "Just tired." She smoothed the memo flat against her thigh, folded it into quarters, and slipped it into her pocket, already dreading the long hours before she'd know anything more.

The lawyer's office smelled like old paper and central heating, and Maya kept counting the ceiling tiles. Fourteen across, nine down. She'd done the math three times. Across the mahogany table, her brother Daniel sat very still, which was worse somehow than if he'd been fidgeting—Daniel was never still. The manila envelope in front of each of them had been sitting there for six minutes while Mr. Hargrove explained procedural things Maya couldn't absorb. Their mother had been gone eleven days. "You may open them now," Mr. Hargrove said, and Maya's fingers fumbled so badly with the envelope's clasp that she bent back a nail. Inside, a single page. She read it once, then again. Then she looked up at Daniel, who had gone the color of old milk. His envelope sat open. His page sat face-up. Even from across the table, she could see that his document was longer than hers. Much longer.

I keep telling myself it's just paper. Documents. Legal language that doesn't mean anything about who Mom loved more, or less, or differently. I've read my half of the will four times sitting in my car in the parking garage, and each time my stomach does the same slow drop it did when I was a kid on the high dive, frozen at the top. My brother Theo called me twenty minutes ago, voice too careful, too measured, asking if I'd "had a chance to review the materials." That's how I knew. That's how I knew his version said something different than mine. I'm gripping the steering wheel even though the car is off. The document says the house goes to me—just me—and a separate account goes to Theo. What I can't stop thinking about is whether Theo's paperwork explains why. Mine doesn't say a word.

I've checked the volume on my TV three times since I heard the knock. It's not even that loud—I had it at maybe a third of the way up, just enough to drown out the sound of the rain. But now I'm standing at my own front door with my hand on the knob, unable to make myself turn it. My upstairs neighbor, Mr. Calloway, is not what you'd call an approachable man. He once left a handwritten note on my car because I parked six inches too close to his spot. I take a slow breath and open the door. He's standing there in his bathrobe, arms crossed, mouth already set in a hard line. I open my mouth to speak and nothing comes out for a full second—just air—before I manage: "Mr. Calloway. Hi." I can feel my pulse in my fingertips where they grip the doorframe. The hallway light feels very bright.

Dani had turned the music off the moment she heard the pounding on the wall—three heavy, deliberate thuds that rattled the framed photo above her desk. Now she stood in the hallway between her apartment and the one next door, her fist raised to knock, then lowered, then raised again. She'd only moved in six weeks ago. She hadn't even learned the woman's name yet, just knew her as Unit 4B, the one with wind chimes on the balcony. Dani knocked before she could talk herself out of it and then immediately wanted to disappear into the carpet. Her mouth had gone completely dry. When the door swung open, she found herself face to face with a small elderly woman in a cardigan who looked more exhausted than angry. "I'm so sorry," Dani started, and her voice came out half an octave higher than normal. She cleared her throat, pressed her thumbnail hard into her palm. "I didn't realize how thin these walls were." She could hear her own heartbeat in the silence that followed.

The envelope had been sitting on my kitchen counter for three hours, and I still hadn't opened it. I'd recognized the property management company's logo the moment I picked it up from the mat, and something cold had spread through my chest like ice water. Now I kept walking past it, touching it, putting it back down. I made tea I didn't drink. I reorganized the dish rack. My hands wouldn't stay still — I found myself straightening the same stack of mail four times. When I finally tore it open, I had to read the first sentence twice because the words kept sliding away from me. *Notice of Eviction.* My mouth went completely dry. I sat down on the floor right there in the kitchen, back against the cabinet, knees pulled to my chest, trying to remember if I had a copy of my lease somewhere.

Maya stood at her window reading the notice a second time, her thumb leaving damp smudges on the paper. Thirty days. The radiator hissed and clanked in the corner of her studio apartment, same as it always had, indifferent to everything. She folded the letter and unfolded it. Folded it again. Her landlord, Mr. Solis, picked up on the fourth ring, and the moment she heard his voice her own seemed to retreat somewhere deep in her throat. "I got something in the mail," she started, then stopped. Her free hand pressed flat against the windowpane, cold glass against her palm. When she finally managed to say the word *eviction*, her voice came out uneven and too small, not the composed, reasonable tone she had rehearsed in the sixty seconds before he'd answered. She cleared her throat. She cleared it again. Outside, a pigeon landed on the fire escape railing, regarded her briefly, and flew away.

I keep refreshing the journal's website like it will somehow change what I'm seeing. My article. My eighteen months of research, my late nights, my argument about post-colonial land rights that my advisor called "genuinely original work." Right there on the homepage, listed under the name Dr. Harlan Webb. I press my fingers against my lips. My coffee has gone cold and I haven't touched it. I draft an email to the editorial board, then delete it. Draft it again. My stomach keeps doing something I can't quite describe—a slow, liquid drop, like missing a step in the dark. I read the abstract and it's word for word. Even the unusual phrasing I'd second-guessed, the sentence I'd rewritten eleven times. I stand up from my desk. Sit back down. I call my advisor and when her voicemail picks up I realize I don't know how to begin, so I hang up without leaving a message. Outside my window, the city is going about its morning with complete indifference.

Delia had printed the article out and laid it flat on the kitchen table, smoothing it with both hands as if trying to iron out the wrongness of it. Dr. Marcus Finch. The byline sat above her introduction, her methodology, her carefully sourced conclusion about antibiotic resistance in rural hospital networks. She'd submitted it to the journal four months ago and heard nothing—she'd assumed rejection. Her jaw ached from clenching it. She kept moving things around the table for no reason: the salt shaker, a mug, the stapled pages themselves. When her housemate wandered in and asked if she was okay, Delia said "fine" in a voice that came out clipped and strange, and then had to clear her throat twice. She looked up Finch's institutional email. Then his department head's. Then the journal's ethics board. She wrote none of them. She stood at the window with her arms crossed tight over her chest, watching a sparrow land on the fire escape and immediately leave again, and she breathed very deliberately through her nose, the way her therapist had taught her, before she went back to the table and began to type.

The grade is right there at the top of the page: 54%. I keep blinking at it like the number might rearrange itself into something reasonable. My essay is coming back to me in fragments—the thesis I'd rewritten four times, the sources I'd tracked down at eleven at night, the conclusion I was actually proud of. I set the paper face-down on my desk and press my hands flat against the surface to keep them still. Around me, people are flipping through their pages, scribbling in the margins, whispering to each other. Someone two rows over says "B-plus, not bad" and I feel my stomach drop another floor. When Professor Haines asks if anyone has questions about the rubric, my hand floats up before I've made a conscious decision to raise it. Then I lose my nerve and lower it again, pretending I was just stretching. My pen keeps clicking. I don't remember picking it up.

Dani had checked the portal three times that morning before class, each time convinced the grade would have corrected itself somehow, the way you're certain a misspelled word will look right if you stare at it long enough. It hadn't. A 61 sat on the screen like something permanent. She'd arranged her notes into a neat stack on the desk in front of her, squared the corners twice, then squared them again. When Mr. Okafor called her name during attendance, her "here" came out smaller than she'd intended, barely reaching the front of the room. She kept rehearsing phrases in her head—*I worked really hard on this*, *I don't understand where I went wrong*, *is there any possibility of a resubmission*—but every version sounded either too confrontational or too desperate. Her foot tapped a rapid, involuntary rhythm against the chair leg. When the bell rang for the end of class, she let the room empty almost completely before gathering her things, moving slowly toward his desk, swallowing once, then swallowing again.

The email had been sitting in my inbox for three days before I finally opened it. Financial Aid Office – Urgent Matter Regarding Your Award. I'd told myself it was probably just a form to sign, some bureaucratic thing. Now I'm standing outside the administration building, rereading the words scholarship disbursement error on my phone screen for what must be the fifteenth time. My breakfast is sitting wrong in my stomach. I push through the glass doors and immediately forget which floor the financial aid office is on, even though I've been here twice before. I check the directory. Second floor. My hand leaves a damp print on the handle of the stairwell door.

The woman at the desk, Ms. Okafor, has the kind of expression that gives nothing away. She asks me to sit. I perch on the edge of the plastic chair instead of settling into it, my backpack still on, like I might need to run. She slides a manila folder across the desk toward me and taps a line near the top of a document. The name printed there is not mine. It's close – same last name, one letter different in the first – and for a split second my brain tries to convince me it's a typo. Then she says, "We believe this award was processed for the wrong recipient," and I watch her mouth keep moving but the sound seems to be coming from very far away.

I ask her what happens now, and the question comes out strangely flat, like I've ironed all the inflection out of it. She explains something about a review process, a timeline, temporary enrollment holds. I nod along and write nothing down, even though I've had a pen in my hand since I sat down. At some point I realize I've been clicking it open and closed, open and closed, without noticing. I set it on the desk. She slides it back to me with a small, not unkind look. "Do you have any questions?" I have approximately four hundred questions. I cannot locate a single one of them.

Outside, the September air is sharp enough that I should feel better. I sit on the low brick wall beside the entrance and pull out my phone to call my mom, then put it back in my pocket. Pull it out again. My roommate texts asking if I want to grab lunch and I stare at the message for a long time. Down the hill, students are crossing the quad in easy clusters, laughing about something, someone's coffee lid flying off in the wind. I watch a girl chase it across the grass. It seems like something happening on a screen. My knee won't stop bouncing.

Maya had read the letter so many times the paper had gone soft at the folds. She'd found it that morning tucked into her campus mailbox between a dining hall flyer and a library notice, and she'd stood right there in the mail room reading it twice before folding it back up and walking to her 9 a.m. lecture and sitting through the entire thing without absorbing a single word. The letter was from the Hartwell Foundation. It said, with considerable bureaucratic delicacy, that a clerical error had resulted in the award being extended to her rather than its intended recipient, one Amara Mensah, and requested that she contact their office at her earliest convenience.

She sat in the campus coffee shop now, her untouched latte developing a cold skin on the surface. Across from her, her friend Deja was talking about something – a professor, a deadline, a party on Friday – and Maya kept nodding at what she hoped were appropriate intervals. Her leg was bouncing under the table hard enough that the mugs rattled, once, and Deja paused and looked at her. "Are you okay? You look kind of pale." Maya smoothed her expression into something she hoped was reassuring. "Long night," she said, which was true, though not for any of the reasons Deja might assume.

The thing that kept snagging in her mind, the thing she couldn't get past, was the timeline. She'd used the scholarship money to pay her housing deposit. She'd deferred her campus job because she thought she had the funding covered through spring. She'd called her mother in October, voice bright with relief, and said, *Mom, it's handled, stop worrying.* Her mother had cried a little. Maya had felt, for the first time in a long time, like the ground was solid under her feet. She pressed her thumb into the edge of her coffee cup until she could feel the ceramic ridge biting into her skin.

The Foundation's number was already saved in her phone. She'd typed it in during the lecture, apparently, though she didn't remember doing it. The contact name she'd saved it under was just a question mark. She kept opening the contact screen and closing it again, the phone face-down, face-up, face-down. A barista called out an order and Maya flinched so hard that Deja reached across the table and touched her arm. "Seriously," Deja said, quieter now. "What's going on?" Maya looked at her friend's face, then down at the letter folded into a small, soft square in her jacket pocket, and found that her mouth had gone completely dry.

The bouquet sat on my kitchen counter like an accusation. Twelve white roses, stems freshly cut, wrapped in brown paper that smelled faintly of a flower shop I didn't recognize. My downstairs neighbor said a delivery driver had left them propped against my door around noon. No card. I'd already torn through the wrapping twice, convinced I'd missed something tucked inside. I hadn't. I stood at the counter picking at the edge of my thumbnail, running through the list in my head—my mother never sent flowers without a phone call first, my best friend was backpacking through Portugal with no signal, and the person I'd been seeing for the past three weeks didn't yet know my address. At least, I didn't think he did. I picked up my phone, set it back down, picked it up again. The roses were beautiful. That almost made it worse. I went to the window and looked down at the street below, scanning for nothing in particular, my breath fogging the glass in small, shallow clouds.

Delilah had read somewhere that white lilies symbolized innocence, and yellow ones meant friendship, and red roses meant something she didn't want to think about right now. The arrangement on her porch step was made up of deep red dahlias—she'd had to look that one up—and the florist's website offered several contradictory interpretations. She carried the vase inside and set it on the dining table, then immediately moved it to the windowsill, then to the coffee table, as if its location in her home might change the meaning of it. Her stomach had been doing something unpleasant since she'd first spotted the flowers an hour ago. She texted her roommate: *Did you order something for me?* Then her coworker Brid: *Random question but did you send flowers?* Then, after a long pause, staring at the contact name she hadn't texted in seven months: nothing. She couldn't quite bring herself to do it. She stood in the middle of her living room with her arms crossed tight over her chest, eyes moving back to the dahlias every few seconds. They were stunning, genuinely. She wished she could just enjoy them. Through the window she watched a car slow at the end of her driveway, then continue on, and she let out a long breath she hadn't realized she'd been holding.

I find the café before Maya does, which gives me twenty minutes to rearrange the sugar packets and check my phone for messages that aren't there. She'd responded to my Instagram DM with a smiley face and "sure, sounds fun!" — no flash of recognition, no "oh my god, is this really you?" I'd told myself it was just her texting style. Casual. Everyone's casual now. The espresso machine screams behind the counter and I flinch. When the door opens with its little bell, I see her immediately — same dark hair, taller, her face carrying all the years between us. She scans the room with polite, searching eyes. Not the eyes of someone looking for a person they know. When they land on me she smiles the way you smile at a stranger who's waved at you by accident. I stand up too fast and knock my knee on the table. "Maya," I say, and my voice does something embarrassing on the single syllable. "Hey!" she says warmly, sliding into the seat across from me. "So how do you know me again?"

Daniel stood at the door of the house for longer than was probably normal, his fist raised mid-knock. The paint was a different color — blue now instead of the yellow he'd grown up staring at from across the street. That felt like a bad omen, or maybe just a bad paint job. His mouth had gone completely dry on the drive over. He swallowed. He knocked. Footsteps, then the door swung open, and there was Chris — broader in the shoulders, a beard where there hadn't been one, but unmistakably Chris — holding a dish towel and looking at Daniel with the politely blank expression of someone answering for a delivery. "Can I help you?" The words hit Daniel somewhere around the sternum. He had prepared for awkward. He had prepared for years of distance turning into careful small talk. He had not prepared for this. His hand found the back of his own neck without his permission. "I'm Daniel," he said. "Daniel Marsh. I used to live across the street." He watched Chris's face carefully, hunting for the small tremor of recognition. Nothing surfaced. "Huh," said Chris pleasantly, leaning against the door frame. "Cool neighborhood, right? You visiting someone?"

The shovel hit something hollow, and Maya's stomach dropped. She'd only been turning the garden bed to plant tomatoes—her mother's old garden bed, which she'd inherited along with the house six months ago. She crouched down and started pulling at the dirt with her bare hands, her breath coming faster than it should for such light work. It was a metal tin, the kind that once held butter cookies, sealed with electrical tape gone brittle with age. Maya sat back on her heels and turned it over. Her own handwriting stared back at her from a strip of masking tape: *DO NOT OPEN UNTIL 2025.* Her mouth went dry. She set the tin down in the grass and stood up, walked to the porch, walked back. She hadn't lived in this house since she was nineteen. She picked it up again, and her hands weren't quite steady. She had no memory—none at all—of burying this. What on earth had nineteen-year-old her needed to hide so badly she'd put it in the ground?

I keep turning the capsule over in my hands, this gray PVC pipe capped at both ends, pulled from the corner of my own backyard where the oak tree's roots have started lifting the fence. I found it digging. I don't remember putting it there. I've lived in this house for eleven years, and I don't remember putting it there. I've set it on the kitchen table and I'm circling it the way my dog circles something unfamiliar in the grass—close, then backing off, then close again. My coffee has gone cold. I keep picking up my phone to call my brother and then setting it back down. What would I even say? The label on the side reads *For later. You'll know when.* That's my handwriting. I'd know it anywhere—the way I loop my L's, the way my letters lean slightly left. A dull pressure has settled behind my sternum and won't leave. Whatever I sealed inside here, some version of me decided I wasn't ready for it then. I pull out a chair, sit down, and just stare at it for a long moment before I reach for the end cap and begin, very slowly, to twist.

The DM had been sitting in my inbox for three days before I finally opened it. A stranger's account, no profile picture, just a message that said: "I think you should see this." Attached was a photograph of someone's forearm, and there it was—my illustration of the moth with the cracked wing, the one I'd posted maybe two years ago and almost deleted because I thought it was too raw, too personal. Someone had put it on their body. Permanently. My coffee cup hovered halfway to my mouth and stayed there. I set it down without drinking. I read the message four more times, then closed the app, then opened it again. My thumbnail found the edge of my phone case and started picking at it, a habit I'd broken months ago. Was I supposed to be flattered? Angry? I typed out three different replies and deleted all of them. Outside, the city went about its business, completely indifferent to the fact that a stranger was walking around somewhere with a piece of my grief embedded in their skin.

Cara's hand froze over the guest's shoulder, her pen inches from the signing table. She recognized it immediately—the asymmetrical rose with the doubled thorns, the strange little teardrop she always hid somewhere in the petals like a signature within a signature. It was right there on the inside of the woman's wrist, rendered in clean black lines by what looked like a skilled tattooist. The woman was still talking, chattering warmly about how long she'd been a fan, but the words had become a kind of soft static. Cara's smile stayed in place through what felt like considerable effort. She glanced up once at Theo, her assistant, who was already watching her with raised eyebrows. She gave him a tiny, barely perceptible shake of her head. She looked back down at the print she was supposed to be signing and realized her hand had left a small smudge across the corner—she'd pressed down without moving the pen. "Sorry," she said, her voice coming out lighter than she intended. "Let me get you a fresh one." She reached under the table for another copy, using the extra seconds to pull a slow breath in through her nose, counting silently, while her pulse did something erratic and urgent against the inside of her wrist.

I keep walking to the window and back, to the window and back, like maybe the fourth time I check, my car will have magically reappeared in the driveway. It hasn't. There's just a rectangle of oil stain on the concrete where my Civic used to sit. My hands won't stop doing this thing where they open and close, open and close, and I've already called the towing company twice and been put on hold both times. The woman at the HOA office said in a flat voice that they had "every right," and I could hear myself starting to talk too fast, stumbling over words, not making the argument I'd rehearsed in my head at all. I have the lease agreement somewhere—I printed it out the day I moved in because something told me I'd need it—but I've rifled through the kitchen drawer three times and my vision keeps going slightly blurry at the edges. My stomach feels like a fist. The towing company's hold music is a tinny, looping guitar riff, and I realize I've been standing in the same spot for six minutes, doing nothing but listening to it.

Derek stood at the end of his driveway for a long moment before his brain fully registered what his eyes were seeing. Or rather, what they weren't seeing. He tugged at the collar of his shirt, which suddenly seemed to have shrunk since this morning. He pulled out his phone to call his wife, then put it away, then pulled it out again. A neighbor walked past with a dog and waved cheerfully, and Derek managed something that might have been a wave back. His mouth had gone completely dry. He called the city's towing hotline, and when the automated system asked him to enter his license plate number, he had to look it up in his phone because he couldn't, for the life of him, remember it. While he waited to be connected to a representative, he sat down on the front step, elbows on his knees, one foot tapping a rapid, irregular rhythm against the concrete. He'd paid his registration. He was almost certain he'd paid his registration. The thought that he might not have sent a cold crawl of something unpleasant up the back of his neck. He stood up again. The hold music played on.

The email had been sitting in my inbox for six hours before I finally opened it. "Important Announcement Regarding Westbrook College's Future." I read it three times, each time hoping the words would rearrange themselves into something less catastrophic. *Permanent closure. End of spring semester.* My coffee went cold on the desk beside me. I pulled up the financial aid portal, then closed it, then opened it again without really looking at it. I called my mom and hung up before it rang. What would I even say? I'm twenty credits away from a degree that might not exist by June? I walked to the window and stood there watching the campus below—students crossing the quad, someone tossing a frisbee, a professor hauling a box of papers—all of them moving through their Tuesday like the ground wasn't quietly dissolving beneath us.

Delia had read the announcement on her phone while standing in the cereal aisle of the grocery store, and she was still standing there twenty minutes later when a store employee asked if she needed help finding something. She shook her head quickly. In the car, she gripped the steering wheel without starting the engine. Westbrook was closing. *Her* Westbrook, where she'd spent three years, where her thesis research lived in a shared drive, where Dr. Okafor had finally agreed to be her advisor after eighteen months of her asking. Her knee jackhammered against the underside of the steering column. She thought about the transfer credit meeting she'd now have to request, the transcripts, the letters of explanation, the possibility that no one else would take her in her final year with a half-finished thesis on medieval trade routes. She pulled out her phone to text her roommate, typed and deleted four different messages, and finally settled on: *"Did you see the email?"* Three dots appeared immediately. Then: *"Yeah."* Delia stared at that single word for a long moment, her thumbnail pressed hard between her teeth.

The magazine was right there in the checkout line, glossy and unavoidable. *Southern Table's Recipe of the Year.* I picked it up with the same idle hands I always use to flip through things I won't buy, and then I stopped breathing. There it was. My grandmother's sweet potato pie. Every detail—the brown butter, the cardamom, the specific fold of the crust at the edges that Mamie called her "little braid." Attributed to a Chef Daniel Forsythe of Charleston. I set the magazine back on the rack. Then I picked it up again. My cart was blocking the aisle and someone cleared their throat behind me, but I couldn't move. I read the headnote three times: *a recipe passed down through Daniel's family for generations.* The fluorescent lights above me seemed to hum louder. I bought the magazine with fingers that wouldn't quite work the zipper on my wallet.

Rosa had told herself, driving to the restaurant, that she was probably mistaken. Lots of people made tamales with dried hibiscus and dark chocolate. It wasn't like she'd invented the concept. But she'd smoothed her skirt seventeen times at the red lights between her house and the valet stand, and now, seated at the bar with the menu open in her hands, she found herself reading the same line over and over. *Oaxacan Hibiscus Tamales — Chef Marco Reyes's signature dish, featured in Bon Appétit and The New York Times.* Her water glass left a ring on the bar top. She pressed her fingertip into it, dragged it in a slow circle. The bartender asked if she was ready to order and she said, "One more minute," in a voice so even and quiet it surprised her. She thought about the index card in her mother's handwriting, tucked inside her own recipe box at home, dated 1987. She thought about the cooking class she'd taught at the community center three years ago, the one Marco Reyes had attended for four consecutive Saturdays. Her thumbnail found the edge of the laminated menu and began, slowly, to pick at it.

The neighbor had mentioned it so casually, leaning over the fence with a coffee mug, that it took Sarah a full three seconds to process the words. A murder. In her house. In the room she'd painted sage green and called her reading nook. She excused herself and walked back inside, each step deliberate and measured, and then stood in the kitchen gripping the counter until her knuckles went white. She'd been so proud of this house. She'd told everyone about the original hardwood floors, the bay window, the claw-foot tub. Now she moved through the hallway like the walls might lean in. She opened her laptop and searched the address, telling herself she wouldn't find anything, and her stomach dropped through the floor when the first result was a headline from six years ago. She closed the laptop. Opened it again. Closed it. Her tea had gone cold beside her and she hadn't noticed, couldn't eat the toast she'd made, just stood there running her thumbnail back and forth across the edge of the counter in a small, repetitive line.

I keep telling myself it's just a house. Bricks and timber and plaster. That's all it is. I've been standing in the doorway of the master bedroom for what must be ten minutes now, one hand on the frame, unable to make myself cross the threshold the way I have every single morning for the past two years. The real estate agent knew. She had to have known. I replay every conversation we had, every cheerful walkthrough, her arm gesturing toward the crown molding, toward the garden, and I wonder what her face was doing when my back was turned. My phone buzzes in my pocket and I flinch so hard I knock my elbow against the doorframe. It's just my brother. I don't answer. Outside, a car backfires somewhere down the street and I'm halfway down the stairs before I've consciously decided to move, one hand sliding fast along the banister, heart slamming. I sit on the bottom step and stare at the front door, breathing through my nose, slow and counted. The house is entirely silent. That doesn't help.

The class list was still in my hand when I sat down at the kitchen table, though I didn't remember carrying it there. Mrs. Calloway. Room 14. I read it three more times, certain I'd misread it somehow. But no — there it was in plain black ink, the same name that had appeared at the top of every red-marked paper I'd brought home in third grade. I set the sheet down and then picked it up again. She couldn't still be teaching. That was thirty years ago. I went to the sink and filled a glass of water I didn't drink. Sophie was going to be in her class in six days. Six days. I thought about requesting a transfer to another classroom and felt my stomach clench at the thought of explaining why. *Because she told me I wasn't a reader. Because I still can't walk into a library without hearing her voice.* I pressed my knuckles against the counter until they went white.

Daniel stood in the school hallway outside Room 7, pretending to study the bulletin board covered in laminated autumn leaves. Other parents flowed past him toward the open classroom door, chatting easily, carrying baked goods in cellophane-wrapped trays. He hadn't moved in four minutes. He knew because he'd checked his phone that many times without registering what it said. Mr. Briggs. The name on the placard beside the door was printed in cheerful block letters that did nothing to soften the blow he'd felt that morning reading his son's class assignment. Mr. Briggs, who had once made a twelve-year-old Daniel stand in front of the whole class and read aloud from a textbook he'd told the man he couldn't understand. Daniel tugged at his collar. He could hear laughter from inside the room, and his son's voice among it, high and easy with excitement. His feet still wouldn't move. A mother he vaguely recognized touched his arm. "First one's the hardest," she said warmly, nodding toward the door. Daniel produced what he hoped was a smile and took one small, deliberate step forward.

My coffee has gone completely cold. I've been staring at Maya's laptop screen for what feels like an eternity, and the numbers keep swimming in front of my eyes. Same color palette. Same data visualizations. Same proposed rollout timeline, down to the specific dates I'd chosen almost arbitrarily on a Tuesday afternoon three weeks ago. My mouth has gone dry. I pick up the cold coffee anyway, just to have something to do with my hands. "Where did you say you got the brief for this?" I hear myself ask, and my voice comes out too carefully measured, too controlled, the way you talk when you're concentrating very hard on not letting your voice do something embarrassing. Maya's expression shifts. She closes the laptop an inch, then stops herself and opens it again.

Daniel spread both sets of documents across the conference table, side by side, and took a single step back. His hands had gone to his pockets. He pulled them out, crossed his arms, uncrossed them. Across the table, Sophie was perfectly still in the way that people are still when they're working very hard at it. The only sound was the ventilation system humming above them. Both proposals had the same title—nearly word for word—and Daniel had chosen that title at midnight on a sleepless Wednesday, alone in his apartment, thinking he was being terribly original. His jaw tightened. He swallowed. "So," Sophie finally said, and then didn't continue. Daniel realized he'd been holding his breath when his lungs began to ache quietly.

The email had been sitting in my inbox for six hours before I finally opened it. "Important Update Regarding My Position" — Dr. Harmon's name in the sender field, the subject line clinical and bloodless. I read it three times standing in the middle of my kitchen, and by the third time the words had stopped making sense. Accepting a position. Effective end of semester. Arrangements will be made. I set my phone face-down on the counter and stood very still for a moment, as if movement might make it more real. My thesis. Two and a half years of work, a chapter and a half written, an outline Dr. Harmon had personally annotated in red pen. I picked up my phone again, put it down again. Then I turned on the faucet and washed my hands, which didn't need washing. Then I dried them. Then I washed them again. I had a meeting with her on Thursday. I'd been planning to show her my revised introduction. I pulled out my laptop and opened the document and stared at the first paragraph until the words blurred at the edges.

Maya heard it from another grad student in the hallway outside the department office — "Professor Aldridge took a chair position at Penn, apparently it's been in the works for months" — and the rest of that sentence dissolved into a low ringing that seemed to fill the corridor. She smiled at something the other student said, nodded at the appropriate moment, and somehow made it to the stairwell before she had to stop and put her hand flat against the cold concrete wall. Her qualifying exams were in April. Her committee had been assembled specifically around Aldridge's expertise in postcolonial frameworks. She pulled out her phone to text her roommate and instead found herself staring at her own reflection in the dark screen, her jaw tight, a small muscle twitching below her left eye. The stairwell door swung open and she straightened immediately, tucking her phone away. She took the stairs to her office, where she sat down, opened her dissertation folder, and then closed it without reading a single word. She opened it again. Outside the narrow window above her desk, two pigeons landed on the ledge and then left, and she watched them go as though they carried something important with them.

The email had been sitting in my inbox for six hours before I finally clicked it. I'd been finding reasons not to — suddenly the dishes needed washing, my desk needed reorganizing, I had to reread three pages of a textbook I'd already understood. Now I'm sitting cross-legged on my bed, laptop balanced on my knees, and the screen keeps blurring because I can't seem to breathe at a normal rate. My roommate is watching something in the next room and the laugh track sounds very far away. I read the first line. Then the second. Then I close the laptop, very carefully, and place it on the mattress beside me like it's something that could break. The radiator hisses. Outside, someone rides past on a bike. I open the laptop again and read the whole thing from the top, my mouth forming the words without any sound coming out. *We regret to inform you.* My financial aid calculator is still open in another tab — all those numbers I'd arranged so carefully, like they could hold the future in place.

Maya had checked her phone four times since sitting down in the financial aid office waiting room, not because anything new had arrived, but because her hands needed something to do. She'd gotten the denial letter that morning and had immediately called her mother, then hung up before the second ring, then stared at the bathroom mirror for a full minute before deciding to come here in person. The waiting room had one other occupant — an older man reading a magazine with practiced calm — and she found herself almost envying him. She pulled at a loose thread on her sleeve until it gave way entirely. When the advisor finally called her name, Maya stood up too quickly and her bag slipped off the chair, scattering her folder of documents across the tile. She gathered them with clumsy hands, pages slipping against each other, her carefully organized records of grades and recommendations and financial disclosures suddenly all out of order. "Sorry," she said to no one in particular. "I have everything, I just—" She pressed the papers against her chest to stop them from shaking, then walked through the door.

The ring sat in the glass case like it had always belonged there, nestled between a saxophone and a stack of video games. My breath caught somewhere in my chest and stayed there. I recognized it immediately—the small oval sapphire flanked by two seed pearls, the slightly bent prong on the left side that Grandma Rose always meant to get fixed. My hand pressed flat against the cool glass before I even realized I'd moved. "Can I help you?" The man behind the counter was watching me, and I realized I'd been staring for too long. "That ring," I said, and my voice came out wrong, thinner than usual. "How long have you had it?"

The pawnshop owner had a bored expression that didn't waver. He pulled out the tray and set it on the counter, and I made myself look down at it slowly, like I was just browsing. My stomach felt like a stone dropping through water. There was the engraving on the inside of the band—*R & T, forever*—Robert and Theresa. My grandparents. I swallowed twice before I trusted my voice again. "What are you asking for it?" He named a price and I did a rapid, silent calculation of what was in my bank account, what I had in cash, what I could transfer. My thumbnail found the edge of my finger and dug in, a habit from childhood I'd never quite shaken.

I didn't want to ask the next question. I turned the ring over once, then again, stalling. The pearls caught the fluorescent light, and I thought about my grandmother's hands, how she'd gestured when she told stories, how that ring had always been part of the picture. "Do you keep records of who brings things in?" I asked, keeping my eyes on the ring instead of his face. My grandmother had been gone four years. My mother thought the ring had been buried with her. My mother didn't know what I knew, which was that my cousin Derek had been in and out of trouble lately, that he'd been at the house around Christmas, that he'd looked jumpy and apologetic in a way I hadn't wanted to name at the time. I set the ring down very carefully. My hands were steady. The rest of me was not.

Owen had ducked into the pawn shop on Delaney Street only to escape the rain, shaking water from his jacket and scanning the cluttered shelves without much interest. It was the kind of place that smelled like dust and old decisions—guitars hanging from the ceiling, boxes of silverware, a spinning rack of forgotten jewelry. He was halfway to the door when something in the glass case made him stop walking entirely. His coffee cup froze halfway to his mouth.

He crouched down until his nose was nearly touching the glass. The ring was exactly as his mother had described it over the years: white gold, an antique cut diamond, and the delicate filigree along the band that his great-grandfather had apparently spent three months' wages on in 1951. His grandmother had worn it until the day she'd moved into the care home. After that, his mother assumed it was with the rest of her things, packed in storage. Owen's leg was bouncing slightly as he crouched there, though he wasn't aware of it. He set his coffee down on the floor beside him.

"You looking at something?" The woman behind the counter had come around to his side. Owen straightened up too quickly and the blood rushed in his ears. "That ring," he said. "The filigree one." She unlocked the case with a small key on a lanyard and lifted the ring out, holding it toward him. He took it. His grip was too tight at first and he made himself loosen it. He tilted it and read the inscription in the thin scratched letters he knew would be there: *always yours.* His throat worked.

He had four immediate problems. First, he had forty dollars in his wallet. Second, he had no idea how the ring had gotten here. Third, he would need to tell his mother. Fourth, and most pressingly, the woman was looking at him with mild curiosity and he needed to appear like a man who was simply interested in a piece of jewelry, not a man whose thoughts were hurtling in seventeen directions at once. "It's lovely," he said, his voice coming out with a careful, deliberate calm that surprised him. He set the ring back on the velvet square. "I'll need to think about it." He asked for the shop's card, tucked it into his pocket, and walked out into the rain without his coffee.

The waiting room chair felt harder than it had twenty minutes ago. Claire turned the folded printout over in her hands for the fourth time, smoothing the crease down the middle as if that might change what it said. Stage two. Those two words had rearranged her entire internal landscape for three weeks—she'd rewritten her will, started calling her mother every Sunday, stopped drinking coffee because what was the point of small preventions now. The receptionist called someone else's name and Claire flinched at the sound. When Dr. Harmon finally stepped into the doorway and waved her back, Claire followed on legs that felt disconnected from her body, her breath coming in shallow increments. She sat in the patient chair and placed both hands flat on her knees to stop them from moving.

"So," Dr. Harmon said, pulling up something on her screen with a small frown. "I have to tell you something before we go any further, and I want you to hear me out completely." She turned the monitor so Claire could see it. Two names. Two dates of birth. A clerical note flagged in red at the top of the file. Claire stared at the screen and said absolutely nothing for a long moment, her mouth slightly open. The fluorescent light above them hummed. "The results we discussed last month," Dr. Harmon said carefully, "belonged to another patient." Claire looked down at her hands pressed flat against her knees. Her thumbnail had left a small white crescent in her palm.

I keep refreshing the patient portal on my phone even though I'm sitting three feet from the actual doctor. Dr. Reyes has this expression I can't read—somewhere between apologetic and careful, the kind of face people make right before they say something that requires a specific tone. I set my phone face-down on my thigh and immediately pick it back up. The office smells like antiseptic and the faint ghost of someone's lunch, and the paper on the examination table crinkles every time I shift my weight, which is every few seconds.

He starts talking and I catch about half of it. Mix-up. Intake process. Another patient with a similar name. I hear the words but I'm also watching a water stain on the ceiling tile shaped vaguely like a boot. My mouth has gone completely dry. He's saying something about the abnormal cholesterol panel, the flagged liver enzymes, none of it mine, all of it belonging to someone named Roland Marsh who was apparently sitting in a room like this one at some other clinic getting very different news. I swallow. The sound of it seems enormous in my own skull.

"So I'm—" I start. My voice comes out at the wrong volume, too quiet and then too loud when I try again. "So none of that was actually—" Dr. Reyes is nodding, patient, waiting. I set the phone down on the table beside me and it slides on the paper and I grab it before it falls, and for some reason that small fumble is what finally makes my eyes sting.

I'm staring at the notification like it's written in a language I don't fully understand. *MrKalloway_Eng11 started following you.* I set my phone face-down on my desk, then pick it up again. Still there. My English teacher. Following my Instagram. The account where I posted that video of me doing a terrible British accent for three minutes straight. The account where I argued in the comments about whether a hot dog is a sandwich. I go to his profile—public, school-official, forty-two followers, mostly parents. So he can see mine. My stomach drops through the floor. I scroll through my own feed like a stranger seeing it for the first time, and every single post suddenly looks like evidence of something. I put the phone down again. I pick it up again. My roommate asks what's wrong and I open my mouth, but what comes out is just a very long, very quiet exhale.

Dani refreshed the page twice, convinced the screen had glitched. It hadn't. There it was in plain white text against the blue banner: *DavisTeaches followed you.* She sat very still at her desk, her hands hovering over the keyboard without touching it. Mr. Davis. AP Chemistry. The man who had handed back her last lab report with the words "sloppy conclusions" circled in red pen. He had seen her TikTok where she ranked every school lunch from worst to worst. He had possibly seen the one where she reenacted a dramatic argument between two pigeons. Her throat tightened. She clicked to her own profile and read her bio as though encountering it for the first time: *"professionally tired. here for chaos."* A small sound escaped her, something caught between a laugh and a groan. She closed the laptop. She opened it again. She navigated to the school's privacy policy and read the entire thing, word by word, the cursor blinking patiently, her leg bouncing a steady rhythm against her chair the whole time.

The cardboard box sat open on my childhood bed, and I couldn't make myself reach inside it again. Mom was downstairs making tea, humming to herself like she hadn't just handed me a time capsule of every humiliating thing I'd ever written or drawn or glued together with crooked fingers. I'd already found the third-grade book report where I'd spelled "recommed" four different wrong ways on the same page. My stomach had dropped straight through the floor. Now I was staring at what looked like a folder labeled "Maya — 5th Grade Science," and my mouth had gone completely dry. She'd heard every word I'd ever said about those assignments. Every "I think I failed it, Mom." Every "It was so bad, just throw it away." I picked up the folder, put it down, picked it up again. The stairs creaked. "Milk and sugar, sweetheart?" she called up. I shoved the folder back into the box and pressed both hands flat against my thighs. "Sure," I called back, and my voice came out about half its normal size.

Daniel had come home to help his mother sort through the house before the move, and now he stood in the spare room doorway with his hand braced against the frame. Floor-to-ceiling plastic bins, each one labeled in his mother's meticulous handwriting. Daniel — Kindergarten. Daniel — Grade 2. Daniel — Grade 7. His mouth worked open and shut without producing any sound. He crossed to the nearest bin and pried the lid off with two fingers, as though something inside might startle. A clay pot, lopsided and painted the color of a bruise, with "FOR MOM LOVE DANIEL" scratched into the base. Beneath it, a worksheet with a red star sticker and a note in a teacher's handwriting: *Daniel struggles to stay focused during independent work.* He set it down carefully and rolled his shoulders back, trying to release some tension that wouldn't budge. His mother appeared in the doorway behind him. "I kept everything," she said simply. He turned to look at her, and the laugh that came out of him was too high, too bright, slightly unhinged. "I can see that," he said, his hands finding his jacket pockets and balling into fists inside them. There were so many bins.

My hands won't stop moving. I'm folding the same paper crane I've folded ten thousand times, but my fingers keep slipping, making creases where there shouldn't be any. Mia is upstairs right now, and I can hear her through the ceiling—the soft, deliberate sounds of her working. She's eight years old and she's been at it for six hours. Six hours. I set down the ruined crane and press my knuckles against my mouth. When I taught her the basics last spring, it was supposed to be something sweet, a rainy afternoon thing. She asked one question, then another, then she was watching tutorials on my phone until I had to pry it away at midnight. I climb the stairs and push open her door. Every surface—her desk, her windowsill, her bed—is covered in origami. Cranes, foxes, lotus flowers. Hundreds of them. She's sitting cross-legged on the floor with a sheet of foil paper, her tongue between her teeth, not looking up. "Mom," she says quietly, "I want to compete." My throat tightens. I was never good enough to compete. I never even tried.

Gerald stood in the doorway of the garage and stared at what his son had done to his workbench. Every jar of lure-tying materials had been opened, their contents sorted into careful little piles—elk hair, peacock herl, pheasant tail fibers—arranged with a precision that Gerald himself had never bothered with. Fourteen-year-old Theo was bent over the vise with Gerald's best magnifying lamp pulled close, threading wire onto a hook no bigger than a baby's fingernail. Gerald's jaw tightened. He'd been tying flies since his own father taught him at age nine, just a quiet thing he did on winter evenings to decompress from the world. He realized his hand was gripping the door frame. Theo hadn't asked. Theo had just—started. Three weeks ago the boy couldn't have named the parts of a hook, and now Gerald watched him whip-finish a size-22 midge with clean, confident turns of thread. "Where did you learn that finish?" Gerald heard himself ask, his voice coming out thinner than intended. Theo looked up. "YouTube. Also your notebooks." Gerald's notebooks. The ones in the locked cabinet. His mouth opened, then closed. He pulled at his collar, though it wasn't tight. "Those notebooks are personal," he finally said. Theo held up the fly between two fingers—a perfect, impossibly small thing—and Gerald felt the ground shift under him in a way he couldn't quite explain.

The coffee shop had maybe thirty people in it, and somehow every single one of them seemed to be watching the girl with the guitar in the corner. Maya stood frozen in the doorway, her iced coffee sweating through the paper sleeve in her grip. That was her chord progression. That was the bridge she'd rewritten eleven times in her childhood bedroom at two in the morning. She hadn't released it anywhere—she'd played it once at an open mic six months ago, once. Her breathing came in shallow little pulls. She found an empty stool at the counter and sat down heavily, keeping her eyes on the girl, who was maybe nineteen and performing the song with her eyes closed, completely unaware. Maya's free hand found her own collarbone and pressed there, a habit she had when her thoughts moved too fast to organize. She should go up afterward. She should say something. She rehearsed the words silently, discarded them, tried again, and by the time the last chord rang out and the room applauded, she still had nothing. She left her barely-touched drink on the counter and pushed back out into the cold.

I recognized it from the first four notes. I was standing backstage at a festival I hadn't even been scheduled to play, just there to support a friend's set, and there it was—my song, the one I wrote after my mother's surgery, the one I'd only ever put on a SoundCloud page that had maybe forty followers. The crowd was enormous from where I stood. My mouth went dry. I pressed myself against the cold metal of the equipment case behind me and watched the monitor showing the stage: some guy I didn't recognize, big voice, good-looking, working the crowd like he'd been doing it for years. He'd changed the key. He'd kept every word. My chest felt strangely compressed, like something was sitting on it. A technician brushed past me and I flinched hard enough to knock my water bottle off the case. I picked it up, set it back down, picked it up again. I had no idea what the right move was. Walk out there? Find a festival organizer? Record it on my phone? I did all three things at once and managed none of them, just stood there with my phone halfway raised, the crowd cheering, his voice carrying my words out over all those people who would never know they weren't his.

The coffee shop was Mara's idea of neutral ground, but now that she was actually here, she couldn't stop straightening the sugar packets into a neat row. She'd found the other woman's number in Jordan's jacket pocket three weeks ago—a name she didn't recognize, a note that said *dinner Thursday*. She'd told herself she just wanted answers. She'd told herself she could handle this. Her knee bounced under the table, rattling her cup in its saucer, and she pressed her hand flat against her thigh to make it stop. When the door swung open and a woman with a yellow scarf walked in, scanning the room with careful eyes, something in Mara's chest dropped straight to the floor.

The woman's name was Claire, and she'd clearly rehearsed something too, because her opening line came out stiff and slightly too fast. Mara couldn't quite look at her directly—she kept focusing just past her shoulder, on the chalkboard menu, on the word *specials* written in looping letters. She took a sip of her coffee and burned her tongue, then set it down too hard. "So," she started, and Claire said "So," at the exact same moment, and the absurdity of it almost cracked Mara open. Almost.

The silence stretched long enough that the barista glanced over. Mara watched Claire turn her phone face-down on the table, then pick it up, then set it down again. There was a photo of Jordan as Claire's lock screen—she caught it for just a fraction of a second before it went dark. The same photo Jordan used everywhere. Something cold moved through Mara's stomach. She pressed her thumbnail into her palm under the table, steadying herself, trying to arrange her face into something that didn't give everything away.

"How long?" Mara finally asked, and her voice came out smaller than she intended.

Claire's jaw tightened. "Eight months," she said quietly. She folded and refolded the edge of a napkin. "You?"

Mara's throat felt like it was full of gravel. She stared at the sugar packets she'd lined up so carefully and tried to remember how to breathe normally. Outside, a bus rumbled past the window, and the whole shop shuddered slightly, and it seemed to Mara like the most appropriate thing that had happened all day.

---

I get to the park twenty minutes early and immediately regret it, because now I have nothing to do but stand here by the fountain pretending to look at my phone. I'd found her Instagram by accident—she'd tagged herself at the same restaurant Marcus had taken me to on Valentine's Day, the one he'd called *our spot*. In the photo she was holding a single red tulip and smiling at the camera. I'd recognized the tablecloth. I'd recognized the exact corner booth. I'd sent her a message before I could talk myself out of it, and she'd replied within the hour, and now here we are.

A woman rounds the corner path, and I know immediately it's her—she's walking the way people walk when they're trying to look casual and absolutely failing. I put my phone in my pocket and take it back out. My mouth has gone completely dry. She's pretty in the way her photos suggested, and I notice stupidly that she's wearing the same kind of earrings Marcus always said he liked, little gold hoops, and that detail lands somewhere unpleasant.

We do the awkward half-wave and she says her name is Dani, which I already knew, and I say mine, which she already knew, and we stand there for a moment like two people who've just survived a minor collision. A kid nearby screams with laughter and we both flinch. "Should we sit?" I ask, which is the bravest thing I've said in about a week.

On the bench I realize I've been gripping my jacket zipper the whole time, pulling it halfway up and letting it fall, pulling it up again. I make myself stop. Dani is chewing the inside of her cheek and staring at the fountain with the focused intensity of someone who has made a career of studying water features. Neither of us knows how to begin, but we both know we have to. Somewhere across the park a dog barks, and pigeons scatter up in a grey cloud, and I think: *I found out on a Tuesday and today is Saturday and I still haven't cried yet and I don't know what that means.*

"Did he tell you he didn't believe in labels?" Dani asks finally. Her voice is even, but her hands are laced so tight in her lap that her knuckles have gone pale.

I let out a breath I've been holding since Tuesday. "Yeah," I say. "He did."

I keep finding reasons to stay near the buffet table. The shrimp cocktail doesn't need this much of my attention, but here I am, rearranging the toothpicks in my cocktail napkin for the fourth time. Marcus is somewhere on the other side of this reception hall—I caught a glimpse of him during the ceremony, three rows ahead, laughing at something the person beside him said. That laugh. I had almost forgotten what it did to my stomach. I drained my champagne glass before the toast was even finished and immediately regretted it. Now my mouth is dry and my replacement glass keeps getting warm in my hand because I'm holding it too tightly. Diane, the bride, catches my eye from across the room and gives me a knowing look. She'd warned me he'd be here. I'd told her it was fine. I mouth the word *fine* at her now, and she has the decency not to laugh. When I finally turn around, Marcus is standing six feet away, and everything I'd planned to say—casual, easy, whatever—dissolves completely. "Hey," I manage. It comes out like a question.

Sophie had changed her outfit three times that morning, which was not something she normally did, and she knew exactly why. She stood now at the edge of the garden ceremony, her clutch bag held in front of her with both hands like a small shield. The venue was beautiful—jasmine climbing white trellises, string lights threaded through the oak trees—and she was cataloguing every detail of it with great focus because cataloguing details meant she was not scanning the crowd for Daniel. A child nearby dropped a program and she flinched at the sound. The woman beside her gave her a sideways look. Sophie smiled back, a tight, brief thing. When the string quartet shifted into the processional, everyone rose, and in the reshuffling of bodies she spotted him—two rows over, already looking at her. Her fingers went white around the clutch. She faced forward. The bride was walking down the aisle, radiant and unhurried, and Sophie fixed her eyes on her with an almost desperate intensity, willing herself to look like a person who was simply watching a wedding and feeling nothing complicated at all.

Coach Marlene Diaz had been sitting in her car for eleven minutes. The engine was off. The parking lot of Westfield Recreation Center was mostly empty at this hour, just a few lights humming over the asphalt, and she could see the silhouette of Danny Reyes through the gym's side window, shooting free throws by himself. He always came in early. That was the thing—he always came in early, stayed late, ran every drill twice. She had the roster printout folded in her pocket and she'd folded and unfolded it so many times the paper had gone soft at the creases. Her coffee sat in the cupholder, stone cold now. She'd forgotten to drink it. When Danny spotted her walking across the lot and raised a hand in a wave, her stomach dropped somewhere down near her knees and she had to concentrate very carefully on putting one foot in front of the other.

I've read the same line on the whiteboard four times. "TRYOUT RESULTS POSTED FRIDAY." Friday is today. I'm the one who wrote it up there three weeks ago, and now I'm standing at the front of my own locker room holding a clipboard like it's the only thing keeping me upright. Marcus should be here any minute. He's fifteen, he's given up two summers for this program, and I have to look him in the eye and tell him the roster doesn't have room for him this season. I've coached for nine years. I know how to do this. I keep telling myself I know how to do this, and then I hear cleats on the hallway floor getting closer and I realize my jaw is clenched so tight my back teeth ache. I set the clipboard down. Pick it back up. The footsteps stop just outside the door.

I find the drawings stuffed between couch cushions while I'm babysitting. Seven of them, all crayon, all featuring the same tall figure with the lopsided hat and the coat made of leaves. My hands go cold. I sit down on the floor without meaning to. That coat—the way the leaves overlap like fish scales, always slightly different shades of brown and green—I never described that to anyone. I never *could* have described it to anyone, because I stopped believing in Corvin when I was nine years old and I've barely thought about him since. My niece Emma is four. She doesn't know what an imaginary friend is yet, let alone mine. I hold one drawing up to the lamplight and notice Corvin's left hand has the extra finger. The one I'd forgotten about until just now. My throat closes up. When Emma's small feet pad into the living room and she asks what I'm doing, my voice comes out completely wrong—too high, too careful. "Where did you meet this person, bug?" I ask her. She climbs into my lap like it's nothing, takes the drawing from my trembling hands, and says, cheerfully, "He told me you used to call him by a different name."

Diane laid the drawings out across her kitchen table one by one, smoothing each sheet of construction paper flat with a hand that wouldn't quite stay steady. Her brother had dropped off a folder of Sophie's artwork—the kid drew constantly, filled whole notebooks in a week—and Diane had started flipping through it while waiting for her coffee to brew. She'd made it three drawings in before she had to stop. The figure kept appearing in the background of every piece: a woman in a patchwork skirt, sitting in trees or standing at windows, always watching. Diane had called her Mabel. For six years of childhood, from age four to ten, she had talked to Mabel every single day. Diane pressed two fingers to her lips. The coffee maker beeped and she didn't move. There were details she hadn't told a living soul—the mismatched buttons running down Mabel's front, one shaped like a star, one a crescent moon. Sophie had drawn them exactly right, in exactly the right order. Diane pulled out her phone to call her brother, then set it back down. Picked it up again. Set it down. She walked to the window, walked back. Her coffee sat cooling, undrunk. When she finally dialed, she wasn't sure what she planned to say, and she stood listening to the rings with her free hand pressed flat against her sternum, as if she could slow something down from the outside.

My uncle set the pocket watch on the kitchen table like it was nothing, like it was just some old piece of junk he'd found in a drawer. I had to grip the back of the chair to stop myself from snatching it up. That watch belonged to my grandfather. It had sat in his vest pocket every single day of his adult life, and I could still remember the weight of it when he'd let me hold it as a kid, the way the gold case was warm from his body heat. "Worth good money," my uncle said, scrolling through his phone to show me an auction site. My mouth had gone completely dry. I swallowed twice before anything resembling words came out. "How much are they asking?" My voice sounded strange and thin, like it belonged to someone standing far away. I pulled out the chair and sat down heavily, because my legs had stopped feeling reliable. The kitchen clock ticked. I stared at the watch and tried to figure out how to say what needed to be said without my uncle deciding I was being ridiculous, without my hands giving me away by shaking.

Diane had known this conversation was coming for weeks, which somehow made standing in the doorway of her mother's bedroom worse, not better. The quilt was already folded and set apart from the others on the bed—her great-grandmother's quilt, the one with the faded blue hexagons that had taken three winters to piece together by hand. Her mother was on the phone with the antique dealer, nodding, writing figures on a notepad. Diane's stomach turned over slowly, like a wheel stuck in mud. She pressed her shoulder against the doorframe and crossed her arms, then uncrossed them, then found she had no idea what to do with her hands at all. Her mother glanced up and gave her a small, apologetic smile that did nothing to help. The figures on the notepad were circled twice. Diane looked at the quilt and thought of every Christmas morning she had woken up beneath it as a child, the particular smell of it, the way certain squares were thinner from years of being held. She opened her mouth. Closed it. Pressed two fingers hard against her lips while her mother finished the call, buying herself just a few more seconds to find the right words before she had to speak.

The page wouldn't stop shaking. Maya told herself it was the air conditioning vent above her head, but the coffee shop was perfectly still. There it was, printed clean and neat in the university's new admissions guidebook: her words, her sentences, the specific metaphor she'd used about her grandmother's kitchen smelling like cardamom and belonging. Her name wasn't attached, but that almost made it worse. Anyone who knew her would recognize it instantly. She read the opening paragraph three times, her coffee growing cold beside her untouched hand. She'd written that essay at two in the morning, crying a little, convinced it was embarrassing and overwrought. She'd almost deleted the whole thing. Her stomach had turned to liquid.
