"Just wanted to reach out because I saw you listed the house... and I thought you should know there may be some disclosure questions from buyers." May be some disclosure questions. I set the phone down on the granite countertop I'd paid three thousand dollars for and walked to the window. My jaw was clenched so tight a dull ache had started spreading up toward my ear. I'd asked Sandra directly—directly, sitting across from her at her little desk with the fake orchid—whether there was anything I needed to know about the property. She'd smiled and slid the papers toward me. I walked back to the counter and picked up the phone again. I put it back down. I opened my laptop and pulled up every email she'd ever sent me, printing them one by one until the printer ran out of paper. Somewhere in this house, in the walls I'd repainted, under the floors I'd refinished, something had happened that Sandra knew about and decided I didn't need to.

Marcus had driven past 14 Holloway Street for three weeks before he finally made an offer, certain it was the place he'd been looking for since moving back to the city. He'd checked the school district, the flood maps, the utility history. His neighbor Glen mentioned it over the fence on a Tuesday afternoon, the way neighbors do, like it was common knowledge, like surely Marcus already knew. A double homicide, 2019. The previous owners. Marcus stood very still while Glen talked, his hand wrapped around the garden hose he'd been using, the water pooling at his feet and soaking into his shoes. He didn't notice. That night he pulled every document from his filing cabinet and spread them across the dining room table. His real estate lawyer had reviewed the contract. The inspector had walked every room. The title company had sent a cheerful closing gift. Marcus lined up their business cards in a row on the table and stared at them, his nostrils flaring. He picked up his phone and began composing an email, deleted it, and started again. His shoulders were rigid, his typing hard enough that the table trembled slightly beneath the keyboard. Every single one of them had known, or should have, and not one of them had said a word.

Margaret's jaw tightened the moment she read the name on the classroom door. Hendricks. Mr. Hendricks. The same faded brass nameplate, the same crooked angle it had always hung at. She stood in the hallway of Millbrook Elementary with other parents milling around her, holding her son's school supply list so hard the paper had gone soft at the edges. She'd spent twenty-three years trying to forget a man who'd read her poem aloud to the class and then laughed. Not with her. At her. "Perhaps stick to mathematics, Miss Cole," he'd said, and everyone had joined in. She hadn't written a single poem since. Now her eight-year-old was going to sit in those same green plastic chairs and be told what he was and wasn't capable of. She folded the supply list into a hard, sharp rectangle and dropped it into her purse. Then she turned around and walked straight to the principal's office without stopping to say hello to a single parent she recognized.

I'm standing at the kitchen counter staring at the class assignment sheet my daughter brought home, and my left hand has gone completely flat against the granite, pressing down like I'm trying to push through it. Mrs. Patel. Room 14. Third grade. I remember Room 14. I remember being sent to stand in the hall so many times that I started keeping a library book in my backpack just so I'd have something to read out there. Mrs. Patel had told my mother at a parent conference that I was "resistant to structure" and "probably better suited to a different kind of environment," which my mother had somehow taken as a compliment. I was nine years old and nobody told me that "different environment" meant the remedial track, which I spent four years climbing back out of. My daughter is sitting at the table behind me, carefully coloring in a map of South America, her handwriting already neater than mine has ever been. She deserves better than someone deciding her story before she's even finished the first chapter. I pick up the phone and dial the school's main number. My voice, when someone answers, comes out very quiet and very even.

The spreadsheet printouts landed on the conference table with a slap that made the water glasses tremble. Maya had been working on the Henderson proposal for four months. Four months of 6 a.m. starts, skipped lunches, a weekend in October she'd never get back. Now Derek from the Portland office was sitting across from her with the same color-coded charts, the same three-phase rollout strategy, the same projected cost savings down to the decimal point. "Isn't this something," he said, laughing a little, leaning back like it was a funny coincidence. Maya's jaw tightened until she could feel it in her temples. She gathered her copies into a neat pile, very slowly, the way you handle something when your hands are not entirely trustworthy. She asked, in a voice stripped of everything, how long he'd been working on his version. "Since June," he said. She had started in June. The fluorescent light above her seemed to buzz louder. She excused herself, walked to the bathroom, turned the cold tap on full, and held her wrists under it for a long time. When she came back, her smile was the kind that doesn't reach anything.

I found out on a Tuesday, which somehow made it worse. Professor Alcott sent a single email to both of us—me and Jonah Reyes, cc'd together like we were being introduced at a party—noting cheerfully that our thesis proposals were "remarkably similar" and suggesting we meet to "sort it out." I read the email four times. My knee was bouncing so hard under my desk that my coffee cup was rattling in its saucer. Jonah and I had been in the same cohort for two years. Two years of study groups, shared notes, sitting three seats apart every Thursday evening in the seminar room. I'd mentioned my research direction to him once, in passing, in the parking lot after class. Just once. I printed both proposals side by side and the highlighter I was using to mark the overlapping sections ran dry before I reached page three. When Jonah texted me that afternoon—hey, funny situation huh, want to grab coffee and figure this out—my thumb hovered over the keyboard for a full minute. I put the phone face-down on the desk. Outside my window, a crow was tearing something apart on the roof of the building opposite, quick and methodical, and I watched it until my breathing slowed.

Maya found out through a department newsletter. Not a phone call, not an email, not even a Post-it note on her office door—a newsletter, formatted like a cheerful alumni update, with a small headshot of Professor Aldridge and the words "exciting new chapter." She read it three times standing in the hallway. Five years. Five years of 6 a.m. lab sessions, of canceled holidays, of restructuring her entire second chapter because Aldridge had decided in October that the theoretical framework needed "fresh scaffolding." Maya's jaw was so tight her molars ached. She folded the newsletter once, then again, then again, until it was a thick little brick she couldn't fold anymore, and then she dropped it in the trash can outside the department office. She went back to her desk, sat down, and stared at the 280-page draft on her screen—her draft, yes, but built entirely around Aldridge's vision, Aldridge's methodology, Aldridge's pet obsession with longitudinal cohort studies. Her cursor blinked. Outside, she could hear two first-year students laughing about something in the corridor. She closed her laptop with a snap that made the woman at the next desk flinch.

I keep picking up my phone to text him and then putting it back down on the desk. What would I even say? Congratulations? Thanks for the four years? I hope Stanford is worth it? The back of my neck is burning the way it does before I get a migraine, and I've been pressing my thumbnail into my palm so hard there's a white crescent there now. I heard it from another grad student—Casey, who'd heard it at a seminar reception, who'd apparently known for two weeks and hadn't said anything to me. Two weeks. Okonkwo knew for two weeks that he was leaving, and at our last meeting he sat across from me, drank his tea, told me my methodology chapter was "coming along nicely," and said nothing. I shoved my chair back from my desk and went to get water I didn't want. Standing in the kitchen, I noticed I'd left the tap running and just watched it for a minute, the water spiraling down the drain. My dissertation proposal is due in six weeks. My entire third chapter hinges on a data set he has access to through his institutional login. His institutional login that will be deactivated the moment he walks out of this building.

The email had been sitting in Marcus's inbox for six hours before he opened it. Now he wished he hadn't. He read the first line—*We regret to inform you*—and stopped. His jaw locked. He set his phone face-down on the desk with a precision that was somehow worse than throwing it. Three months. He had spent three months on that application. He'd rewritten the personal statement eleven times. He'd driven forty minutes to get a letter of recommendation from his old chemistry teacher because the committee specifically wanted someone who'd known him before high school. His roommate said something from across the room, something casual and forgettable, and Marcus stood up without responding and walked straight out of the dorm into the cold. He didn't take his coat. The air hit him like a wall and he kept walking anyway, his fists shoved deep into his pockets, his teeth pressed together so hard his temples ached. His parents had already rearranged the budget around this scholarship. His mother had told her coworkers. He stopped at the edge of the parking lot and stood very still, staring at nothing, his breath coming out in short hard clouds.

I got the rejection at 8:14 in the morning, right between my alarm going off and my first class. I know the exact time because I kept staring at the notification timestamp like it was evidence of something. I put my laptop in my bag so carefully. I zipped it up. I walked to class and sat in the back and did not hear a single word the professor said for fifty minutes. When people talk about news hitting you, they mean it—there was an actual pressure behind my eyes, a tightness across my chest like my ribs were doing something structural. The scholarship committee's feedback letter used the phrase *highly competitive applicant pool*, which I read four more times in the hallway after class. My GPA was a 3.9. I had volunteer hours. I had two internships. I called my advisor and she picked up on the second ring and said, "Oh, sweetheart," in that soft voice, and I had to move the phone away from my ear for a second because I did not want soft. I wanted to put my fist through the wall of the administrative building where the committee had sat in a room and decided my future for me. I thanked her and hung up and walked to the library and pulled every book off the nearest cart and restacked them in the wrong order, and I felt absolutely nothing about it.

The ring is sitting in a glass case between a broken trumpet and somebody's old fishing reels, tagged at forty-five dollars. I recognize it immediately—the tiny pearl set in twisted gold, the small chip on the left prong that Grandma always said happened when she was kneading bread dough in 1962. My jaw is clamped so tight I can feel it in my temples. I know exactly who did this. My uncle Dennis had been the one to "handle arrangements" after Grandma passed, swooping in with his clipboard and his rental car before any of us had even finished crying. The glass case leaves a smudge where I press my palm flat against it. I buy the ring for forty-five dollars, and the pawnbroker makes pleasant small talk that I cannot hear over the sound of blood moving through my ears. Outside, I sit in my car in the parking lot and hold the ring in my fist for a long time. Then I pick up my phone and call my mother, and when she answers I don't even say hello—I just start talking, my voice coming out clipped and flat and terribly controlled, each word placed down like something breakable.

Marcus had been inside dozens of pawn shops over the years, hunting for vintage guitar parts, never giving the jewelry cases much thought. But something made him stop this time. There it was under the fluorescent hum—a gold band with a cluster of seed pearls, a chip on one of the prongs. He'd spent every Christmas of his childhood watching his grandmother gesture with that hand, that ring catching the light while she laughed and dealt cards at the kitchen table. His nostrils flared. He stood completely still while a vein in his neck made itself visible. His grandmother had been buried eight months ago, and the ring had supposedly been buried with her. That had been his cousin Tara's insistence—a tearful declaration at the funeral home that Grandma would have wanted it that way. He thought now about Tara's new car. Her recent vacation to Portugal. The carefully composed Instagram photos from the airport. Marcus set both hands on the glass display case and leaned his weight into it, staring at the ring until the pawnbroker shifted uncomfortably behind the counter. "I need to know," Marcus said, his voice stripped of every pleasantry, "exactly who brought this in."

The papers made a sound like a whip crack when I slapped them down on the reception desk. Two years. Two years of treatments for a heart condition I apparently don't have, of beta-blockers that made me dizzy every morning, of canceling hiking trips and cutting out salt and sitting in waiting rooms reading magazines from 2019. The receptionist took a small step backward. "Sir, if you could just—" I held up one finger and she stopped talking. My jaw was so tight I could feel it in my temples. Somewhere in this building, a man named Gerald Hooper had my actual file, and I had his, and nobody had noticed until I requested copies for a second opinion. I pressed both palms flat on the desk because I needed something solid underneath me. "I want the head of this department," I said, each word deliberate and separate, like I was reading from a list, "standing in front of me in the next five minutes."

Mara sat in her car in the hospital parking garage for a long time after they told her. The engine was running. She kept meaning to turn it off. The folder of printed records sat on the passenger seat, and she found herself unable to look at it directly, the way you can't look at something too bright. Four months ago she'd been told she was a carrier for a degenerative genetic condition—four months of her mother crying over the phone, of her boyfriend going quiet in ways that changed the shape of their apartment, of Mara herself deciding quietly and privately that she would never have children. She'd grieved a future and then buried it. Now a nurse named Patricia had called, voice professionally apologetic, explaining that there had been a clerical error with a patient whose last name was also Vasquez. Mara's knuckles had gone white around the steering wheel without her noticing. She finally turned off the engine. The garage around her was dim and smelled of exhaust. She picked up her phone and set it down again. She thought about the boyfriend, who had never quite come back from those quiet months. She thought about her mother, who had started lighting candles at church. Her vision felt grainy at the edges. She picked up the folder from the passenger seat, and then she put it in the back seat, because she could not have it next to her for one more second.

I'm standing in the bathroom at school, phone in hand, heart hammering so hard I can feel it in my throat. Mr. Calloway. My English teacher. Mr. Calloway, who grades my essays and tells my parents about my "attitude problem" at conferences, has sent me a follow request on Instagram. My personal Instagram. The one where I post pictures of my friends and stupid videos of my cat and the occasional rant about how much homework we get. I lock my phone, then unlock it again, just to confirm it's real. His profile picture—that same boring cardigan he wears every Thursday—stares back at me. I shove the phone into my pocket so hard I nearly tear through the fabric. He has no right. I scrub my hands under the faucet, the water scalding hot, and I just let it burn.

Maya saw the notification during third period and had to read it four times before it sank in. Ms. Hendricks—the woman who had stood in front of the class last week and lectured them about "maintaining appropriate boundaries in the digital age"—was now requesting to follow Maya's private TikTok account. Maya's jaw tightened. She set her phone face-down on the desk with a controlled precision that left a small white mark on her palm from the corner of the case. She spent the rest of the lesson staring at the whiteboard, her pen pressed so firmly into her notebook that the words she wrote carved grooves through three pages beneath. After the bell rang, she walked directly to the school counselor's office without stopping at her locker. She didn't trust herself to see Ms. Hendricks in the hallway and say nothing.

Maya's hands moved faster now, pulling folders from the storage boxes her mother had left behind. Third-grade spelling tests. Fifth-grade book reports with gold stars still stuck to the corners. Every single one, dated, labeled in her mother's careful handwriting. She dropped a stack of them and they fanned out across the attic floor like a deck of cards. Her mother had never once said she was proud of her. Not once, not at graduation, not when Maya got the promotion, not in twenty-eight years of trying to be enough. Maya had driven four hours to clear out this house and sell it, and now she was sitting cross-legged on the dusty floorboards, jaw clenched so hard her molars ached, staring at a crayon drawing of a horse she didn't even remember making. She stuffed it back into the box. Then she took it out again. Then she pressed it flat against the floor and smoothed it with her palm until the paper tore.

I found them in the hall closet, behind the winter coats — a dozen accordion folders bound together with rubber bands that crumbled when I touched them. Every assignment I'd handed in from kindergarten through twelfth grade, arranged by year. I stood there for a long time with a seventh-grade history essay in my hands, my back against the doorframe, a pulse throbbing in my temple that I could actually hear. She had kept all of this and never breathed a word. She'd watched me spiral at twenty-four, convinced I had nothing to show for myself, nothing worth preserving. She'd watched me quit the teaching program I'd loved because I thought I wasn't smart enough. She'd said nothing. Just nodded and made tea. I set the essay down on the kitchen counter very carefully, the way you set something down when you are afraid of what you might do if you stop controlling your hands. Then I picked up my phone and called her, and when she answered on the second ring with that warm, unbothered "hello, sweetheart," the sound of it made my whole chest go tight and hot, like a room with all the windows sealed shut.

My model trains have lived in this basement for twenty-two years. Twenty-two years of careful acquisition, meticulous track planning, hand-painted miniature figures placed with tweezers. I come down here at 6 a.m. on Saturdays before anyone else wakes up, coffee in hand, and for two hours the world is exactly the size I need it to be. Or it was, until Marcus decided trains were suddenly his whole personality. I find him down here every night now, his backpack still on, his shoes leaving scuff marks on the mat I explicitly put there for shoes. Last week he ran a locomotive off an elevated curve because he wanted to "see what would happen." The locomotive is in three pieces in a plastic bag on my workbench. I've organized my tools into a new locked cabinet and said nothing. I haven't invited him down once. When my wife asked why Marcus seemed hurt that I always work alone now, I walked out of the kitchen and stood in the backyard in the November cold until I couldn't feel my fingers.

She set Priya's watercolor painting on the counter without saying a word. It was good—everyone kept telling her that, the teacher, the neighbors, her husband—and that was precisely the problem. Diane pressed her fingertips hard against the edge of the counter and looked at her daughter's painting of the harbor, the light on the water rendered in loose, confident strokes that had taken Diane herself a decade to develop. She had given Priya her old brushes two Christmases ago, meaning it as a small thing, a passing gift. Now Priya had a following on an art account Diane hadn't known existed, and last month a local gallery had reached out—to Priya. To her fourteen-year-old daughter. Diane's jaw ached. She realized she had been grinding her teeth since opening the email. She picked up her own sketchbook from beside the sink and moved it to the bedroom closet, sliding it behind a stack of sweaters, and when Priya called from the hallway asking if they could go to the art supply store together on Saturday, Diane said she was busy in a voice that came out flat and thin as paper.

The moment the opening chord progression floated through the bar speakers, Marcus went completely still. His beer bottle hovered halfway to his mouth. That was his melody. His chord structure. The descending minor third he'd spent three weeks perfecting in his bathroom at two in the morning. On the small stage at the far end of the room, a guy in a flannel shirt was performing it to a crowd of maybe forty people, grinning like he'd invented fire. Marcus set his bottle down on the table with a precise, deliberate click. The muscles in his jaw had locked up. He recognized every single note—the unusual timing change in the bridge, the way the chorus dropped into that half-step resolution nobody else wrote anymore. He pulled out his phone and started recording, his thumb pressing the button so hard it left a white mark on his skin. The flannel guy took a bow. People clapped. Someone whistled. Marcus stood up, his chair scraping back against the hardwood, and walked toward the stage with the kind of measured, unhurried steps that made the bartender look up from polishing glasses and quietly set down his rag.

I'm standing in the cereal aisle of a grocery store when I hear it piped through the overhead speakers, and for a second I genuinely think I've imagined it. I haven't. It's "Cartographer"—the song I wrote after my sister's accident, the one I recorded in my kitchen on a borrowed microphone and uploaded to SoundCloud three years ago with maybe twelve listeners total. Except the voice singing it isn't mine. It's cleaner, more polished, auto-tuned into something radio-friendly, and there's a produced beat underneath it that makes my stomach clench like a fist. I abandon my basket on the floor and find a store employee, someone teenage and bored, and ask who this song is. She shrugs and says it's that new artist, Dominic something, really blowing up right now. My fingernails are pressing crescents into my palms. I walk back to my car and sit in the parking lot with the engine off. I open my laptop and start pulling up every file I have—timestamps, drafts, email threads, anything with a date. The screen glows in the dim car. My knee won't stop bouncing. I have seventeen versions of that song saved, the earliest from October 14th, three years ago. I start composing an email to a music attorney I'd once saved the number of and never thought I'd need, and I type the entire thing without stopping once.

The woman sitting across from me in this coffee shop has his handwriting on a birthday card she's just pulled from her purse. I can see it from here—that ridiculous looping cursive he always used for capital letters. My chai goes cold in my hands. She noticed me staring at the card and we started talking, the way strangers sometimes do, and within four exchanges I learned his name from her mouth, his apartment from her description, the same Tuesday-Thursday schedule he'd given me as "work commitments." My jaw is so tight I can feel it in my temples. She's still talking, her voice getting quieter as the pieces assemble themselves for her too. I set my cup down with a deliberate, controlled motion that is the only thing I have control over right now. The table rattles. The couple beside us glances over. I press my palms flat against the wood and breathe through my nose, slow and measured, the way my therapist taught me. His contact in my phone is already pulled up. My thumb hovers. The woman across from me has stopped speaking entirely. She's looking at her own phone. We don't need to say anything else to each other. We both know what we're about to do.

Derek had been standing in the restaurant's foyer for ten minutes before he noticed the other man's keychain—a small, painted wooden fish, the exact one sold at the craft market on Delancey Street, where Marcus had a booth every Saturday. The kind Marcus made by hand. The kind Marcus had given Derek on their two-month anniversary, saying he'd never given one to anyone before. Derek's nostrils flared. He studied the other man—tall, well-dressed, scrolling through his phone with the particular restlessness of someone waiting for a date. Derek pocketed his own matching keychain and walked to the bar, ordering a whiskey he didn't want. The glass came back full and he stared into it. When the restaurant door opened behind him, he heard Marcus's voice first, warm and carrying, saying the other man's name with that same note in it that Derek had spent two months believing was reserved for him alone. Derek didn't turn around. He finished the whiskey in one motion and set it down precisely, not hard enough to make a sound, which cost him something. He flagged the bartender for another. Behind him, he could hear Marcus's step falter, then stop completely. The silence from that direction was total. Derek still didn't turn around. He wanted Marcus to stand there in it for a while.

The champagne flute nearly snapped at the stem when I saw him walk in. Marcus. In a suit I didn't recognize, standing next to a woman I'd never seen, laughing that laugh I used to think was only for me. My jaw locked so tight my back teeth ached. I turned away and fixed my gaze on the centerpiece—white peonies and eucalyptus, very Pinterest—and breathed through my nose until the floaters cleared from my vision. He'd told Jamie he wasn't sure he could make it. Jamie had told me. I had specifically asked. My heel ground into the carpet as I shifted my weight. Three years I gave that man. Three years, and he couldn't once show up to anything that mattered to me, but here he was, cufflinks catching the light, right on time for someone else's wedding. When he spotted me and raised his glass in a casual little salute, like we were old colleagues, like I was a person he vaguely remembered from somewhere, I set my champagne down on the nearest table and walked to the other end of the venue without acknowledging him. My hands stayed in fists the entire walk.

She was at the sweetheart table before the ceremony even started, arranging place cards, acting like she belonged there. Dana had told Priya she was bringing "a plus one," which was technically accurate, in the same way a house fire is technically "warm." Six months since the breakup, and already Nathan was practically glowing at her side, adjusting his tie while she smoothed his collar. The gesture made something seize up behind Priya's ribs. She knew that gesture. She had done that gesture in airports and lobbies and outside dinner parties for two and a half years. The seam of her bridesmaid glove was cutting into her finger and she realized she'd been pulling at it without noticing. She released it. She straightened her back. When the wedding planner asked if everyone in the bridal party was ready, Priya said yes in a voice that came out clipped and strange, barely her own. She smiled through the processional. She smiled through the vows. During the reception, when Nathan tried to catch her eye across the dance floor, she turned to the nearest groomsman and said, very clearly, "Tell me something interesting," and kept her face toward his for the rest of the song.

Coach Derrick Paulson folded the roster sheet in half and then in half again until it was a thick, useless square in his fist. Seventeen years. He had given seventeen years to this program, built it from a laughingstock into a regional powerhouse, and now the Athletic Director was sitting across from him telling him who to cut. Not asking. Telling. Marcus Webb—the kid Derrick had personally recruited out of a gym in Tulsa, the kid who'd driven four hours every Saturday for a year just to practice with the team before he was even enrolled—was the name on the list. Derrick pressed his knuckles flat against the desk. The Athletic Director kept talking about "optics" and "donor relationships" and the name of the donor's nephew, who had a vertical leap like a stack of pancakes and hands like oven mitts. Derrick stood up without being excused. His chair scraped back so hard it hit the filing cabinet. He walked to the window and looked out at the empty court below, jaw clamped so tight his back teeth ached. "I'll handle it," he said, his voice flat as pavement. He didn't turn around.

I've been sitting in my car outside the gym for twenty minutes and I can't make myself go inside. My knuckles are white on the steering wheel. I keep running over the conversation with Coach Tillman in my head—the part where he told me I was being "reassigned to a developmental role," which is the coward's way of saying I'm cut, stripped of my starting position, handed to the JV squad like equipment that's too worn out for the varsity court. I gave up a scholarship offer from State for this program. I turned it down because Tillman looked me in the eye and said I was exactly what his team needed. That was fourteen months ago. Now apparently what his team needs is a freshman whose father just donated a new scoreboard. My fist hits the dashboard before I even decide to do it, and the pain shoots up my forearm and I'm glad for it, glad for something solid and real. Outside, a couple of my now-former teammates are shooting around through the gym windows, laughing about something. I watch them and feel something in my chest pull tight like a wire being wound past its limit. I start the car. I don't trust myself to walk through that door tonight.

My niece Petra is six years old and has never met anyone from my childhood. She's never seen my old bedroom, never rummaged through my mother's attic, never had a conversation with me that went beyond what flavor of ice cream she wanted. So when she handed me her drawing across the dinner table tonight—a crayon figure with one eye higher than the other, a zigzag mouth, and three fingers on each hand—my fork stopped halfway to my plate. That's Cobb. That is exactly, precisely Cobb, down to the lopsided triangle he had instead of a nose. I invented Cobb when I was seven. I invented him the year my parents split the house into two cold silences and I needed something that was entirely mine. He was mine. I asked Petra where she'd seen this, keeping my voice very level, the way you do when something underneath is straining against its bolts. She just shrugged and said he visits her room at night and tells her stories. I excused myself to the bathroom and stood at the sink with the tap running, gripping the cold porcelain until my knuckles went white.

Dana had been sorting through her sister's house for two hours before she found the drawings, rubber-banded together inside a shoebox under Lily's bed. Her sister had called her that morning in a panic about Lily—the nightmares, the talking to walls, the things she described seeing. Dana had driven four hours with her jaw set, because nobody else was going to take this seriously. But as she flipped through the drawings one by one, something else entirely seized her. Page after page of the same figure: tall, thin, wearing what looked like a coat made of feathers, with two dots for eyes arranged at slightly different heights. Dana's breath left her in a sharp exhale that had nothing to do with surprise. She pressed the drawings flat against the floor and stared. Mira. The name surfaced from somewhere she'd sealed shut decades ago. Her Mira, who had lived in the willow tree in the backyard of her parents' first house. The house Lily had never set foot in. The house that had been demolished in 1994. Dana rolled the rubber band back into place around the drawings with slow, precise movements, then sat back on her heels. Her sister was already calling her name from downstairs. Dana didn't answer. She was staring at the wall, very still, a specific kind of stillness that had teeth in it.

My uncle called it "just a clock," and those three words have been rattling around my skull for the past hour like a marble in a tin can. Just a clock. I'm standing in the hallway staring at it right now—the mahogany case my great-grandfather carved by hand on a ship crossing the Atlantic, the brass pendulum my grandmother polished every Sunday morning without fail, the tiny crack along the left panel from the earthquake of 1971 that she always said gave it "character." My uncle wants four thousand dollars for it from some antique dealer in Phoenix who wouldn't know our family's name if it was engraved on his forehead. I press my palms flat against the wall and feel the plaster cool against my skin. When my uncle called, I'd said very little. I couldn't. My jaw had locked up somewhere around the word "appraisal." I'd set the phone face-down on the counter and left it there.

The clock ticks on, indifferent to all of it, marking seconds with the same patience it has kept for a hundred years. I pull out a chair from the dining table and sit directly in front of it, like I'm standing guard. My leg won't stop bouncing. When my mother was dying in the hospital, she'd asked me specifically—eyes clear, voice steady—to make sure the clock stayed in the family. I'd held her hand and promised. My uncle was in that room too. He was standing right there. I pick up my phone and open a new text to him. I type four full sentences, read them back, then delete every word. I set the phone face-down again. My thumbnail is bleeding where I've been pressing it into my palm without noticing.

Diane had not touched her dinner. The fork sat beside her plate at a precise angle, positioned there twenty minutes ago when her brother had made his announcement over the pot roast, casual as a weather report—he'd found a buyer for their grandmother's ring. A collector. Good money. The ring that had survived the Depression in a flour tin, that had been smuggled out of Hungary in a coat lining, that their grandmother had worn every single day until the morning she died and pressed into Diane's hand at the end. Diane's chair scraped back from the table and she carried her plate to the kitchen without a word.

She stood at the sink running water over the untouched food, watching gravy dissolve and swirl down the drain. Behind her, she could hear her brother still talking—explaining the figure, the collector's credentials, the practicality of it—his voice carrying easily through the small house the way it always had. Her grip on the plate tightened until her knuckles went pale. She set it down carefully, deliberately, with a control that cost her something. The window above the sink showed her own reflection back in the dark glass: jaw set, shoulders drawn up toward her ears. She didn't recognize herself for a moment. She turned off the faucet and dried her hands on the dish towel, folding it into a precise square before setting it on the counter. Then she walked back to the dining room doorway and stood there until her brother stopped talking mid-sentence and finally looked at her.

Maya refreshed the page three times, convinced her eyes were playing tricks on her. They weren't. There it was — her essay, the one she'd spent four weeks writing and rewriting at her kitchen table junior year, the one where she'd described her grandmother's death in careful, aching detail — sitting on a college prep website under the header "Model Essays: Personal Statements That Work." No byline. No permission request in her inbox. No compensation. Just her grief, formatted in Arial 12-point, available for any stranger to click on and read. She slammed her laptop shut hard enough that her roommate looked up from across the room. Her jaw ached from clenching it. She opened the laptop again and began screenshotting everything, her movements clipped and mechanical, each click of the trackpad more deliberate than the last.

The guidance counselor who'd collected those essays — Mrs. Pellegrino, with her motivational posters and her candy dish — had specifically said the essays were for "internal use." Maya remembered the exact phrase because she'd almost asked what it meant before deciding it didn't matter. Now her fingers were moving fast across the keyboard, drafting an email and then deleting it, drafting it again. The draft folder filled up with unsent versions. Her roommate quietly left the room. Maya stood up, walked to the window, and stood there staring at the parking lot below until her breathing slowed enough that she could start over with something she might actually send.

I was helping my younger cousin prep for his applications when I found it. He'd pulled up some website — PrepElite or ElitePrep, one of those — and was scrolling through sample essays for inspiration. The one at the top of the page was about learning to cook after a parent's cancer diagnosis. My stomach dropped before I even fully processed why. I read the opening sentence again. Then the second paragraph. Then I just sat there, very still, while my cousin kept talking about something I couldn't hear anymore. That essay was mine. I had written it at seventeen years old, sitting on my bathroom floor at two in the morning because I couldn't sleep and I didn't know how else to say what I needed to say.

I excused myself and walked to the kitchen, where I poured a glass of water and then just held it. The glass was cold. I noticed that I was pressing my thumbnail into my palm hard enough to leave a mark. The website had no contact information listed, just a submission form that led nowhere. I filled it out anyway, selecting "content removal request" from a dropdown menu that felt insultingly casual for the situation. I submitted it. Nothing happened. The little checkmark confirmation sat there on screen looking cheerful, and I walked back to the kitchen and poured the water down the drain for no reason I could explain, just needing something to do with my hands.
afraid: The LinkedIn notification glowed on Maya's phone screen. "People you may know: Derek Holloway, Licensed Clinical Therapist." The same Derek Holloway who had spent three years in middle school narrating her every awkward moment to anyone who would listen. Who had given her the nickname that followed her through high school. Maya set the phone face-down on the counter. Picked it up again. Put it away in a drawer. She stood in her kitchen for a long moment, gripping the edge of the sink until her knuckles went white. She'd spent the last year on a waitlist for mental health services. The referral letter from her GP sat on the refrigerator, held up by a magnet from a vacation she couldn't remember enjoying. When she finally retrieved her phone and opened his profile again, his credentials were impeccable. Dozens of glowing testimonials. Specialization in adolescent trauma. She read that last part twice, a strange ringing starting in her ears. Her thumb hovered over the "Connect" button until her screen went dark from inactivity.

I found out through my cousin, the way you find out most things that rearrange something fundamental inside you. "Oh, you probably know him," she said cheerfully, scrolling through her new therapist's website. "Derek Holloway? Grew up in your neighborhood?" I watched my own hand set down my wine glass with extraordinary care, the way you handle something you suspect might shatter. I asked her to show me the photo. There he was, soft lighting and a cardigan, a professional smile. The boy who used to wait by my locker. My cousin was describing how insightful he was, how safe he made her feel, and I was nodding along while a low hum took up residence behind my sternum. I excused myself to use her bathroom and sat on the edge of her tub for six minutes, counting the black and white tiles. The thing is, I need a therapist. I've needed one for years. I finally have insurance that covers it. I pressed my palms flat against my thighs and stared at my own reflection in her mirror, barely recognizing the expression I found there.

The office door had a new nameplate. I stood in the hallway reading it three times, the fluorescent lights humming above me like something was about to short-circuit. Dr. Harmon's name was gone. I'd been in that office every week for six years. I pressed my back against the wall and pulled out my phone, scrolling to her contact—the reassuring weight of all those saved messages, the voice memos she'd sent me before big presentations. My thumb hovered over her number but wouldn't press it. What if she'd left because of me? What if the last project had been the thing that finally wore her out?

The receptionist at the front desk confirmed it with a smile that had no idea what it was doing. "Oh, Dr. Harmon retired last month. Quite sudden, actually." She said it the way you'd mention a change in the cafeteria menu. I nodded and walked to the elevator and stood there after the doors opened, not getting in. The department had thrown a party, apparently. There were photos on the intranet. In all of them, Dr. Harmon was laughing, surrounded by colleagues, and I was not there. I had not been invited. Or I had been and something had gone wrong with my email. I checked my email in the elevator with both hands gripping my phone until my knuckles ached.

I sat at my desk and opened a document I was supposed to be editing and stared at the same sentence for forty minutes. My leg wouldn't stop moving under the desk. Without Dr. Harmon, there was no one who knew the full scope of what I was working on, no one who understood why I'd chosen this particular angle, no one who would catch me if the whole thing collapsed. I'd built my entire methodology on her feedback. I thought about the manuscript on my screen and felt something in my stomach drop, the way it does when you think you've missed a step in the dark.

I called my sister that evening and talked around the subject for twenty minutes before she asked me directly what was wrong. I opened my mouth to explain and what came out instead was, "Do you think people can tell when they've been a burden to someone and just never say anything?" She went quiet. Outside my window, the street looked the same as it always did, and somehow that made it worse.

Marcus checked the faculty directory again on his phone, standing outside in the parking lot even though it was January and his coat was still at his desk. Professor Bellamy's profile had been replaced with a small gray silhouette and the words *Position Vacant*. He'd been trying to reach her since Tuesday. He refreshed the page. The gray silhouette looked back at him. His breath came out in visible clouds that dispersed before he could track them.

Inside, his graduate cohort was eating lunch together, laughing about something. Marcus took the long way around to avoid them, cutting through the stairwell and taking the back entrance to his carrel. He sat down and lined up his pens on the desk in a row, then moved them back. Professor Bellamy had been the one who'd pushed his application through when the committee had doubts. She'd been the one who'd read his first chapter draft at midnight and called him the next morning before he'd even woken up. He opened his laptop and navigated to his dissertation folder, a document called *Ch1_BELLAMYedits_FINAL.docx*, and then closed the laptop again without opening it.

He tried to reconstruct the last time he'd seen her. November. She'd been distracted, he thought now, re-examining the memory like a photograph he'd developed wrong. She'd asked twice how he was doing. Not once—twice. He hadn't thought anything of it then. His jaw tightened. He pressed his tongue against the back of his teeth and stared at the middle distance of his carrel wall, covered in index cards, all of them pointing back to her guidance, her frameworks, her marginalia in his printed drafts.

When his cohort-mate Delia knocked on the carrel door an hour later to ask if he was coming to the department talk, Marcus turned around and found he couldn't quite arrange his face correctly. "Who's taking over her advisees?" he asked, instead of answering her. Delia tilted her head. "They haven't said yet." Marcus turned back to his wall of index cards. "Okay," he said, in a voice that came out quieter and flatter than he'd intended, and did not move from his chair.

The book had my name on the cover. My full name—middle name included—the one I never used, the one that didn't appear on my driver's license or my social media or anywhere I'd ever written it down publicly. I set it back on the bookstore display table and picked it up again, as if the second time might produce different words. *A Life Examined: The Story of Marcus Owen Delacroix.* My palms were slick against the glossy cover. I stood there long enough that a clerk asked if I needed help, and I heard myself say "no thank you" in a voice that belonged to someone else entirely. I bought it with cash. I don't normally carry cash.

I sat in my car in the parking garage for forty minutes before opening it. The author's name meant nothing to me: G. R. Voss. The dedication page said, simply, "Because someone had to." Chapter three described, in accurate detail, the summer I spent at my uncle's cabin when I was eleven—the summer I had never told anyone about, not a therapist, not a lover, not a single living person. I put the book face-down on the passenger seat and checked my rearview mirror. Then I checked it again.

I've been sleeping with the lights on for six days now. This started when my colleague Priya dropped the package on my desk and said, brightly, "Someone sent you a book!" like it was a pleasant thing. The return address was a P.O. box in a city I've never visited. Inside, no note, no card—just a paperback with a green cover and my face on it. A photo I recognized: me, outside the apartment I'd lived in four years ago, taken from a distance, taken without my knowing.

I called my sister before I'd even read past the introduction. "Is this a joke you and Mom set up?" I asked, and she could tell immediately from whatever was happening in my voice that it wasn't a question, it was a plea. She said no. I believed her because I know what her lying sounds like, and this wasn't it. I've since called everyone I can think of. Nobody knows anything. The book is sitting on my kitchen counter right now. I've read sixty pages. Every detail is correct. Every address, every job, the name of my dog who died in 2019, the results of a medical test I took alone in a clinic and told no one about. I've deadbolted my front door three times tonight already, each time getting up from the couch to check it as though the lock might have changed its mind.

Maya found the laptop open on the kitchen table while Marcus was in the shower. She hadn't meant to look—she'd only wanted to check the weather—but the document title stopped her cold: *The Woman Who Didn't Know*. She told herself it was probably nothing. She told herself this three times. Her hand moved the cursor anyway. The protagonist's name was Lena. Lena had Maya's laugh, her habit of folding napkins into triangles, her childhood story about the dog that had bitten her at seven years old. Maya stepped back from the table as though it had burned her. She pressed both palms flat against her thighs.

She was standing exactly where she'd been standing when Marcus came in, toweling his hair, and she watched his expression shift from easy warmth to something careful and unreadable. "You're home early," he said. Maya's mouth went very dry. She looked at the laptop, then at him, then at the window, because the window was the only thing in the room that wasn't loaded. "What is that document?" she asked. Her voice came out smaller than she intended, stripped of the edges she'd meant to give it. He set the towel down slowly, and the slowness of it made her legs feel hollow.

"It's a novel," he said, and that single sentence opened up a distance between them she could almost see. She thought about Lena knowing Marcus's middle name, Lena knowing the exact way Maya gripped her coffee mug with both hands. She thought about how long the document had been open on that table, whether he'd left it deliberately, whether this whole kitchen had been arranged like a set. She pulled her cardigan tighter around herself even though the room was warm. "How much of it is me?" she asked. The shower was still running somewhere in the back of the apartment, filling the silence like white noise.

I scroll and scroll, and the pages keep going. Three hundred and forty-two of them. Jonah has been working on this for what looks like two years, based on the file's creation date, which means he started it eight months before we even met. No—wait. I do the math again. Four months after. He started it four months after our first date, when I thought we were still learning each other's coffee orders. The character's name is Claire, and Claire has the scar on her left knee that I got falling off a bicycle at age nine, and I never told Jonah that story. I have no memory of ever telling him that story.

My hands are doing something strange—a fine, continuous trembling that I can't stop by pressing them together. I sit on the edge of our bed. I stand up again. I go to the window and look at the street below where nothing is happening, where a man is walking his dog, where the world is completely ordinary, and I cannot connect that ordinary world to the document still glowing on Jonah's desk. I read Claire's dialogue again. It sounds like me. It sounds more like me than I sound like me. I notice I've been holding my breath for some interval I can't measure, and I let it out very carefully, like I'm defusing something.

I hear his key in the door before I've decided what to do with myself. I'm still standing by the window when he comes in, and I watch his eyes find me, and then find the desk, and then find me again. "Hey," he says. Just that. Just the one word, carrying everything. I realize I've pressed myself back against the wall without noticing, my shoulder blades flat against the plaster, the way you'd press yourself into a doorway during a storm. "Who's Claire?" I ask. My voice is completely level and that levelness is the most dishonest thing I've ever produced.

The pull-down attic stairs groaned under Marcus's weight as he climbed, flashlight sweeping across the dark. He'd bought the house three weeks ago and kept finding reasons not to come up here. Tonight, a water stain on the ceiling forced his hand. The beam caught something immediately — cardboard boxes, at least a dozen, arranged in a loose circle around a child's wooden rocking chair that faced the far wall. His foot hovered over the next step. He made himself take it anyway.

He opened the nearest box with two fingers, like it might bite. Baby clothes, neatly folded. He opened another. Journals, rubber-banded in sets of three. He didn't touch those. His breathing had gone shallow without him noticing, and when a branch scraped the roof from outside, he spun so fast he nearly lost his footing on the stairs.

The rocking chair was what he couldn't stop looking at. It sat precisely equidistant from each wall, which wasn't something that happened by accident. Someone had measured. Positioned. Left it there pointing at something — or for something. Marcus took one photograph with his phone and descended with his back pressed against the folding ladder, watching the dark square of the attic opening until it closed flush with the ceiling.

He sat in his kitchen with every light on, phone on the table, not calling anyone in particular but needing it close. The water stain could wait. It could wait a very long time.

I found the first thing by accident — a wooden jewelry box wedged behind the insulation near the far eave. I almost left it there. Instead I crouched in the hot, close air of the attic and pried the lid open with my thumbnail. Inside was a photograph of a woman standing in what was unmistakably my kitchen, before the renovation, and she was looking directly at the camera with an expression I couldn't quite read. On the back, in pencil: *she knows now.*

My mouth went dry. I set the box down carefully, the way you set something down when you don't want to make noise.

There were other boxes further back, half-hidden under old insulation batting that had been peeled up and laid back down — peeled and replaced, I realized, more than once. My hands were doing something embarrassing, a faint trembling I kept noticing when the flashlight beam shuddered against the rafters. I told myself it was the heat up here, the poor air circulation. I kept telling myself things.

The last box I opened had a bundle of envelopes addressed to a name I didn't recognize at my address, my actual address, stamped *Return to Sender* and bound with a red ribbon. Beneath them, a child's drawing of a house — this house, the distinctive arched window above the door — and inside it, in crayon, a small figure standing at the top of a staircase, looking up.

I left everything exactly as I found it. I've been sleeping with the bedroom door locked since Tuesday, which I know is irrational, because whatever this is, it's old, it's over, it has nothing to do with me. I know that. I just keep locking the door anyway.

The notification glowed on Priya's laptop screen: "Rahul has completed Level 12: Intermediate Tamil!" She stood very still in the doorway of the home office, her hand frozen on the light switch. Level twelve. She ran the math involuntarily — that was months of lessons, maybe six, maybe more. Six months of secrets. She became aware of her own heartbeat in a way that felt wrong, too loud, like it was happening outside her body. She thought about every phone call with her sister, every conversation with her mother, every whispered confession spoken in Tamil precisely because Rahul was in the next room. Her mouth went dry. She clicked the laptop shut and walked to the kitchen and poured herself a glass of water she didn't drink. When she heard his key in the door, she gripped the counter's edge until her knuckles ached.

I found the notebook by accident, wedged between his side of the mattress and the wall. Portuguese vocabulary lists, dozens of pages, filled front and back in Marco's careful handwriting. My hands started doing something strange — trembling, but very slightly, the way a bridge vibrates rather than shakes. I sat on the edge of the bed and turned the pages one by one. He'd organized them by topic. Family. Home. Feelings. Arguments. There were full sentences at the back, with little pronunciation notes in parentheses. I thought about my grandmother's last visit, the long calls I take in the bathroom, the things I tell my brother when I need to say something I believe only the two of us will ever carry. I put the notebook back exactly as I found it and went to stand by the window. The street below looked completely ordinary. When Marco came out of the shower humming, I smiled at him, and the smile fit over my face like something that belonged to a stranger.

The notification appeared at 11:47 PM, which was when Maya did most of her scrolling. She saw the name and her thumb stopped moving entirely. Derek Howell. The profile picture showed a man in a golf shirt smiling at a barbecue, but she recognized the jaw, the set of the eyes. She put the phone face-down on her nightstand and lay very still in the dark, listening to sounds she didn't usually notice—the settling of the building, footsteps somewhere above her. She picked the phone back up and checked that her location settings were turned off. Then she checked again.

She hadn't thought about him in years, or told herself she hadn't. But her body seemed to have kept its own records. She got up and made sure the deadbolt was thrown, even though she was on the fourteenth floor of a building with a doorman. Standing in her kitchen with the lights on, she read his profile with the careful attention of someone studying a document for hidden clauses. Forty-three mutual friends. She didn't understand how that was possible. Her mouth had gone completely dry.

She didn't accept. She didn't decline either—declining felt like it might do something, alert him somehow. She sat at her kitchen table until 2 AM with a glass of water she never drank, turning her phone over and over in her hands like she was trying to read something written on the back of it. At some point she moved to the couch and pulled a blanket around her shoulders even though the apartment was warm. The request just sat there, pending, a small grey button she couldn't bring herself to touch.

I recognized the name before I recognized that I recognized it. There was just this half-second where my whole system seemed to lag, like a computer accessing something stored very deep. Brandon Kerr. Then it hit me, and I set my laptop on the coffee table with both hands, very slowly, like it was something that could break or bite.

He'd moved away in eighth grade. I had spent part of that summer just standing in my own backyard in the sun, doing nothing, feeling something loosen in my chest that I hadn't even fully known was clenched. That was twenty-two years ago. I'm a person now who owns furniture and files taxes and has a job title with three words in it. I repeated these facts to myself while staring at his profile photo, which showed him at what looked like a wedding, laughing with his head thrown back. A normal human man at a wedding.

My roommate came home and asked if I wanted takeout. I said sure and named a restaurant, and she went into her room to order, and I sat there with my arms wrapped around myself, rocking very slightly without meaning to. I found myself calculating. His profile listed a city four hours away. I looked at it three times to make sure I was reading it right. Four hours. I ran the numbers on whether that was far enough and then hated myself for running the numbers.

I still haven't touched the request. I've thought about deactivating my whole account, making up some reason about digital wellness. My roommate slid a container of noodles across the table to me and I smiled and said thanks, and my hands were steadier than I expected when I picked up the chopsticks, which felt like a small, private victory I couldn't explain to anyone.

I found the LinkedIn profile by accident, three clicks deep into a rabbit hole about industry certifications. Marcus Chen, the page read, Sales Associate at a grocery chain—not Senior Software Engineer at a tech startup, not the job he'd described to me in elaborate detail for two years. I set my phone face-down on the table. I picked it up again. I put it down again. My palms had gone slick and cold, the way they used to before swim meets in high school. I thought about every dinner where he'd complained about his "toxic coworkers in engineering," every time I'd wired him money for "startup equity paperwork fees," every holiday where he'd shown me fake screenshots of his salary deposits. The apartment felt smaller suddenly. I got up and checked that both deadbolts were locked, then stood in the kitchen not remembering why I'd walked there. The number of times he had a spare key to this place. My throat felt like I'd swallowed gravel.

I texted him something casual—*hey, want to grab lunch this week?*—just to see what he'd say. His response came within seconds, cheerful, normal, completely ordinary. I read it four times. I'd known Marcus since we were eleven years old. I stared at his contact photo, a grinning face I'd trusted across three decades and two cities, and realized I had absolutely no idea what else he might have made up. I moved my spare key from the bowl by the door into my sock drawer, then thought better of it, and moved it again to the locked box in my closet. I didn't sleep that night. I lay on top of the covers listening to every sound the building made, cataloguing them, measuring them, deciding what each one was.

The pay stub fell out of Priya's jacket when Renata was hanging it up—an ordinary accident, the kind that happens every day. Renata bent to pick it up, saw the employer name printed at the top, and went absolutely still. For a year and a half, Priya had described herself as a forensic accountant for the city's financial crimes unit, someone who worked alongside detectives, who helped build fraud cases, who had access to sensitive investigations. The pay stub said she was a data entry clerk for a parking violations office. Renata carefully folded the paper back the way she'd found it and tucked it into the jacket pocket. She stood at the coat rack for a long moment. Her pulse had risen somewhere into her ears.

She moved to the living room window and looked down at the street. Priya was still downstairs getting something from her car. Renata watched her without waving. She thought about the stories—the wiretaps, the stakeouts, the cartel case Priya said she couldn't talk about, the evenings she'd shown up mysteriously late with an explanation that always somehow discouraged further questions. Renata's mind began rearranging memories like furniture, seeing the room completely differently. She didn't know what the lie meant. She didn't know if it was lonely invention or something more deliberate. She had no way of knowing which, and that was the problem. When she heard Priya's footsteps in the hallway, Renata sat down on the couch and arranged her face carefully, pressing her knees together to stop them from bouncing. She kept her hands in her lap where she could feel them. "Got it," Priya said brightly, holding up a bottle of wine, and Renata smiled back, slow and measured, buying herself time to figure out what kind of person she was actually looking at.

The email had been sitting in Maya's inbox for six hours before she opened it. "Academic Integrity Violation – Required Meeting." She read it three times, then closed her laptop and opened it again, as if the words might rearrange themselves into something benign. They didn't. She spent the next hour reorganizing her desk – stacking books by height, then by color, then by height again. Her roommate asked if she wanted dinner and Maya said yes, then sat in front of a full plate and moved the rice around with her fork until it was cold. She washed the same mug twice that night without realizing it.

I had written that paper myself. Every word of it. But try explaining that to Professor Hendricks, who sat across from me now with a printed copy and a red pen and a face like stone. The office was small and warm, but I couldn't stop the goosebumps racing up my forearms. I kept my hands flat on my thighs under the desk so he wouldn't see them trembling. "The phrasing here," he said, tapping a paragraph I remembered writing at two in the morning, "is remarkably similar to a 2019 journal article." My mouth had gone completely dry. When I tried to speak, what came out was barely above a whisper, and I had to stop and swallow and start over, certain that the pause itself made me look guilty.

The package had no return address. Margaret turned it over three times before setting it on her kitchen table, where it sat for the better part of an hour while she stood in the doorway pretending to check her phone. The handwriting on the label was looping and deliberate, the kind a teenager might use when they were trying to look grown-up. She recognized it immediately. Darian. He'd been in her tenth-grade English class seven years ago, the quiet boy who used to wait by her car after school, who'd left notes under her classroom door long after she'd asked him to stop. Her palms had gone damp the moment she'd seen it on her doorstep. She made herself a cup of tea she didn't drink. She called her sister and hung up before it connected. When she finally sat down at the table, she pulled the package close with two fingers, like something that might move on its own.

Inside was a first edition of *Their Eyes Were Watching God*, wrapped in tissue paper. A note card tucked inside the cover read: *You always said this one changed you. Hope you're well. — D.* Margaret read the note four times. She got up and checked that her front door was locked, then the back door, then the windows facing the street. She sat back down. The book was beautiful. She knew it was beautiful. Her leg wouldn't stop bouncing under the table, and every time a car slowed on the road outside, her head snapped toward the window.

I found it propped against my classroom door on a Tuesday morning, a small wrapped thing with a gift tag that just said *Ms. Okafor* in handwriting I hadn't seen in eleven years. I knew whose it was before I even unwrapped it. Some part of me knew the way you know a sound in the dark. I stood in the empty hallway holding it for longer than I can account for. My first period students would arrive in twenty minutes. A janitor rolled his cart past and said good morning and I think I nodded.

It was a snow globe. Inside, a tiny figure sat at a tiny desk, surrounded by tiny bookshelves. On the base, someone had painted the words *The best classroom I ever had.* I set it on my desk and sat in my chair and stared at it. Marcus had been fourteen when I'd had to report the bruising on his arms to the school counselor. He'd looked at me like I'd pulled the floor out from under him. I'd spent a year wondering if I'd done the right thing, two years wondering what had happened to him, and then I'd made myself stop. My mouth had gone dry. I kept glancing at the hallway outside my open door as though expecting someone to be standing there. I turned the snow globe upside down and watched the white flakes settle, and when my students began filing in I smiled at each of them and my hands, clasped on the desk in front of me, did not move.

Marcus had been staring at the same paragraph in his performance review for eleven minutes. He knew this because he'd been watching the clock on his computer screen, then looking away, then looking back. His hands had left damp prints on the paper. Down the hall, he could hear David laughing with someone in the break room — that easy, unguarded laugh that Marcus had always admired and now found unbearable to listen to. He got up and quietly closed his office door. Then he opened it again, worried about how that might look. He sat back down and began composing an email to his manager, deleted it, and composed it again. His throat felt like it had been packed with sand. He'd been at this company for six years. David had been here for three. That had to count for something. It had to.

The email from HR said the decision would be announced on Friday. It was Monday. I've been sleeping in ninety-minute chunks, waking at 3 a.m. to stare at the ceiling of my bedroom, running through the same loop of thoughts until my pulse thumps in my ears. I've started taking the long route to the kitchen at work just to avoid passing Janet's desk, because when I do walk past her, my voice comes out wrong — too bright, too careful, like I'm carrying something fragile. She's been here longer. She knows the VP by name. Yesterday she touched my arm in the hallway and said, "May the best person win," and I smiled with every muscle in my face while my stomach dropped through the floor. I've been eating lunch alone in my car. I told myself it's because the weather's nice.

The coach's words were still ringing in Marcus's ears as he laced up his cleats in the locker room. Striker to goalkeeper. They wanted to put him in goal. His hands moved mechanically through the familiar loops and knots, but he had to start over twice because the laces kept coming undone. He'd spent eleven years training as a forward — eleven years of muscle memory, of instinct, of knowing exactly where his body was supposed to be. Now they wanted him to face the ball coming *at* him instead of chasing it down. He sat motionless on the bench long after everyone else had filed out to the pitch, staring at the goalkeeper gloves they'd left beside him on the bench, thick and foreign, like something belonging to another species.

The assistant coach poked her head around the corner. "Marcus? We're starting warm-ups." He stood too fast and had to grip the locker beside him. "Yeah," he said, his voice coming out strange and thin. "Yeah, I'm coming." Outside, the stadium was still mostly empty, but the few thousand early arrivals already filling the seats felt like a wall of eyes. He pulled the gloves on during the jog out, working his fingers into the padding one at a time, and found himself studying the goal mouth as if he'd never actually looked at it before. He had scored in front of goals his entire career. He had never once stood inside one.

Coach Reyes called me into her office on a Tuesday, which was already a bad sign. She had this way of smoothing papers on her desk when she was about to say something she wasn't sure you'd want to hear. I watched her hands and felt my stomach drop before she even opened her mouth. She told me they needed me at center, that my natural reading of the game was wasted at shooting guard, that this could unlock something real for me. I nodded. I kept nodding. I think I said "okay" four or five times while she talked.

That night I couldn't eat dinner. I moved food around my plate until my roommate asked if something was wrong, and I told her it wasn't, which is what I always say. I lay in bed running through the responsibilities of a center — the screens, the post defense, the box-outs — and my mind kept skipping like a scratched disc. I've been 5'9" my whole life. Centers are not 5'9". I pulled the blanket up over my face even though the room wasn't cold, lying completely still, replaying every center I'd ever watched get backed down in the post.

At the first practice in my new position, I set a screen and immediately apologized to the person I'd hit, even though setting the screen was the entire point. My teammate gave me a look. I laughed, but the sound came out wrong — too quick, too sharp. I kept drifting toward the perimeter out of habit, and each time Coach Reyes blew the whistle and pointed back inside, I walked back to the block slowly, bouncing on the balls of my feet, my jaw tight.

The return address was a city I hadn't lived near in eleven years. Maya turned the envelope over twice, then set it on the kitchen counter and walked to the other side of the room. She filled a glass of water and drank it standing at the sink, watching the envelope like it might move. Her upstairs neighbor's footsteps crossed the ceiling and she flinched at each one. She opened the envelope standing up, not sitting, because sitting felt too committed. *Dear Maya, I've wanted to write this for a long time.* She recognized the handwriting before she recognized the name at the bottom, and the glass slipped in her wet hand, knocking against the faucet with a hollow clang. She read it standing at the door to her apartment, one hand resting on the deadbolt without turning it, the other holding the single page at arm's length like it was something hot.

The letter was an apology. She read it three times. It explained regret, it explained therapy, it explained growth. The words were careful and measured and did not ask anything of her. She folded it back into thirds and put it in the drawer under the takeout menus. Then she took it out again and put it behind a box in her closet. She checked that her phone was charged. She texted her sister, just a casual message, just to hear back from someone, and when the three dots appeared she let out a long, unsteady breath that she hadn't known she was holding.

I read it twice before I understood who it was from. The name at the bottom, *Thomas Greer*, hadn't crossed my mind in eight years — or so I'd told myself, though my therapist would have laughed at that. I was sitting in my car in the parking lot of a grocery store when I opened it, which meant I sat in that parking lot for forty-five minutes afterward, engine off, watching strangers load bags into their trunks. My hands had gone cold. I pressed them flat against my thighs and they didn't warm up.

He was sorry, he wrote. He'd been in a program. He understood now what he'd done to me and to others and he was not asking for forgiveness, only offering this. The letter was two pages, single-spaced, and the handwriting was cramped and looped in a way I remembered from the birthday cards his mother used to make him sign. I kept glancing at the passenger window. A woman walked past with a cart and I tracked her until she was out of sight, a reflex I barely noticed anymore. I called my husband from the car but when he answered I just said I'd be home late and I was fine. My voice came out flat and unconvincing and he asked twice if I was sure. I said yes. I folded the letter carefully, like it deserved that, and tucked it into my bag, and then I sat there a while longer with the doors locked, watching the light change.

Margaret found it by accident. She'd borrowed her daughter's tablet to look up a recipe, and there it was—a profile picture of Lily, sixteen years old, in an account with 4,000 followers that Margaret had never heard of. Her hands went very still on the screen. The username wasn't Lily's name. The bio said she was nineteen. Margaret sat down on the kitchen floor without meaning to, her back against the cabinet, scrolling through months of posts. Lily posing in ways that made Margaret's throat close. Comments from strangers with numbers in their usernames. She checked the timestamp on the most recent post: forty minutes ago. She could hear Lily upstairs, music playing, completely unaware. Margaret's mouth had gone dry and she couldn't seem to swallow. She put the tablet face-down on the tile beside her and pressed both palms flat against her thighs, staring at the refrigerator, trying to remember how to organize a single thought.

I'm sitting outside my son's bedroom door and I can't make myself knock. It's been twenty minutes. I found the account three hours ago while helping him troubleshoot his laptop, a profile under a name I didn't recognize, and when I clicked on it, my son's face looked back at me from photo after photo. He's fourteen. The account had no privacy settings. None. I wrote down the username on a sticky note and then I folded it and unfolded it so many times the paper split along the crease. My husband keeps texting asking where I am—we were supposed to leave for dinner at seven. I can't explain to him yet. I can't explain it to anyone until I can get my pulse to behave normally, until my voice stops feeling like it belongs to someone standing far away from me. I keep thinking about the comments on the most recent photo, the ones I screenshotted before I closed the laptop. One of them had a location tag. A specific street corner, two miles from our house. I put my forehead against the cool wood of his door. Inside, I can hear him laughing at something on his phone, easy and unconcerned, the way only someone who doesn't know yet can laugh.

The drilling started at 7 a.m. on a Monday. Marcus set down his coffee and stood very still in the kitchen, listening. It was coming from next door — the Heller place, which had sat empty for eleven months since old Gerald Heller was taken to the care home. Someone had bought it. Marcus hadn't introduced himself. He'd watched the moving truck from behind his curtains instead, cataloguing the furniture, the boxes, the silhouettes of strangers. Now he moved slowly to the wall they shared and pressed his palm flat against the plaster. The vibrations moved through his hand and up his arm.

He called his sister that evening and couldn't quite explain why he was calling. "I'm fine," he said, when she asked why he sounded strange. He kept walking to the front window while they talked, parting the blinds with two fingers, checking the driveway next door. A white van was still parked there, unmarked. He'd googled the license plate that afternoon and found nothing. He didn't tell her that part.

By Thursday the workers had started on the shared fence line. Marcus watched from his upstairs window as two men pulled up the old panels, exposing a gap between the properties he hadn't known existed. He took a step back from the glass without deciding to. That night he pushed the small bookcase in front of the back door — just temporarily, he told himself — and then stood in the hallway for a long moment, listening to the house breathe around him.

I heard it the first morning — a low, mechanical groan coming through the bedroom wall, followed by the unmistakable crack of something structural giving way. I sat up fast. My heart was going at a strange, lurching pace, the kind that doesn't settle immediately. I put my feet on the floor and just sat there in the dark for a while.

I introduced myself to the new owner that afternoon, because I thought it would help. Her name was Diane. She shook my hand and told me it was going to be a "full gut renovation" — right down to the foundation in places. She smiled when she said it. I smiled back. Walking home I noticed my jaw ached from how tightly I'd been holding it.

The permit appeared on her door a week later. I photographed it and spent an evening reading about load-bearing walls and soil disturbance and the kinds of cracks that appear in neighboring foundations when contractors don't know what they're doing. I made a document. I started a folder on my desktop labeled simply "Diane." I checked my own basement every morning, running my flashlight along the base of the walls, crouching low to look at the corners, pressing my thumbnail into the mortar between the old stones.

Last night her crew hit something unexpected — I heard the shouting even from my living room, and the work stopped suddenly. I turned off all my lights without thinking, standing in the dark of my kitchen, listening. The shouting died down. An engine started. Headlights swept across my ceiling as a vehicle backed out slowly. I stayed where I was for a long time after the sound disappeared, one hand resting on the kitchen counter, not quite ready to turn the lights back on.

The knock at the door came on a Tuesday morning, and Margaret's stomach dropped before she even reached the handle.
