It was Mr. Calloway from next door, holding a seed catalog, smiling with all his teeth. He wanted to use the strip of land along the back fence—just for tomatoes, he said, and maybe some squash. Margaret's hand gripped the door frame. She nodded along while he talked, her eyes drifting past his shoulder to the fence line, to the gate she always kept padlocked. The gate her ex-husband had installed, had a key to, had threatened to use.

She told Mr. Calloway she'd think about it. Her voice came out smaller than intended. After she closed the door she stood with her back against it for a long moment, pressing her palms flat against the wood grain.

The problem was that a garden meant someone out there regularly. It meant the gate would need to be unlocked, or a new one put in. It meant checking who was moving around back there when she heard footsteps. She went to the kitchen window and looked out at the yard without touching the curtain.

She picked up her phone and put it down three times before calling her sister. She didn't say why she needed to talk. She just needed someone to stay on the line with her for a while.

I've been renting this house for two years and I've never once used the side yard—it's just a narrow strip of grass between my place and the Chen family's fence. So when Linda Chen came over last Sunday and asked if her husband could plant vegetables over there, the reasonable thing would have been to say yes immediately. I could see that. I understood that with my whole brain. My mouth still went dry.

She stood there with a mason jar of pickled cucumbers she'd brought as a kind of advance thank-you, and I held the jar with both hands because one wasn't steady enough. I could feel my pulse in my throat.

The thing is, it's not Linda. Linda is perfectly nice and her husband Walter waves at me when he gets his newspaper. The thing is that the side yard borders the alley, and the alley is how he used to leave. My old neighbor. The one who moved away eight months ago and who I have a restraining order against, which the police were very clear means nothing if he decides to ignore it.

I told Linda I had some concerns about the soil drainage and asked if I could get back to her in a few days. She smiled and said of course and I watched her walk back down the driveway, and then I went inside and checked every window lock, which I already knew were all fine, which I check every single night before bed.

I found the statements by accident, blown into my yard by the wind when Mr. Calloway's recycling bin tipped over. Brokerage accounts. Plural. The kind of numbers that had too many digits. I stood there in my slippers on the wet grass, reading the same line over and over until the cold crept up through the soles of my feet and into my ankles. Mr. Calloway, who wore the same three flannel shirts and drove a rusting Corolla and borrowed my lawnmower twice a summer. Mr. Calloway, who had asked me just last month how much I paid in rent. My mouth went dry.

I folded the paper into a tight square and shoved it into my pocket. The normal thing would have been to walk next door and hand it back. I knew that. Instead I went inside and stood at my kitchen window, watching his house. The lights inside were on. His shadow moved behind the curtain, slow and unhurried. I pressed my back against the wall beside the window, just far enough that I could see without being seen. He had known all along, every conversation about money troubles, every time I'd complained about the heating bill, every time he'd smiled that patient, quiet smile. What else did he know that he hadn't said? The folded paper felt hot inside my pocket, like it was burning.

I ate dinner standing up because I couldn't settle into a chair. I kept rehearsing what I would say. *Hey, this blew into my yard.* Simple. Normal. But my hand wouldn't reach for my phone to text him, and when his porch light clicked on at nine o'clock I stepped back from the window too fast and knocked a glass off the counter. It shattered and I didn't move for a full five seconds, just standing there in the dark with glass around my feet, listening to make sure he hadn't heard.

Priya had lived next to old Mrs. Hennessey for six years without giving her much thought. A widow in a small house, cardigan-wearing, bird-feeding, the kind of woman who appeared at the fence to discuss the weather and then disappeared back inside. Then the renovation crew showed up. Not the modest kind with one truck and a radio playing country music. The kind with multiple vans bearing the logo of a firm whose website Priya, sitting cross-legged on her bathroom floor at midnight, found to specialize in estates worth north of five million dollars.

She put her phone face-down on the tile. Her heartbeat was doing something strange and uneven in her chest, and she pressed the heel of her hand against her sternum like she could smooth it out. She thought about the morning Mrs. Hennessey had watched her load groceries from the discount store into her car. The way the old woman's gaze had lingered, that small unreadable expression. She thought about the conversation two winters ago when she'd mentioned she was behind on her electric bill and Mrs. Hennessey had nodded and said only, *these things have a way of working out, dear.* Priya's teeth pressed together.

The next morning she took her usual route to the bus stop, which meant passing Mrs. Hennessey's front path. The old woman was outside, directing one of the workers with a calm, authoritative gesture Priya had never seen from her before. Mrs. Hennessey turned and raised her hand in greeting, the same gentle wave as always. Priya raised her hand back. She kept her pace steady, kept her expression neutral, concentrated on the rhythm of her own footsteps. She didn't look back. She could feel the space between her shoulder blades the whole way to the corner, an odd, prickling attention, as though someone were watching her walk away and drawing very careful conclusions.

Marcus had been rehearsing the route to the fourth floor all weekend, mapping it in his head like a man preparing for surgery. Monday morning, he stood at the elevator bank with his box of desk belongings, pressing the button three times even though it was already lit. His stomach had been wrong since Friday — not sick exactly, just hollow and tight at the same time. The elevator opened and he stepped in beside two women from Finance who were laughing about something. He smiled at the floor. He'd never met anyone from the Marketing division. Didn't know the rhythms of the place, who to eat lunch with, what the unspoken rules were. He set his box down on the first empty desk he found and sat very still, watching people move through the open office with the ease of people who belonged somewhere.

I kept clicking through the staff directory for the Operations team, reading the same six names over and over until the words stopped making sense. My manager had called it an "exciting opportunity." Had smiled when she said it. I'd nodded and said "absolutely" and then gone to the bathroom and sat on the lid of the toilet for eleven minutes. That was Thursday. Now it was Sunday night and I was ironing a shirt I'd already ironed, running the iron back and forth over the same collar while the TV played something I hadn't been following. My new desk would be next to a man named Dale who had apparently "strong opinions about process," according to the one colleague who'd whispered a warning. I put the iron down and picked up my phone, then put the phone down. I went to the kitchen for water. I didn't drink it. I stood at the counter with the full glass in both hands, not drinking it, listening to the refrigerator hum.

The text from Margot read: "Hey, so the party thing for Saturday — it's off. Everyone bailed. Sorry." I set my phone face-down on the counter and stood very still for a moment. Then I checked the deadbolt on the front door, even though I'd locked it twenty minutes ago. The thing was, I hadn't told anyone it was my birthday. I hadn't told anyone there was a party. I'd found out about the surprise through a pocket-dial from my brother three weeks ago, and I'd kept quiet, waiting. Now I walked slowly to the window and looked out at the street below, scanning each parked car. They'd all bailed at once. Every single one of them. I pulled the curtain a little further across and stood back from the glass, just enough that I couldn't be seen from outside. My brother never forgot my birthday. Not once in thirty-one years.

Dana had been checking her phone every few minutes since noon, waiting for some hint that the afternoon might turn into something festive. Her coworkers had been whispering for weeks — she wasn't stupid — and she'd been looking forward to the warm, easy feeling of being surprised, of being loved. But then Marcus had pulled her aside at her desk, his voice low and apologetic, explaining that the reservation fell through and everyone had already scattered for the long weekend. "No big deal, right?" he'd said, clapping her shoulder. She'd nodded and smiled. What she hadn't mentioned was that Marcus himself had organized the party. She'd seen the email chain by accident — a reply-all that landed in her inbox before someone noticed and deleted it. Marcus had been cc'd on every message. There was no reservation. There was no scattered weekend. She gathered her bag slowly, methodically checking its contents twice. Outside, the parking lot was emptying fast. She kept her steps even and measured, not fast enough to seem like she was running, but she didn't stop moving until she reached her car, locked the doors, and sat watching the building's exit in her rearview mirror, her knuckles pale against the steering wheel.

Marcus found the email by accident. He'd been cc'd on a thread clearly not meant for him—subject line: "Acquisition Timeline - CONFIDENTIAL." His eyes moved across the screen slowly at first, then darted back to the top to read it again. His mouth went dry. He sat very still for a long moment, then quietly closed his office door, something he almost never did. The words "workforce restructuring" and "redundant positions" seemed to hover in the air after he'd minimized the window. He picked up his phone to call his wife, then put it back down. Then picked it up again. He found himself staring at his desk—the framed photo of his kids, the mortgage paperwork he'd brought in to double-check the numbers during lunch—as though cataloguing it all. His lunch sat untouched and cooling beside his keyboard. When his colleague Greg knocked and poked his head in, Marcus turned around too quickly and knocked his coffee onto the floor. "Whoa, you okay?" Greg asked. "Fine," Marcus said, already crouching to clean it up, pressing paper towels hard against the carpet long after the spill was gone.

I keep refreshing the company's internal portal, though I don't know what I'm looking for. I overheard two words outside the conference room this morning—"the buyers"—and my whole body went cold, like I'd swallowed ice. I've been at this company eleven years. I know every crack in the ceiling tiles of my office. I know which elevator takes thirty seconds longer. I know the smell of the place on Sunday mornings when I come in to get ahead of the week. Now I'm sitting at my desk and I can't seem to make my hands do anything useful. I typed the same email four times and deleted it each time. A colleague walked past and said, "You look pale, did you eat today?" and I laughed, which came out wrong—too sharp, too short. On my lunch break I called my brother and said nothing was wrong before he'd even asked. I walked to the parking garage and sat in my car for twenty minutes, engine off, running the numbers in my head: how many months of savings, how long to find something new, what I'd tell my landlord. My grip on the steering wheel left red marks across my palms.

The envelope had been sitting on the kitchen table for three days before Maya finally opened it. Her brother Theo had gotten his copy of their father's will the same morning, but he'd called her immediately, voice bright with something she couldn't name. "Did you read yours yet?" he'd asked. She hadn't answered. She'd made tea instead, then let it go cold, then made more. Now she sat with the document in her hands, and the words kept sliding off her eyes. The house, which she'd assumed they'd split, was listed under her name alone. The investments — their father's careful decades of saving — went entirely to Theo. She read it twice, three times, pressing her finger under each line as though the text might rearrange itself into something more sensible. Her stomach had dropped somewhere around her knees. She put the will down on the table with very deliberate care, the way you'd set down a glass you suspected was already cracked. Theo was driving over now. She could hear, distantly, that her own breathing had become too loud for the room. She went to the window and watched the empty street, her arms folded tight across her chest, her thumbnail pressed hard into her palm. She didn't know which version of the will was right. She didn't know which version was wrong. She didn't know, most terrifyingly, whether her brother did.

I found the second will tucked inside a hollowed-out copy of Moby Dick on the third shelf of my father's study, and for a long moment I just stood there holding it with both hands, not reading it, just feeling the weight of it. Marcus had shown me his copy two hours earlier at the dining room table, smoothing it flat with his palms, walking me through each clause with the calm efficiency of someone who'd already processed his grief and moved on to logistics. His version left the lakehouse to him, the portfolio to me, everything divided cleanly and without sentiment. The document in my hands described something else entirely. I sat down on the floor of the study — not a chair, the floor — with my back against the bookcase. My jaw ached. I realized I'd been clenching it. The will in my hands was dated four months later than Marcus's copy, and it redirected everything: the lakehouse, the portfolio, the small storage unit I didn't even know our father owned, all of it funneled into a trust I'd never heard of, managed by a lawyer whose name I didn't recognize. My name appeared once, Marcus's not at all. The radiator ticked in the corner. I could hear Marcus downstairs, opening and closing cabinets, making himself comfortable in a house that both of our documents, in entirely different ways, had just made treacherous. I folded the will back along its original creases. My hands were doing something I couldn't quite control. I put the paper in my coat pocket and went to stand at the top of the stairs, listening to my brother move around below me, and I did not call down to him.

The knock came at 9 PM, three sharp raps that made me drop my phone onto the hardwood floor. I stood completely still in the middle of my living room, the music still playing, watching the door like it might do something on its own. My palms were sweating. I turned the speaker off, then turned it back on, then off again. Three more knocks. I'd lived in this building for six months and I knew which neighbor this was — the man in 4B who had once left a handwritten note under my door about my "excessive footsteps." I crossed to the peephole and pressed my eye against it, my heart slamming so hard I could feel it in my throat. He was standing there in the hallway with his arms crossed, his face arranged into something that didn't look patient. I stepped back. I didn't open the door. I stood there in my socks on the silent floor, barely breathing, for a full four minutes until I heard him shuffle back down the hall.

Linda was halfway through vacuuming when she noticed him through the window — Mr. Holt, from next door, crossing her front lawn with that particular stiffness in his walk. She switched the vacuum off. Her stomach dropped somewhere low and heavy. She'd had a small gathering last Saturday, just four people, gone by ten o'clock, nothing unreasonable by any measure she could think of. But she moved away from the window anyway, pressing herself against the kitchen wall as though the refrigerator might offer some kind of cover. The doorbell rang and she flinched visibly, her shoulder blades pulling tight against her spine. She set down the vacuum handle with exaggerated care, making no sound. Her breathing came in short, quiet pulls through her nose. The bell rang again, and she stood there counting the seconds, one hand flat against her collarbone, feeling her own pulse jump under her fingers. When she finally opened the door, her voice came out smaller than she intended. "Mr. Holt," she said, and then said nothing else, watching his mouth begin to move.

The letter was still in Maya's hand when she sat down on the kitchen floor. She didn't remember deciding to sit — her legs had simply stopped working. Thirty days. The words kept rearranging themselves every time she tried to read them again. She set the paper down, picked it up, set it down. Her upstairs neighbor was running a vacuum, and each pass of it felt like a physical thing pressing against her skull. She opened her phone to call her sister, then closed it. Opened it again. Her daughter's drawings were taped to the refrigerator above her head — a house with a red door and four stick figures standing out front. Maya pulled her knees to her chest and stared at the water stain on the linoleum until her vision went soft.

I read the notice three times standing in the hallway, then folded it into quarters and shoved it deep into my coat pocket like I could unfind it. All through dinner I watched my husband talk about his day — something about a coworker, something about traffic — and I nodded and moved my fork around the plate. The notice was in my pocket the entire time, and every time I shifted in my chair I could feel the folded edge of it against my hip. I washed the dishes afterward even though it wasn't my turn. I scrubbed the same pan twice. When Marcus asked what was wrong, I said I was tired and turned back to the sink. My reflection looked back at me from the dark window above it, soap bubbles dissolving around my wrists, and I thought about the fact that we had thirty days and nowhere I could think to go.

The byline read "Marcus Webb." I read it three times, my coffee going cold beside me. My article. My words. My eighteen months of research into deep-sea thermal vents, the fieldwork that had left me with a permanent ache in my left knee, the draft I had emailed to Professor Hargrove for "feedback" back in February. I closed the laptop. Opened it again. The journal's website loaded slowly, and I watched the progress bar like it might reverse course and show me something different. My hands had gone to my lap, pressing flat against my thighs. Marcus Webb was Hargrove's graduate assistant. Marcus Webb had never been to the Pacific.

I called my sister instead of the journal's editorial board, which told me something about the state I was in. She said, "Just send them your original files, the timestamps will prove everything." I nodded into the phone as if she could see me. My apartment felt very small and very quiet. I kept glancing at the window, though the third floor looks onto nothing but a brick wall. The original files were on my laptop, and my laptop was right there, open on the table, and still I couldn't make myself move toward it. Professor Hargrove had written my last two recommendation letters.

Later I found the screenshots I needed, the emails, the annotated drafts. I printed everything and stacked the pages into a folder with shaking hands, pressing down on the edges as though they might fly apart. I even labeled it, neat capital letters, my name and the date. The folder sat on my kitchen table for four days before I walked it to the department head's office. The secretary asked if I had an appointment. I told her I could wait, and I sat in the plastic chair outside his door for two hours, watching people come and go, my folder on my knees, my fingers laced on top of it so tightly that when I finally stood, the indentations from my own nails were still white on the backs of my hands.

Diane had been refreshing the journal's page since six in the morning, a habit she'd developed in the two weeks since submitting her piece on urban heat islands. When it finally loaded—"New Issue: Environmental Policy Quarterly"—she scrolled to the contents and found the title immediately. "The Invisible Crisis: Heat Inequity in Mid-Sized American Cities." Her title, word for word. The author listed was Dr. Colin Strause. Her department chair. The man who had personally encouraged her to submit, who had read her draft over coffee and called it "genuinely important work." She sat very still in her office chair. Down the hall she could hear the espresso machine and someone laughing at something.

She pulled up the published PDF with the precision of someone handling something that might go off. The abstract was hers, restructured by a paragraph. The methodology section used her exact phrasing in eleven places she counted before she stopped counting. The acknowledgments thanked a research assistant Diane had never heard of. She became very aware of the sound of the ventilation system, the hum it made, the way it never quite stopped. When her officemate knocked and opened the door to ask about lunch, Diane said "headache" and turned back to her screen and waited for the door to close again before she allowed herself to exhale.

She had told no one the article was under review. That now felt like something other than coincidence, and the recognition of that sat in her stomach like swallowed glass. She opened a new email, typed "Dear Editorial Board," and then minimized the window. She opened her original submission files and checked the metadata on each one, verifying timestamps she already knew by heart. She got up and poured herself a glass of water and drank it standing at the sink, looking at her own reflection in the dark window above it. Then she went back to the desk and maximized the email and moved the cursor to the body of the message, where it blinked at her for a long time before she began to type.

The notification loaded slowly on Maya's phone, the little progress wheel spinning like it had no idea what it was about to do to her. A 54. She read it four times. Her roommate was talking about something — dinner, maybe, or a party — and Maya just nodded, her thumb pressing the home button over and over without purpose. That exam was supposed to fix everything. Her scholarship review was in three weeks. Her parents had no idea she'd been struggling. She set the phone face-down on her desk and sat very still, the way she used to sit as a child when she thought staying perfectly quiet might make a bad thing pass without noticing her. Her hands had gone cold.

She pulled up her email to contact the professor and got as far as "Dear Professor Walsh" before closing the laptop. Outside her window, two students were laughing on the quad, their voices carrying up easily through the glass. Maya watched them without seeing them. She opened her scholarship portal and just looked at the GPA requirement — 3.4 — and then looked at what hers would be if she failed this course. Her jaw tightened. She got up, made herself a cup of tea she didn't drink, and stood in the kitchen for a long time, listening to the refrigerator hum.

I kept refreshing the grade portal, as though the number might change if I caught it off guard. It didn't. A 61. I'd spent eleven days on that paper. Eleven days and two all-nighters and a level of caffeine dependency my doctor would not have approved of. I stood up from my desk and immediately sat back down. My roommate knocked and asked if I wanted to grab food and I said "yeah, one sec" and then didn't move for forty minutes.

The thing that kept circling in my head wasn't even the grade itself — it was my dad's voice the last time we talked. *You're going to be fine, you've always landed on your feet.* He'd said it like a fact, like physics. I opened our text thread and stared at it. My thumbs hovered. I told him I had to study and that I'd call soon. That was technically true. I put the phone in my desk drawer so I wouldn't have to see it.

I went to the bathroom and splashed cold water on my face and looked at myself in the mirror for a while. My reflection looked back at me like it was waiting for a plan. I didn't have one. I gripped the edge of the sink, let the water run until it went from cold to warm, and listened to the pipes knock somewhere inside the wall.

The email had been sitting in my inbox for six days before I finally opened it. "Clerical Error – Urgent Matter – Scholarship Award #4471." My coffee went cold on the desk. I read it four times, each pass making the words blur worse than the last. The financial aid office wanted me to come in "at my earliest convenience," which everyone knows means now. I scheduled the appointment and then immediately moved every item on my desk one inch to the left, then back again. I told my roommate I was going for a walk and instead sat in my car in the parking lot for forty minutes, engine running, watching students cross the quad with their backpacks and their uncomplicated afternoons.

My palms left damp marks on the steering wheel the entire drive to campus the next morning. Mrs. Calloway from financial aid had a kind face, which somehow made it worse. She explained that the scholarship committee had confused two applicants with nearly identical names — Marcus Allen Webb and Marcus Allen Webb Jr. — and that the funds had been deposited into the wrong account. Mine. A full academic year's worth. I nodded along, watching my own hands grip each other in my lap. "We'll need to discuss a repayment arrangement," she said, sliding a paper across the desk. The number on it was the same as my mother's monthly salary. My mouth went completely dry.

I took the long way back to my dorm, cutting through the parking structure where it was dim and smelled like oil and cold concrete. I sat on a low barrier wall and stared at my phone without unlocking it. Next semester's tuition. My on-campus housing. The work-study hours I'd already been scheduled for — hours that wouldn't even come close to covering it. I kept thinking about the night I'd called home after receiving the original award letter, my mother crying a little on the phone, saying she'd known, she'd always known things would work out. My throat tightened until swallowing felt like a small act of effort. A car pulled out nearby and I flinched at the sound of the engine.

Jordan had read the letter so many times the paper had gone soft at the folds. She sat outside Dean Hargrove's office in a plastic chair with wooden armrests, her foot tapping an irregular rhythm against the floor that she couldn't make herself stop. Around her, the administrative building hummed with ordinary business — a printer somewhere, someone laughing behind a closed door — and none of it seemed like it belonged to the same world as the words "administrative misallocation" and "funds disbursed in error." She pressed her thumbnail hard into her palm. The appointment was for 2:15. It was 2:09.

When the door opened, Dean Hargrove gestured her in without warmth. He was the kind of man who kept his desk very clean, Jordan noticed. She sat across from him and fixed her eyes on a stapler near his left hand, waiting. He explained the situation the way you'd explain a plumbing issue — regrettable, fixable, impersonal. The Huang Family Scholarship had specific criteria. Her application had been strong, but the intended recipient had been Mei-Lin Huang, a distant relative of the founding family, which had somehow been overlooked during the selection process. "We've been in contact with Ms. Huang," he said. "She's been very patient." Jordan's stomach turned slowly over. She realized her jaw had been clenched so hard her back teeth ached.

She asked only one question: whether she could keep her enrollment status for the current semester while things were sorted out. Her voice came out at the wrong pitch — too careful, too steady, like a person walking across ice. Dean Hargrove said he'd see what could be arranged. On the way out, she passed the waiting area where she'd been sitting and noticed the impression her hands had left in the fabric of the armrests, pale crescents where she'd gripped without realizing. She pushed through the building's heavy front door into a gray afternoon. Her next class started in twenty minutes. She stood on the steps and couldn't remember which direction it was.

The bouquet was on my doorstep when I got home from work—two dozen red roses, wrapped in cellophane, no card. I stood in the hallway for a long time without going inside, my keys still in my hand. I've told no one where I live. Not since the move. I finally picked the flowers up with two fingers, like they might bite, and carried them straight to the dumpster around the back of the building without unwrapping them. Back in my apartment, I checked the deadbolt twice, then a third time, then pulled a chair from the kitchen and wedged it under the door handle. I sat on the couch with my coat still on and my phone face-up on the cushion beside me, the screen bright, my sister's number already pulled up.

Maya had been watching the bouquet on her kitchen counter for two hours. Sunflowers and white lilies, her favorites—information she was certain she had never shared with anyone at her new job, and she'd lived in this city for exactly six weeks. She'd asked the delivery driver three times if he was absolutely sure he had the right address, her voice coming out thinner and higher than she intended. He'd shown her the label. Her name. Her street. Her apartment number. She'd smiled and thanked him with a voice that didn't feel like hers. Now her tea had gone cold and she kept walking to the window, then back to the counter, then to the window again. She photographed the bouquet from every angle. She photographed the wrapping. She sat down and stood up again almost immediately. Outside, a car she didn't recognize had been idling at the curb for what felt like a very long time, and she couldn't quite make herself stop looking at it.

The profile picture was unmistakably her. Same gap between her front teeth, same way she tilted her head in photos. Maya had spent twenty minutes on the mutual friends page before finally sending the message: *Hey, it's Maya. We used to be inseparable in elementary school. Sandcastle competitions, remember? The summer we tried to dig to China in your backyard?* She'd been checking her phone every few minutes since. When the reply came, she nearly dropped her coffee. *Hey! Sorry, I think you might have me mixed up with someone else? I don't really remember having friends named Maya growing up lol.* Maya read it four times. Then she put her phone face-down on the counter and stood very still, staring at the wall. She pressed her fingertips hard against the edge of the counter. *Not having friends named Maya.* She had a box somewhere in her closet with a photograph of the two of them at Jenny's seventh birthday party, Maya's arm around Jenny's shoulders, both of them missing their front teeth. She went to the closet. She needed to find that box. She needed it in her hands right now.

I rehearsed what I'd say the whole drive over. Twenty-three years is a long time, sure, but some people you just don't forget. Danny and I had been the kind of friends who had a secret knock. We'd mapped every inch of that neighborhood on hand-drawn paper, named the alleyways, claimed territories. When I finally spotted him at his cousin's barbecue, I walked over too fast, smiled too wide. He looked at my face with polite, total blankness. "Danny, hey. It's Cal. Cal Merritt. We lived on Sutter Street?" His eyebrows came together in a patient, searching expression that told me everything before he even opened his mouth. "Oh, man, sorry, I'm terrible with—" he started. I laughed. I don't know why I laughed, but I did, too loud, and then I said something about not wanting to interrupt and backed away toward the drinks table. I stood there holding a cup of lemonade I never drank. My pulse was doing something strange in my throat. There were forty people at this barbecue and the yard felt like it was tilting slightly, like a table with one short leg. I kept glancing at his face from across the lawn, waiting for recognition that never moved in.

The metal box was only six inches down, which was the first thing that made Marcus's hands go still in the dirt. He'd just been turning the soil for a new garden bed, moving methodically along the fence line, when the trowel hit something hollow. He brushed the lid clean and sat back on his heels. His name was written on top in blue marker — his own handwriting, unmistakably — along with a date from eleven years ago. He didn't move for a long time. A neighbor's dog barked somewhere down the street and he flinched hard enough to drop the trowel.

He carried the box to the porch instead of opening it right there in the yard. He didn't know why. He sat with it on his lap for ten minutes, watching the tree line at the edge of his property like he was expecting something to step out of it. His therapist had told him that the memory gaps from his late twenties were a normal response to stress. She had used calm, reasonable words. He had believed her, mostly. The box was cold against his thighs even through his jeans. He pressed his palm flat against the lid and made himself breathe slowly through his nose.

Inside, he found a folded piece of paper, a coin he didn't recognize, and a photograph of himself standing somewhere he had never been — a gray coastline, waves behind him, wearing a jacket he didn't own. He looked happy in the photo. He looked completely at ease. His jaw tightened as he stared at it, and he realized his foot was tapping against the porch boards rapidly, involuntarily, like it was trying to leave without him. He turned the photograph over. On the back, in the same blue marker, were four words: *Don't look for it.*

He set the photograph face-down on the railing. He didn't touch it again for a while. When his phone buzzed in his pocket he made a sound he was embarrassed by, something caught and high in his throat. It was just a calendar reminder. He declined it without reading what it said and sat very still, watching the yard, while the evening came in around him gray and slow.

I found it while I was digging up the hydrangeas I killed last summer — a rusted Altoids tin wrapped in two layers of plastic grocery bags, knotted tight. I remember standing up too fast and getting a head rush that made the yard tilt sideways. I set it down on the grass and walked a full loop around my house before I came back to it. I told myself I needed to stretch my legs. That wasn't what it was.

I lived alone in this house for three years before my sister moved in last spring. Before that it was our grandmother's, and before that I don't know. But the handwriting on the masking tape label on the tin — that was mine. I know my own handwriting. The looping lowercase e, the way I cross my sevens. I sat cross-legged in the wet grass and pressed both palms flat on my thighs and tried to think of a normal reason for this, a funny story I'd forgotten, something I'd done as a kid maybe. The date on the label was nine years ago. I was twenty-six nine years ago. I was living in Portland nine years ago, subletting an apartment with two roommates. I had never been to this house.

My sister came out the back door and asked what I was doing. "Nothing," I said, and my voice came out strange and clipped, like I'd bitten the word in half. She looked at the tin. I picked it up and put it in my jacket pocket before she could ask about it, which was not a normal thing to do, and I could see on her face that she thought so too. I told her I was coming inside in a minute and turned back to the flower bed.

That night I sat at the kitchen table after she went to sleep and opened it. A rolled piece of paper, a key with no label, and a single baby tooth. The paper just had an address — not this address, a different one, a street name I didn't recognize in a city I'd never been to. I got up and checked that the back door was locked. Then the front. Then I stood in the hallway between them for a long time with the key in my fist, not quite ready to go back to the table, not quite ready to go to bed.

The Instagram notification came at 2 a.m., and Maya's thumb had hovered over it for a full minute before she tapped. Someone had tagged her in a photo. A stranger's forearm, photographed against a white bedsheet, bearing her painting—the exact painting, down to the three-legged deer she'd hidden in the corner that nobody ever noticed. Her chest clenched like a fist. She sat up in bed and turned on every light in her apartment, one by one, even the bathroom, even the little lamp in the hallway she never used. She'd never sold prints of that piece. She'd never uploaded a high-resolution version. She set her phone face-down on the mattress, then picked it up again, then set it down. The only person who'd ever photographed that painting up close was her ex, the one who'd made her feel like her work was never fully hers.

The stranger's account was public, which somehow made it worse. She scrolled through it with her jaw clamped shut. There were other tattoos, landscapes and geometric patterns, nothing alarming. The caption beneath her deer painting read simply: *custom piece from a private collection.* She didn't recognize the tattoo artist's handle. She didn't recognize the studio watermark. She put her phone in a kitchen drawer, made tea she didn't drink, and spent the rest of the night sitting cross-legged on her couch, knees pulled to her chest, listening to the sounds her building made. She kept thinking about the word *private*. Private collection. As if someone had been collecting her, piece by piece, without her knowing.

I spotted it on the subway, a Tuesday morning in October, the kind of cold that gets into the back of your throat. The woman across from me had her jacket half-off her shoulder, and there it was on her upper arm: my charcoal sketch of the burning house. The one I'd only ever shown at a single gallery show in 2019, the one I'd taken down before the closing night because it felt too personal. I made myself look out the window at the tunnel wall. I looked back. It was unmistakably mine—the asymmetrical windows, the smoke curving left instead of right, the way I'd drawn the front door slightly open.

My stop came and went. I stood up too late and the doors closed and I just stood there in the rattling car, gripping the pole until my knuckles went white. The woman was reading something on her phone, unbothered, her jacket still loose. I tried to think of a normal way to ask her where she'd gotten it, but every sentence I rehearsed in my head fell apart. I got off at the next stop and walked the long way to work in the cold without my gloves on, not noticing until my hands had gone completely numb. I've never put that sketch online. I've never made a copy. I've spent three days now going through every person I remember from that gallery show, writing their names in a notebook, crossing them out, writing them again.

The driveway was empty. Maya stood in the doorway for a long moment, her coffee mug suspended halfway to her lips, before she stepped outside in her socked feet. She walked the perimeter of the concrete slab twice, as though the car might be hiding somewhere in the twelve feet of space. Her neighbor across the street was watching from his porch, and she opened her mouth to say something but closed it again. A tow truck. She'd heard one, hadn't she? Around 3 a.m. She'd told herself it was a dream. Her hand found the side of the house and pressed flat against the brick.

She went inside and looked up the city's towing hotline, dialing twice because her fingers hit the wrong numbers the first time. The hold music played for eleven minutes. She stood at the kitchen window the whole time, eyes fixed on where her car should have been, the empty space seeming to grow wider the longer she stared at it. She hadn't had a ticket in six years. She owned this property. She had the deed in a manila folder in the second drawer. She pulled that drawer open and checked, even though nobody had asked.

"Ma'am, the vehicle was flagged under a warrant connected to the registered plate." The operator's voice was flat, professional. "You'll need to visit the impound lot on Garfield with a valid ID and proof of ownership." Maya wrote the address down on three separate pieces of paper and photographed each one. She realized she'd been holding her breath during most of the call only when she tried to say thank you and the word came out fractured and thin, barely recognizable.

The impound lot was forty minutes away by bus. She sat in the back row, her folder of documents pressed against her chest with both arms, and watched out the rain-streaked window. A warrant. The word kept arriving in her mind like a knock at an unfamiliar door. She'd bought that car used, three years ago, from a dealership. Paperwork, inspections, a clean title — she'd done everything right. She'd been sure of it. She was less sure now. The bus lurched at a stoplight, and she grabbed the seat in front of her hard enough that the man sitting in it turned around to look at her.

I noticed it before I'd fully woken up. Something was different through the bedroom window — the morning light came in at a different angle, without the usual shadow of my old Civic breaking it up. I went to the window and pressed my forehead against the cold glass. The driveway was bare. A single oil stain on the concrete. For a stupid second I thought maybe I'd parked on the street last night, but I hadn't. I never do. A chain-link feeling settled somewhere behind my sternum.

My landlord, Dale, hadn't said anything about towing. We'd been over this exact spot in the lease — one designated space, included with rent, mine without restriction. I pulled up our text chain and read it from the beginning, slowly, scanning for anything I might have missed or misunderstood. Nothing. My knee was bouncing under the table without me telling it to. I made a cup of tea and didn't drink it.

I knocked on Dale's door at 7:45 a.m. He looked surprised to see me, or maybe just irritated. "Someone complained," he said, not quite looking at me. "Said the car was blocking visibility at the corner. I should've told you." I asked him which company he'd called. He gave me a number on a Post-it. I took it with both hands, which seemed like too many hands for one small piece of paper. "Don't worry, it happens," Dale said, already closing the door. I stood in the hallway staring at the Post-it for a while after the click of his latch.

The lot attendant had a long face and slow movements and seemed unbothered by everything that had ever happened to anyone. He slid a form through the window slot. Two hundred and forty dollars, plus a forty-dollar daily storage fee. I asked him whether a car could legally be towed from a private residential space, and he said I'd need to talk to someone else about that, and I asked who, and he shrugged and said maybe a lawyer. I wrote the check. My signature looked unfamiliar, like someone had practiced imitating it and hadn't quite gotten it right. I drove home the long way, checking the rearview mirror more than I needed to.

The email had been sitting in my inbox for six hours before I opened it. "Important Update Regarding Westbrook College's Future Operations." I read the first paragraph three times, the words rearranging themselves into something I couldn't absorb. My roommate asked me something from across the room and I answered her, though I have no memory of what either of us said. I pulled up my student loan portal in one tab, my unfinished thesis document in another, my parents' contact page in a third, and then just sat there with all of them open, clicking between them without reading any of them. My financial aid. Four semesters of credits. My advisor's research we'd built together. I got up and made tea I didn't drink, standing at the kitchen counter with both hands wrapped around the mug until the heat became uncomfortable and I still didn't move.

Marcus sat in the back row of the emergency town hall and watched the dean speak from behind a podium. Around him, students were crying openly, raising their hands, demanding answers. Marcus didn't raise his hand. He was counting the ceiling tiles. He'd started doing that sometime around the phrase "transfer credit agreements are being negotiated," his eyes drifting upward while his leg bounced a rapid, involuntary rhythm against the chair leg. He was a first-generation student. His parents had flown out for orientation. His mother had kept the campus map folded in her purse for three months. When the student next to him leaned over and whispered, "What are you going to do?" Marcus opened his mouth and found nothing waiting there. He pressed his thumbnail hard into his palm. "Working on it," he said, in a voice that came out much quieter than he intended, barely clearing the space between them.

The magazine fell out of my hands and hit the linoleum with a slap. There it was, full page, glossy: *Marchetti's Famous Cardamom Honey Cake*, photographed on a slate board with little sprigs of thyme I never would have thought to add. My grandmother's cake. Her handwriting still lived in my memory — the smudged pencil, the way she wrote "pinch" for every spice measurement because she said you had to feel it. I picked the magazine back up with both hands, and they were trembling so badly the pages rattled. Carlo Marchetti. I'd worked in his restaurant for eight months in 2019. I'd made that cake for the staff Christmas party. I sat down slowly on the kitchen floor, back against the cabinet, pulling my knees up. My throat had gone very small.

I kept reading the article even though my eyes weren't tracking properly. *A beloved family recipe passed down through three generations of the Marchetti family.* The blood drained from my face so fast I put my head between my knees. Outside, my neighbor was mowing his lawn, and the sound seemed to come from very far away, muffled, like I was underwater. I thought about calling someone — my sister, a lawyer, anyone — but I just sat there with the magazine pressed flat on the floor in front of me, reading the same paragraph over and over. *Three generations.* I thought about my grandmother's hands, dusted in flour at her kitchen table in Łódź. The mower droned on. I couldn't make myself stand up.

Priya had been watching the cooking competition for twenty minutes before her husband noticed she hadn't blinked. On screen, a contestant in a red apron was plating what the judges called "Chef Dominic Reyes's signature turmeric lamb with tamarind glaze," and the studio audience was applauding. Priya's fingers had gone white around the remote. She'd developed that dish over three weeks in her apartment in Leeds, testing it for a catering proposal she'd sent to a dozen restaurants in 2021. One of those restaurants had been Reyes's new place in Manchester. They'd never responded.

Her husband said her name twice before she turned to look at him. "That's my recipe," she said, and her voice came out flat and strange, nothing like her own. She watched him watch her face, and whatever he saw there made him sit forward. She put the remote down very carefully on the cushion, as though it might detonate. On the television, Chef Dominic Reyes was now speaking directly to camera, warm and expansive, explaining that the dish came from "years of experimentation with South Asian technique." Priya stood up and walked to the kitchen. She stood at the counter in the dark for a long time, listening to the television murmur in the other room, pressing her fingertips hard against the cold granite until she could feel them again.

The neighbor left after handing over the newspaper clipping, and Maya stood in her kitchen doorway for a long time without moving. The article was from eleven years ago. The family's last name was different from the sellers', which meant someone had flipped the property in between. She read the headline three more times, her lips moving slightly but no sound coming out. Her coffee had gone cold on the counter.

She started sleeping with the hall light on that week. She told herself it was practical—easier to see if she needed water in the night. But she also found herself pausing at the threshold of the master bedroom each evening, one hand braced against the doorframe, breathing in slow and deliberate. The realtor had disclosed nothing. She had signed papers in a sunlit office and shaken hands and carried in boxes while music played from her phone. She had painted the living room a warm yellow.

The warm yellow looked different now when she stood in front of it. She couldn't explain why. Nothing had changed—the walls were the same walls, the floorboards the same floorboards she had refinished herself over a long spring weekend. But she kept her phone unlocked and her keys on the nightstand rather than the hook by the door, and when the house settled in the deep part of the night, she pulled the blanket up and lay very still, staring at the ceiling and listening until she could identify every sound.

I found out from a true crime podcast, of all things. The host described the address so casually—matter-of-fact, like reciting a bus route—and I was halfway through washing dishes when I heard my street name and set the sponge down very carefully on the edge of the sink. I didn't move for a while after that. The podcast kept playing. I didn't turn it off. I just stood there with my wet hands dripping onto the floor.

That was three weeks ago, and I still haven't told anyone. I rehearsed telling my sister once, even dialed her number, then ended the call before it connected and sat in my car in the driveway for twenty minutes instead. The house looked completely ordinary from outside. Shutters I'd painted myself. A pot of herbs by the front door. It looked like a place where nothing had ever happened. I went back inside eventually, but I stood in the entryway longer than I needed to, like I was weighing something.

The part I keep returning to is the basement. I never thought much about it before—used it for storage, went down occasionally to flip a breaker, didn't dread it any more than basements are generally worth dreading. Now I find reasons not to go down there. I've rerouted my whole day around it without fully admitting that's what I'm doing. Last Tuesday the breaker tripped during a storm and I sat upstairs in the dark for two hours before I made myself descend, one hand tight on the railing, the other holding my phone with the flashlight on and my thumb hovering over my sister's contact. I reset the breaker in about four seconds and came back upstairs so fast I tripped on the last step.

The name on the classroom door made Marcus stop walking so abruptly that his daughter bumped into the back of his legs. "Daddy, come on," Priya said, tugging his hand. He couldn't move. Mr. Callahan. The same block letters, the same dark green door, the same room at the end of the east corridor. He'd stood in this exact spot thirty-one years ago, stomach knotted, learning very quickly that the safest desk was the one farthest from the windows. "Dad." Priya tugged harder. Marcus realized he'd been gripping her hand too tight and forced himself to loosen his fingers.

The open house was almost over. Other parents drifted past with coffee cups and name tags, laughing easily. Marcus stood against the back wall with his arms crossed over his chest, watching the door. When it finally opened and a tall man with the same gray eyes and the same unhurried walk came through, Marcus felt the room tilt slightly. Mr. Callahan had barely aged. That felt wrong, like a violation of some natural law. The man moved through the classroom touching things—a globe, a stack of textbooks—with the same proprietary ease Marcus remembered from childhood.

"Parents, welcome." The voice was identical. Low. Measured. Marcus's jaw had been clenched so long it had started to ache. He watched Priya pull out her new notebook and uncap her pen with complete trusting readiness, and something surged up through him so fast he had to press his back harder against the wall. He told himself he was just watching. He told himself he was just being thorough.

On the drive home, Priya talked about how cool Mr. Callahan seemed, how he had a whole shelf of fossils, how he'd said everyone in his class was treated fairly. Marcus kept his eyes on the road and said "mm-hmm" at the right intervals, his knuckles white on the steering wheel, his mind already drafting an email to the principal that he wasn't sure yet whether he would send.

I recognized the handwriting before I recognized the name. That particular looping capital M, the way the letters leaned too far right, like they were all trying to escape off the edge of the page. It was on the welcome letter tucked into Jonah's back-to-school folder, and I stood over the kitchen counter reading it three times while my son ate cereal behind me and talked about baseball.

Mrs. Mercer had been teaching fourth grade for thirty-eight years. The letter said so, cheerfully, in the second paragraph. I did the math without meaning to and then stood there with the paper in my hand while my stomach did something slow and unpleasant. Jonah was in fourth grade now. I had been in fourth grade in 1989. The numbers worked out exactly.

I told myself there was nothing to do. I told myself it had been a long time. I stood in the shower that night until the water ran cold, replaying incidents I hadn't thought about in years—the way she'd read my answers aloud to demonstrate what not to do, always sweetly, always with that same tilted smile. The way I'd started faking stomachaches on Mondays. I scrubbed my arms like I was trying to remove something.

I volunteered to drive Jonah to school the next morning just so I could walk him in and see for myself. She was at the classroom door greeting students, and her eyes landed on me with the bright professional warmth of someone who had never made a single child feel small. She did not recognize me. I held out my hand and said my name, watching her face for any flicker. Nothing. "Jonah's mom, wonderful," she said, and turned to my son with that tilted smile, and I heard myself say, from somewhere far away, "He's really been looking forward to this year."

The moment Reyes pulled up her portfolio on the conference room screen, my stomach dropped through the floor. Every slide. The color palette, the typographic hierarchy, the user flow diagrams — identical to mine, down to the unconventional serif I'd spent three weeks selecting. My hands found each other under the table and held on. I could feel my pulse in my fingernails. She was still talking, bright and confident, while I sat there doing something with my face that I hoped looked like polite attention. I had been working on this for eleven months. Eleven months of weekends and cold coffee and rescheduled birthday dinners. My breathing had gone very shallow. When my manager glanced at me with an expression I couldn't decode, I looked down at my notebook and wrote nothing, pressing the pen so hard into the paper it nearly tore.

She found Marcus's GitHub repository at 11:47 on a Tuesday night, and for a long moment Nina just sat there, the laptop's glow the only light in the room. Same dataset. Same preprocessing pipeline. Same novel approach to the noise-filtering problem she'd believed, genuinely believed, was hers alone. She got up and walked to the kitchen and poured a glass of water and set it down without drinking it. She checked the commit history. His first commit was dated four days before hers. Four days. She walked back to the bedroom and sat on the edge of the bed and looked at her hands. She'd quit her postdoc position for this. Moved cities. Told her advisor she had something genuinely new. Her dog lifted his head from the corner, watching her, and she didn't reach for him the way she normally would have. She just sat there, very still, listening to a sound she eventually realized was her own breathing.

Maya refreshed her email four times before she accepted that the words weren't going to rearrange themselves into something better. "It is with mixed emotions that I announce my departure from Hartwell University, effective January 31st." Professor Chen's name sat at the bottom of the department-wide message like a stone dropped into still water. Maya's hands went cold. She was thirty-two pages into a thesis that only one person on earth fully understood, and that person was apparently moving to Copenhagen. She opened a new email to Professor Chen, typed "Dear Professor," and then sat staring at those two words until her office hours alarm went off forty minutes later. She hadn't typed another syllable.

The next morning, Maya showed up to their weekly meeting with her annotated chapter draft, her color-coded notes, and a thermos of coffee she'd forgotten to drink. She'd organized everything the night before at 2 a.m., reorganizing it twice more by 4. Professor Chen was warm, practical, reassuring—she talked about remote supervision, about Skype meetings, about colleagues who could serve as secondary readers. Maya nodded at each point and wrote everything down in her notebook with careful, deliberate handwriting. That evening, she found she'd pressed so hard with the pen that three pages below were embossed with the same words, over and over: *remote supervision, secondary reader, transition plan*.

I found out from Marcus, who found out from the graduate coordinator's assistant, which meant it had been circulating for at least a week before anyone thought to tell me directly. Dr. Okafor was taking a chair position at Stanford. I laughed when Marcus said it—this short, clipped sound that didn't belong to any emotion I recognized. "Good for him," I said, and meant it, and then excused myself to the bathroom where I gripped the sides of the sink and stared at my own reflection until someone knocked on the door.

I've been in this program for four years. My thesis is on vernacular architecture in post-colonial West Africa, which is not a subject you can walk down a hallway and find another expert in. I know this because I spent the rest of that afternoon walking down hallways—literally, the third floor of the humanities building, end to end, eleven times—stopping occasionally to read faculty profile pages on my phone as though the bios might have changed since the last time I checked. My stomach felt like I'd swallowed a fistful of gravel. I kept picturing my thesis document on my laptop, all 187 pages of it, floating untethered.

My meeting with Dr. Okafor is Thursday. I've drafted seventeen different opening sentences and deleted every one. This morning I packed my bag for the meeting and then unpacked it and then packed it again, pulling out my draft chapters, sliding them back in, standing in my kitchen doorway for a long moment before I finally made myself leave.

The email had been sitting in Maya's inbox for six hours before she opened it. She'd seen the subject line—"Regarding Your Scholarship Application"—and put her phone face-down on her desk, then moved it to her drawer, then taken it out again. When she finally read it, standing in the kitchen, she had to read the first sentence four times. *We regret to inform you.* Her roommate walked in and asked what she wanted for dinner. "I don't know," Maya said, her voice coming out barely above a whisper, a thin and breakable thing. She set the phone down very carefully, like it might explode. Without her parents' tuition money, without the scholarship, she had already done the math a hundred times, and the math had never once worked out. She stood at the kitchen counter and stared at a water stain on the ceiling tile until her roommate said her name again.

I kept refreshing the portal even after the decision had loaded, as if the numbers might rearrange themselves into something survivable. Denied. The word sat there, small and final. I walked to my afternoon lecture and took notes on things I couldn't afford to learn anymore. I wrote the date at the top of the page in very neat handwriting. My handwriting is never that neat. The professor asked me to stay after class—something about my participation grade—and I nodded and said "of course" with a smile so rigid it hurt my jaw. On the walk back to my dorm I called my mother and then hung up before the second ring, sitting down on a bench outside the library instead, watching students stream in and out through the glass doors with their backpacks and their futures, my thumb hovering over her contact for a long time. I didn't know yet how to say the words out loud. I wasn't sure what would happen to me once I did.

The ring sat in the glass case like it was waiting for me. I recognized it immediately—the small oval sapphire, the tiny chip on the left prong that Grandma always said gave it character. My hand left a smear on the display case where I'd pressed my palm flat to steady myself. Grandma had been buried with that ring. I had stood at her graveside and watched them close the casket. My mouth went dry and I looked up at the pawn shop owner, then at the door, then back at the ring, a triangle of glances I couldn't stop repeating.

"Can I help you?" the man behind the counter asked. He had a beard and kind eyes and I did not trust any of it.

I told him I was just browsing. My voice came out wrong, too clipped, too high. I pulled out my phone and pretended to check a message, but what I was actually doing was photographing the ring through the glass without him noticing. Or trying not to let him notice. My hands were moving in a way that didn't feel connected to my body anymore. The fluorescent lights hummed. Someone had sold my grandmother's ring to this shop, and that meant someone had opened her casket, and I needed to leave, and I needed to stay, and I could not decide which.

Marcus had been searching for his grandmother's engagement ring for three years, ever since it went missing from the estate before the family could account for it. He'd assumed it was lost to the chaos of grief, to the shuffling of furniture and the well-meaning relatives who'd helped themselves to small mementos. He had not assumed he would find it on a Tuesday afternoon in a pawn shop on Delancey Street, nestled between a trombone and a tray of mismatched cufflinks.

He stood very still. A man beside him was haggling loudly over a guitar amp, and the noise felt far away. Marcus's uncle had handled the estate. His uncle, who had three kids in private school and a boat he couldn't afford. Marcus pressed his tongue against the back of his teeth and said nothing. The ring's distinctive filigree band caught the light—his grandmother had shown it to him a hundred times, traced the pattern with her fingertip while she told him about the night his grandfather proposed. There was no question.

The pawn shop owner was watching him now, probably reading something in the way Marcus had gone rigid. "Interested in that one?" he asked, nodding at the ring. Marcus made himself nod slowly. He asked how much, and listened to the number, and said he'd think about it. He was already calculating—whether the ticket date could tell him anything, whether a pawn shop kept records, whether he should call someone before he called his uncle, and who exactly that someone should be. He walked to his car and sat in it for a long time before he could make himself start the engine.

The nurse's voice on the phone was too careful, too measured. "We need you to come in as soon as possible, Ms. Reyes. There's been an administrative matter with your file." I sat down on the kitchen floor without meaning to. My dog came and pressed his nose against my knee and I gripped his collar like a railing. Administrative matter. I turned the phrase over and over. I'd been calling about my bloodwork for three weeks. Three weeks of "still processing" and "the doctor will follow up." My mouth had gone completely dry. I grabbed my keys twice before I was able to actually stand up and walk to the car.

The doctor, a man I'd never met before, slid a folder across his desk and then seemed to reconsider it, pulling it back slightly. He explained slowly that my records had been merged with another patient's—someone with the same first and last name, same birth month. The treatment notes, the diagnoses, the medication history. Mixed together. His pen tapped a steady rhythm against his notepad. I realized I'd been pressing my thumbnail into my palm hard enough to leave a mark. "So the abnormal results from six months ago," I started, and stopped. I couldn't finish the sentence. I watched his face instead, looking for something to hold onto.

I drove home on a route I didn't recognize until I was already parked outside my building. I sat in the car for a long time with the engine off. My hands were still on the wheel. The folder they'd given me—a corrected copy of my actual records—sat on the passenger seat and I could not bring myself to open it, not yet. Every few minutes I would reach toward it and then pull back. Upstairs, my dog was probably waiting by the door. The streetlight above me flickered once, twice. I stared at the folder. Eventually I got out of the car without it.

Marcus had been sitting in the hospital's patient services office for eleven minutes, and he had counted every one of them. The walls were the pale yellow of old newspapers. A poster about hand-washing hung slightly crooked above the administrator's empty chair. When the woman finally returned, she was carrying a thick manila envelope and wearing the particular expression of someone who had rehearsed what they were about to say. His leg had not stopped moving since he sat down. He pressed his foot flat to the floor to make it stop.

She explained it efficiently: a data entry error two years ago had linked his file with another patient, a man named Marcus T. Holloway to his Marcus J. Holloway. The other man had a significant cardiac history. Stents, irregular rhythms, two hospitalizations. Marcus J. had read those notes at his last physical and his doctor had asked him—gently, carefully—about his heart. He had gone home that night and Googled symptoms for three hours. He had changed how he ate. He had woken up at 2 a.m. more times than he could count, one hand pressed flat against his sternum, feeling for something wrong. Now the administrator was telling him that none of it applied to him. His actual records were, she said, "unremarkable." She said it like good news. He nodded. His jaw ached from how tightly he'd been holding it. "And going forward," he said, his voice landing somewhere quieter than he intended, "how do I know this won't happen again?"

Maya refreshed her followers list three times, convinced she was misreading it. Mr. Hargrove. Her AP Chemistry teacher. The little circular profile photo was unmistakably him — same square glasses, same beige cardigan. Her thumb hovered over the screen without moving. She set the phone face-down on her desk, then picked it up again almost immediately. Her account was public. Had been public for two years. Every stupid video, every photo from the lake house last summer, every late-night thought she'd typed out and posted without thinking. She walked to her window and stood there looking at nothing in particular, her arms crossed tight over her chest. When her mom called her down for dinner, she didn't respond for a full minute.

Her best friend Jessie thought it was funny. "Just block him," she said, shrugging over her lunch tray the next day. Maya nodded like that was a reasonable thing to do, like she hadn't already spent forty minutes the night before scrolling back through two years of her own posts trying to figure out which ones he might have seen. She kept thinking about the video she'd made last month, the one where she'd complained about the chemistry midterm being unfair, doing an impression of his monotone voice. Her stomach had been off since yesterday morning. She'd only eaten half her sandwich. "Yeah," she said. "Yeah, I'll just block him." She didn't touch her phone for the rest of the lunch period.

She sat in third row during chemistry on Thursday, which was her usual seat, but it felt different now. Every time Mr. Hargrove walked past her desk she found something very interesting to examine in her notebook. She'd worn her hair down, which she almost never did, as if somehow this made her less recognizable. When he handed back their quizzes and paused a half-second too long near her desk before moving on, her pen pressed so hard into the paper it nearly tore through. She'd gotten a ninety-four. She stared at the number like it was written in a language she didn't understand. After class she took the long route to her locker, the one that added six minutes to her walk, just so she didn't have to pass his open classroom door.

I noticed it on a Tuesday night, while I was lying in bed doing the thing where you mindlessly scroll through your own profile just to see what other people see. There was his name in my followers. Just sitting there. Mr. Deluca. I remember putting my phone down on my chest and staring at the ceiling for a long time. My heartbeat was doing something strange and irregular, like a drum being played by someone who didn't quite know the rhythm. I kept thinking: how long has he been there? I have 847 posts. My account is set to public. He could have been there for months.

I didn't sleep well. I know this because my alarm went off and it felt like being hit by something. On the bus to school I sat in the very back, which I never do, and kept my bag on my lap even though there was plenty of room around me. I practiced what I might say if I passed him in the hallway. Something normal and unbothered, like "morning" or nothing at all. I couldn't land on the right option. Every version I rehearsed in my head came out sounding like a question. When the bus pulled up to the school building I was the last one to stand up.

He teaches English. I have him second period, every day. I walked in and took my seat and he was writing something on the board with his back to the class, and my whole body went very still in the way it does when you think something is about to happen but you don't know what. We were doing peer review that day, which meant he was circling the room. Every time his footsteps got close to my row I adjusted my paper or recapped my pen or found some reason to be busy with my hands. When he stopped beside me and said "good work here, the structure is strong," I said "thanks" in a voice I barely recognized. I looked at the page the entire time. My handwriting looked foreign to me, like someone else had written it.

The boxes had been sitting in my mother's attic for three weeks before I could bring myself to open them. Even then, I did it in increments—lifting one flap, then letting it fall. When I finally looked inside, I found them: thirty years of school assignments, organized by grade, each one labeled in my mother's handwriting. My palms went cold. I sat back on my heels and stared at the wall for a long moment.

It wasn't the math tests that did it to me. It wasn't even the crayon drawings from first grade, my name spelled wrong in wobbly letters. It was the journal entries from seventh grade, the ones Mr. Horowitz had collected every Friday. I'd forgotten about those. My throat closed. I'd written things in those pages—real things, the kind of things a twelve-year-old writes when they believe no one will ever truly look.

I picked up one entry and read my own handwriting: *I think there's something wrong with me that can't be fixed.* My mother had kept it. She had folded it neatly and placed it in a plastic sleeve and carried it through four house moves. My hands started trembling before I fully understood why. I set the page down very carefully, the way you set down something that might shatter.

I pulled out my phone and dialed her number. She answered on the second ring, and I opened my mouth, and then closed it again. "Hey, Mom," I finally said. "I'm going through your attic." The silence on the other end stretched out, and I heard her breathing change, and I realized she knew exactly which box I'd found.

Marcus pulled another folder from the storage unit and his stomach lurched sideways. He'd come here to clear out his late mother's belongings—a practical errand, something to get done before the unit's lease ran out. He had not expected this. Every report card, every science project rubric, every handwritten essay going back to kindergarten, sorted and dated with a librarian's precision.

His shoulders hunched forward without him deciding to move them. He set the folder down on top of a box and walked to the unit's entrance, stood in the cool air for a moment, looked at the parking lot.

The problem wasn't the assignments themselves. The problem was a specific memory surfacing—tenth grade English, Mrs. Calloway's class, the essay he'd written about his father. He'd said things in that essay. Bitter, specific, detailed things, the kind of teenage cruelty that gets written down once and then, mercifully, disappears. Except it hadn't disappeared. He could see from the sheer organizational commitment in these folders that his mother had kept everything. Everything.

He went back inside. His hands moved through the folders with mechanical steadiness, but his jaw was clenched tight enough to ache. He found the tenth grade section. His fingers stopped moving. He stood there with his hand resting on the tab, not lifting it, not pulling anything out, his breath going shallow and quick.

"Just look," he said aloud, to no one. His own voice sounded unfamiliar in the low concrete space, and he did not look, not yet. He stood very still, listening to traffic outside, giving himself one more minute before whatever he'd written at fifteen became something he had to reckon with now.

My hands won't stop moving over the spines of the records, pulling them out, sliding them back. Forty years of collecting—the mint-condition pressings, the signed sleeves, the rare imports I'd hunted across three continents. Delia was nine years old when she first started asking questions, and somewhere between then and now, the questions became knowledge that eclipsed mine. Last Tuesday she found the Velvet Underground acetate I'd kept in its sleeve behind the water heater. She wasn't snooping; she just *knew*. "Dad, this belongs in a museum," she'd said, matter-of-factly, already photographing it with her phone. I'd laughed then. But now I'm standing in my own collection at two in the morning, chest hollow, because she's been posting about it. The comments are in the thousands. Strangers know my address. She told one of them—casually, in a reply I found by accident—that the collection is "all going to me eventually." She's thirteen. I don't know when I stopped hearing pride in that sentence and started hearing something else entirely. I pick up a record and put it back. Pick it up. Put it back.

From across the backyard, Margaret watched her son through the greenhouse glass. He was twelve, and he had his hands in her soil the same way she'd been putting her hands in soil since before he was born—but there was nothing familiar about what she was seeing. He moved through her rows with a precision she'd never taught him, pruning things she hadn't asked him to prune, relocating seedlings according to some logic she couldn't follow. He'd started sleeping out there on weekends. He'd started ordering supplies with the emergency credit card she'd given him for school trips. When she'd opened the compost bin last week, she'd found her entire collection of heirloom seed envelopes, decades of careful saving, submerged in a new mixture he'd developed after watching a YouTube channel she'd never heard of. He'd said it would work better. His jaw had a set to it that her own jaw had. She'd nodded like she agreed. Now her palms pressed flat against the cold greenhouse glass and her breath fogged it in small, rapid clouds. He didn't look up. He never looked up anymore when she came to the door.

The notes reached me before I even turned the corner. I stopped walking so abruptly that the man behind me muttered something and stepped around my frozen body. It was my song. Not a song like mine. Not an influence or a coincidence. My song—the chord progression I'd never shown anyone, the unresolved suspension in the bridge that my producer had called "too strange to work." My hands found the cold brick wall beside me. The busker was young, maybe nineteen, playing my song on a battered guitar outside the entrance to the subway. I watched him for a full minute without approaching. Who had given it to him? I hadn't released it. I hadn't played it at an open mic, hadn't recorded a demo anyone could access. The file lived on one laptop and one external hard drive, both in my apartment. My stomach clenched so hard I had to press my fist against it. I took out my phone to film him, then put it away, then took it out again. When he finished and looked up, I opened my mouth and nothing came out.

Marcus sat very still in the third row as the girl on stage played the opening bars of "Harbor Light." He had written that song in a hospital waiting room six years ago, on the back of a parking receipt, the night his father had his first stroke. He had never released it. He had played it once at a small venue in Portland, a Thursday night with maybe forty people present. The girl was seventeen at most, performing it now at a regional showcase, adjusting a few of the words but leaving the melody completely intact, right down to the little hesitation he always put before the final chorus—a personal tic, not a written instruction, something that lived only in his muscle memory. His program was folded into a tight rectangle in his lap. He kept smoothing it flat and folding it again. The woman beside him leaned over and whispered that the performer was wonderful, really talented, didn't he think? Marcus nodded without turning his head. He was cataloguing every person he had ever let close enough to hear him play that song alone, privately, working through the list and finding no clean answer, the applause around him seeming to come from a very great distance.

The woman across the coffee shop kept staring at me. Not rudely—more like she was trying to solve an equation. I recognized her from Marcus's Instagram, one of those old photos he'd explained away as "just a friend from work." She was walking toward my table now and I wrapped both hands around my mug even though the coffee had gone cold. My stomach dropped floor by floor, like a slow elevator.
