She was also the one who had complained to the landlord—he knew that much from the building manager's tight-lipped explanation. What the building manager didn't know was that Marcus had been the one anonymously reporting Rosa's late-night piano playing for the past two years, the notes she'd leave about noise accumulating in her file, the picture he'd quietly painted of her as a problem tenant. He'd wanted her apartment. It was bigger, faced the park. He'd never thought it would loop back around like this. He stood in the doorway holding a half-empty bag of sugar, unable to meet her eyes, listening to her talk about her granddaughter's recital.

I read the notice three times standing in the hallway, and both times I got to the part about "repeated lease violations" I had to stop and put it down on the floor. My landlord, Mr. Osei, is seventy-one years old and has owned this building since before I was born. He gave me a payment plan when I lost my job two years ago. He fixed my radiator on a Sunday. I had been subletting the spare room since March—cash, no record, a steady stream of strangers I found online—and telling him I lived alone. I used the money for things I don't need. New shoes. A weekend trip. The subletting clause is right there on page four of my lease, underlined, and I had read it carefully before I signed. I can hear Mr. Osei's footsteps sometimes through the ceiling of his ground-floor office, slow and deliberate, and right now the sound of them makes me press my back against the cold wall and stare at the water stain on the ceiling until my eyes ache.

Marcus refreshed the journal's website for the third time that morning, watching Dr. Ellison's name sit neatly above the title of the article. His article. He'd written every word of it over fourteen months, staying late in the lab while Ellison attended conferences and faculty dinners. The acknowledgments section didn't mention him at all. His coffee had gone cold an hour ago and he hadn't touched it. He kept thinking about the graduate student down the hall, Priya, who'd asked him last week if he was going to submit anything this cycle. He'd told her to keep going, that the work mattered even when recognition was slow. He'd said it with such confidence. Now he sat with his hands flat on the desk, pressing them down like he was trying to keep something from rising up through the floor.

He had known, in a way. Ellison had asked to "review" the final draft two weeks before the submission deadline, and Marcus had handed it over without asking questions. He told himself it was deference, professional respect, the natural order of things. He was good at telling himself things. The truth was that he'd wanted Ellison's approval badly enough that he hadn't looked too closely at what the arrangement implied. He found himself unable to write a single line of the complaint email he'd opened and closed four times. Each time the cursor blinked at him in the empty subject line, he found a reason to minimize the window.

His phone buzzed. It was his mother, who had been waiting for months to hear that her son had finally published his research. He stared at her name on the screen until it stopped ringing. The silence that followed felt like something he deserved.

---

I saw my own words in print for the first time on a Tuesday, in a waiting room of all places, in a copy of a literary magazine someone had left on a chair beside an artificial fern. The byline read "Catherine Holt." My editor's name. I'd submitted the piece eight months ago and heard nothing back, so I'd assumed it was rejected. I sat holding the magazine with both hands while the receptionist called someone else's name twice before I registered that she was talking to me.

I keep returning to the revision notes Catherine sent me in March, the ones I incorporated almost entirely before the final submission. I keep thinking about how I didn't push back, how I thanked her for the feedback like I was grateful to be reshaped. My stomach has been doing something strange and persistent ever since the waiting room, a low rolling sensation that arrives whenever I pick up my phone and think about calling her. The thing is, I know why I haven't called. It isn't only that I'm afraid. It's that calling would mean saying out loud what I believe happened, and once I say it, I'll also have to say that I helped make it possible.

My writing group meets on Thursday. They've been asking about that piece for months. I've been telling them I'm still waiting to hear back, which was true until Tuesday and is now a different kind of thing entirely. Last week one of them said I should be proud of how far my voice has come. I laughed and said something modest, and the laugh is still sitting in my chest like a stone.

Marcus stared at the 47% scrawled in red ink at the top of his essay. Around him, other students were folding their papers away, some groaning softly, some nodding with quiet satisfaction. He couldn't fold his. His hands just held it flat on the desk while Professor Ellison moved between the rows. The night before the submission deadline, Marcus had found the essay his roommate Derek had written for a different class two years ago—same prompt, different course. He'd told himself he was just using it as a reference. He'd told himself that four times, actually, each time he copied another paragraph and changed a few words. Now he sat very still, and when Professor Ellison paused at his desk and said gently, "Come see me during office hours, Marcus," he couldn't meet her eyes. He nodded at his desk instead. His lunch was in his bag and the thought of eating made his stomach clench like a fist.

I got the email at 6:47 in the morning, before my alarm went off. I remember the exact time because I've thought about it every day since. 58%. For a moment I genuinely couldn't understand it—I'd spent three weeks on that research paper, had color-coded my citations and everything. Then I scrolled down to the feedback section and read the words Professor Nakamura had written, and I put my phone face-down on my nightstand and lay there staring at the ceiling. "Significant portions of this paper reflect ideas and phrasing borrowed without proper attribution." I'd found an article online the night before the deadline, when I was exhausted and behind, and I'd lifted whole sentences from it and scattered them through my draft like I was hiding something in plain sight. I called my mom that afternoon and she asked me how school was going. I said, "Fine, busy." I've said that four times now. My roommate asked why I keep cleaning the kitchen at midnight and I told her I just couldn't sleep.

Maya had read the email four times, and each time she set her phone face-down on her desk. The financial aid office had made a clerical error eighteen months ago. The Hargrove Merit Scholarship—the one that had paid her tuition, her housing, her meal plan—had been intended for a girl named Priya Nair, whose application scores were nearly identical to Maya's. Priya had apparently spent the last year and a half working double shifts at a diner, deferring her enrollment. Maya had known something felt off when the award letter arrived, had even picked up the phone once to call the office. She'd talked herself out of it both times, told herself it must have been correct, told herself she'd earned it somehow. Now she sat at the desk the scholarship had paid for, in the dorm room the scholarship had funded, and she couldn't look at her own textbooks. She got up and opened the window even though it was November, needing the cold air on her face.

I've been avoiding the commons area all week. That's where I first saw the flyer—somebody had printed it out and taped it to the bulletin board, a profile piece from the local paper about Priya Nair, the student who had nearly dropped out of higher education entirely after a financial aid mix-up left her without funding. I'd ripped it down before anyone could see me reading it. The scholarship committee had reached out to me yesterday through a very careful, very polite letter, explaining that a review had uncovered the discrepancy. They weren't asking me to return the money. That was somehow worse. I keep starting sentences in my head—apologies I could write, explanations I could offer—and abandoning them before they become anything real. At dinner last night my roommate asked why I wasn't eating and I said I wasn't hungry, which wasn't a lie exactly. My fork just kept moving things around the plate. I've looked up Priya's email address three times now and closed the browser window three times. My hands feel strange whenever I sit down at my laptop, like they don't quite belong to me.

The bouquet sat on Maya's kitchen counter, still wrapped in cellophane, water pooling at its base. Twelve white tulips. She'd circled them three times since the delivery driver left, picking up her phone and putting it down again. Her sister had been in the hospital for four days now, and Maya hadn't visited once. She told herself it was the drive, the parking, the fact that she didn't know what to say. The tulips had no card, no explanation, just this quiet beauty sitting in her kitchen while her sister lay forty minutes north in a hospital gown. Maya filled a vase with water and then set it in the sink empty and stood there with both hands gripping the rim of the basin, staring at the drain.

The flowers were probably from her neighbor, or maybe from the book club women who didn't know what else to do. It didn't matter who sent them. What mattered was that Maya kept seeing her sister's face on the phone screen, the way she'd smiled tiredly and said no, don't worry, it's fine, don't make the drive. And Maya had said okay. She'd actually said okay. She transferred the tulips into the vase now, one stem at a time, her movements slow and deliberate, the way a person moves when they are trying not to think about something specific and failing completely.

She put the vase on the windowsill and texted her sister a photo of the flowers. Beautiful, right? No idea who sent them. Her sister replied with a heart emoji eleven minutes later, and Maya stared at that small red shape until her vision blurred. She turned away from the window. She picked up her car keys. She put them down. She picked them up again and stood in the middle of her kitchen holding them hard enough that the teeth pressed pink ridges into her palm.

I've kept the flowers on my desk for going on five days now. They're starting to brown at the edges, these strange purple irises that appeared outside my apartment door on Tuesday morning. No card, no note, nothing. The first thing I thought of, before I'd even picked them up, was Danny. That's the problem right there. Danny has been trying to reach me for three weeks since the dinner party, and I haven't returned a single call. I didn't defend him when Marcus started in on him. I sat at my end of the table and I laughed, actually laughed, because I wanted Marcus to like me and Marcus was on a roll and the table was laughing and it was just so much easier. Danny left early and I watched him go and I refilled my wine glass.

The irises weren't from Danny. I know that rationally. He doesn't even know where I live. But every time I sit down at my desk to work, they're there in my peripheral vision, nodding slightly when a truck passes on the street below. I've started doing odd things. I reorganized my entire bookshelf on Wednesday. I deep-cleaned the bathroom grout with a toothbrush on Thursday, crouched on the tile floor for two hours, scrubbing in small circles. Last night I baked a loaf of bread I didn't want and left half of it in the hallway for whoever might take it. The flowers shed a petal this morning, purple against white paper, and I left it exactly where it fell.

I've drafted the text to Danny probably seven or eight times. Each version is worse than the last, more elaborate, more explaining, more justifying what I did and why the moment got away from me. This morning I deleted all of them and just typed: I should have said something. That's it. I've been staring at it for an hour. The irises are going over. By tomorrow they'll be done, and I'll have to throw them out, and I still won't know who sent them or why, and none of that is actually the thing that's keeping me from sleeping.

Marcus had typed and deleted the message four times before finally sending it: "Hey, it's Marcus. We grew up together on Fenwick Street. Just saw you got promoted—congratulations." He'd found Daniel's LinkedIn profile while procrastinating at his desk, and the old photograph in the corner of the screen—Daniel's easy smile, those same wide-set eyes—had stopped him cold. The reply came back in forty minutes. "Hey! Sorry, I'm blanking—did we go to Riverside together?" Marcus put his phone face-down on his desk. He already knew, somewhere deep in his chest, why Daniel didn't remember. Marcus had moved away without saying goodbye. He'd been twelve, furious about the divorce, furious at everything, and when his mother had finally packed the car, he'd refused to come to the door when Daniel knocked. Just sat on his bedroom floor listening to the knocking stop. He picked up his phone again and stared at the blinking cursor in the reply box for a long time. His thumbs hovered. He set it back down and went to get a glass of water he didn't want.

I recognized her immediately—Priya, standing in the checkout line at the grocery store, older but unmistakably herself. Same way of standing with her weight on one foot, same silver earrings she'd always favored. I almost called out her name before I remembered that the last time I'd seen her, I'd told the other girls in sixth grade that she'd cheated on the geography test. I hadn't known for certain. I'd guessed, and repeated it anyway, because Caitlin Park had laughed and included me in things after that. Priya had eaten alone for the rest of the year, and then her family had moved that summer. I watched her place her groceries on the belt. I could feel my own heartbeat in my throat. When she glanced over, I smiled, and she smiled back with the polite, mild expression you give a stranger. "Priya?" The word came out before I could decide whether to say it. She tilted her head. "Yes?" "I'm—we went to Millbrook Elementary together. Mrs. Callahan's class." She studied my face with genuine effort, searching. "I'm so sorry," she said finally, kindly. "I don't—I moved away pretty suddenly. It's all a bit hazy." She turned back to her groceries. I stared at the magazine rack and did not move through the line for a long time.

The rusted tin was half-exposed where the frost heave had pushed it up through the garden bed, and Margaret almost stepped on it with her trowel. She sat back on her heels and turned it over in her muddy gloves. There was her own handwriting on the lid, in the careful block letters she'd used as a teenager: *DO NOT OPEN UNTIL 2025.* She was forty-three. She had no memory of burying this. She had no memory of a lot of things from that year, the year after Daniel's accident, when she'd spent most of it moving through her days like water moving through a cracked pipe.

She carried it inside and set it on the kitchen table without opening it. Made coffee she didn't drink. Sat down and got up twice. The lid was stuck with rust and she had to use a butter knife to pry it open, and even then she hesitated with both palms flat on the table, looking at the ceiling for a moment the way you look at something other than the thing that's right in front of you.

Inside: a folded note in her handwriting, a friendship bracelet in blue and yellow, and a photograph of herself at sixteen with her arms around a girl named Petra. Petra, who had told Margaret a secret that summer and made her promise not to tell anyone. Margaret had told everyone. Not out of cruelty—she'd been scared, she'd been young, she hadn't understood what that kind of secret cost a person. She understood now. The note inside the tin just said: *You need to find her and say it out loud.* She'd apparently known, even then, that a reckoning was coming. She just hadn't been brave enough yet to go through with it.

She turned the photograph over. On the back, in different handwriting—Petra's—it said simply: *I still hope you will.* Which meant Petra had known about the tin. Which meant Petra had been waiting. Margaret's throat tightened and she pressed her fingers hard against her mouth and stared at the bracelet for a long time, at the careful knots, at all that patient, faded blue.

---

I found it while I was digging out the old fence posts at my dad's place, the weekend we were finally clearing it out to sell. Solid ground most of the way down, and then my shovel hit something metallic, and I thought: pipe, conduit, nothing. But it wasn't. It was a plastic container about the size of a shoebox, sealed with electrical tape that had gone brittle and gray, and written across the top in black marker in handwriting I recognized as mine: *TIME CAPSULE — RYAN AND COREY — AGE 12.*

I sat down in the dirt next to the hole I'd made. I didn't open it right away. I just sat there with the container in my lap while the September wind moved through what was left of the grass, and I thought about Corey Chen, who I hadn't spoken to in twenty-six years. Not since the day I let his older brother believe it was Corey who'd broken into the Mackenzies' garage, when we'd both known it was me. I'd said nothing. Worse than nothing. I'd let the silence settle and harden like concrete, and Corey had taken the consequences from his family—a strict family, a family for whom that kind of thing mattered enormously—and I had ridden my bike home and eaten dinner.

My hands weren't cooperating with the electrical tape. I kept pulling at it wrong, getting nowhere. I had to stop and breathe twice.

Inside there were baseball cards, a folded map we'd drawn of our "territory," two quarters, and a note in Corey's handwriting that said: *If we find this when we're old, we'll probably be best friends still, living next door, and we'll laugh.* I read it three times. The wind picked up and one of the baseball cards shifted in my hand and I grabbed it before it could go anywhere, holding on too tight, the way you hold something when you've already let go of it once and you know you can't afford to again.

Maya refreshed the notification on her phone three times, staring at the image someone had tagged her in. A stranger's forearm, photographed in afternoon light, bearing her illustration—the one she'd posted without permission from the original photographer whose landscape had been the reference. The tattoo was meticulous, the lines clean, the shading almost better than her digital original. She should have messaged the photographer before this went viral. She'd told herself she would. That was fourteen months ago. Now the post had four thousand likes and comments calling the work "breathtaking," and she was sitting completely still at her drafting table, not drawing, just looking at the wall.

She typed and deleted a response to the tattoo photo six times. Something kept stopping her fingers before she hit send. The photographer's account was still active—she'd checked, more than once, in the way you check something you're hoping will somehow have disappeared. His portfolio was full of that same golden-hour landscape work, the kind that took patience and equipment and years of developing an eye. She'd cropped his name out of the file before she uploaded it. She hadn't thought about what that meant, not carefully enough. She put the phone face-down on the table and pressed her fingertips hard against the wood grain until the tips went white.

The stranger with the tattoo had commented: *I've been saving up for this for two years. It means everything to me.* Maya read that line four times. Her coffee went cold beside her. Outside her window the city was loud and ordinary, indifferent to the fact that she couldn't seem to make herself stand up and do the one simple thing that would begin to fix this. She pulled her knees to her chest in the drafting chair, making herself small, and stared at the photographer's name in her browser search bar, her cursor blinking in the empty message field, waiting.

I saw it at the farmers market, of all places. The woman was reaching across a vegetable stall and her sleeve rode up, and there it was—my fox sketch, the one I drew in 2019 and sold the rights to a client I'd since lost touch with. Not a copy. My exact line work, every hesitation mark in the brush, the slightly uneven ear I'd always meant to fix. I stood there holding a bag of apples, not moving, while she paid for her tomatoes and walked away without ever knowing I existed.

The thing is, I know exactly where I went wrong. I was new to freelancing and I'd signed a contract I hadn't read thoroughly, and when I found out the client had licensed the image further, to a tattoo flash sheet distributor, I said nothing because I needed the reference and I needed the goodwill and I told myself the original photographer—I'd worked from a stock photo I later discovered wasn't cleared for commercial art—would never find out. Two birds, one convenient silence. I've had four years to send that email. I keep a draft of it, still unsent, in a folder I've labeled with a date that keeps not arriving. My hands were cold all the way home from the market, even in the sun, even with the apples warm from sitting out in July.

Last week I got a commission request with a budget bigger than anything I've made in a year. I stared at it for a long time before I answered. Before I did, I opened that draft folder. The email was still there, composed and waiting, addressed to a photographer named Carl Briggs whose work I'd borrowed without asking and whose name I'd never once said out loud to anyone. I read the draft through. I changed nothing. I still didn't send it. I accepted the commission instead and told myself next week, and I've been sleeping badly ever since, the fox sketch appearing in that half-awake space between midnight and morning, the lines too perfect, the ear still slightly wrong.

Marcus stood at the edge of his driveway, staring at the rectangle of oil stain where his brother's Civic used to sit. He'd called the towing company himself — that was the part he kept circling back to. Darius had asked to park there for just two weeks while his apartment situation sorted itself out. Marcus had said yes. Then Sandra had complained about the driveway looking cluttered, and he'd waited until Darius was at his night shift to make the call. He'd told himself it was practical. He'd told himself Darius would understand. He stood there now with his phone in his hand, unable to press his brother's name, watching a neighbor's sprinkler tick back and forth across the lawn like nothing had happened.

Darius's voicemail picked up on the first try, which meant his phone was off, which meant he was still at work. Marcus listened to the whole greeting before hanging up without speaking. He went inside and poured himself a glass of water he didn't drink. Sandra was upstairs, and he couldn't bring himself to go up. He sat at the kitchen table instead, turning the house key over and over in his palm — the spare key he'd made for Darius last winter, the one with the little blue cap on it. He'd been so pleased with himself for making it. He set it flat on the table and pressed his thumb down on it until the metal edge left a mark.

The tow yard closed at nine. Marcus looked it up three times, as if the hours might change. He did the math on what it would cost to get the car out — the hook fee, the daily storage, the mileage charge — and he already had his wallet open before he'd consciously decided anything. He could have it back in the driveway before Darius finished his shift. He could pretend the whole thing was a mistake, a mix-up with the city, something bureaucratic and impersonal. His hand hovered over his wallet for a long moment. Then he took out all the cash he had and counted it twice, very slowly, on the kitchen table.

I keep telling myself I had a reason. The thing is, I did have a reason — Craig hadn't paid rent in two months, and every time I brought it up he looked at me with those eyes like I was the one being unreasonable. It was my house. It was my driveway. I'd been perfectly within my rights to have his car towed while he was at work. I'd looked up the local ordinance and everything. I'd even taken photographs of the car as documentation, like I was building a legal case, like I needed armor for something I was supposedly allowed to do.

I'm standing in the kitchen when I hear his key in the lock, and I realize my shoulders have been up around my ears for the last hour. He comes in and goes quiet when he notices the driveway through the back window. I watch his face go through several things before it settles somewhere flat and tired. "Did you call them?" he asks, and his voice is very even, which is somehow worse than if he'd shouted. I say yes and start to explain about the lease agreement, about the second notice I'd sent, about the photograph evidence I'd compiled. The words come out faster than I mean them to, clipping over each other.

Craig sits down at the counter and puts his face in his hands. Not dramatically — just the way someone sits when they're trying to do the math on a very bad day. I push a piece of paper toward him without saying anything. I'd already called the yard and paid the release fee, and the address and confirmation number are written down in my handwriting, clean and careful, like good penmanship might mean something. He looks at the paper for a while without picking it up. I go to the refrigerator and open it and stand there staring into it for no reason, letting the cold air out.

The letter from the administration sat open on Marcus's kitchen table, its letterhead embossed with the college seal he'd walked past every day for four years. "Due to insufficient enrollment and funding, Harwell College will close its doors at the end of the academic year." He read it three times, then folded it neatly and put it back in the envelope. He'd been on the alumni board two years ago when they'd asked him to lead the fundraising campaign. He'd declined, said he was too busy, said his consulting firm was going through a transition. The truth was he'd been embarrassed by Harwell lately—too small, not prestigious enough for the clients he was trying to impress. He'd quietly removed it from his LinkedIn profile and started just saying he'd "studied in the northeast." Now he kept straightening the letter on the table, aligning it with the edge of the wood, then straightening it again.

I heard about Aldenmoor closing from my old roommate Priya, who texted me a news article with no message attached. Just the link. I set my phone face-down on my desk and didn't pick it up for twenty minutes. Professor Holt had called me personally three years ago—I still have the voicemail somewhere—asking if I'd be willing to speak publicly about my experience there, maybe do an interview with a journalist writing a piece about small liberal arts colleges worth attending. I'd been in the middle of getting my master's at a university with an actual reputation, and I remember thinking, with a clarity that now turns my stomach, that associating myself with Aldenmoor again would only make me look small. I never called him back. The article Priya sent says enrollment dropped sharply starting around four years ago and never recovered. I'm aware, as I sit here, that I've been holding my breath for a very long time, and I don't seem to be able to let it out.

Marina found the cookbook at the airport newsstand, killing time before her flight. The cover was glossy and expensive-looking, the author's name embossed in gold: *Chef Renard's Heritage Kitchen*. She almost walked past it. Then a photograph on the back stopped her cold — a terracotta dish filled with a lamb and preserved lemon stew, the exact amber color, the exact scattering of green olives, the exact way the sauce pooled at the edges. Her hands went numb around the book's spine. That was her grandmother's stew. Her grandmother's recipe, which she had written out word for word, with all its handwritten margin notes about the spice ratios, and slid under Philippe Renard's apartment door four years ago when he was struggling and she felt sorry for him and couldn't think of a better way to help a man she'd loved briefly and badly.

She turned to page forty-seven. *Agneau Renard*, it was called. Renard's lamb. There was a full-page spread and a small paragraph about how the recipe had been "inspired by a journey through North Africa" and "refined over years of experimentation." She read the paragraph three times. Her carry-on bag sat on the floor beside her, and she realized she'd been gripping the shelf for balance without knowing it. The fluorescent lights of the newsstand felt very bright.

She bought the book. She told herself it was because she needed proof, something concrete to hold. On the plane she opened it to page forty-seven and then closed it again and put it in the seat pocket and stared at the clouds. She had never told her grandmother that she'd given the recipe away. Her grandmother was eighty-one years old and lived alone in Lyon and still cooked that stew every Sunday, convinced it was a secret that had traveled with the family for three generations. Marina pressed her knuckles against her mouth and kept them there for a long time.

I watched the cooking segment from my couch with a blanket pulled up to my chin even though it wasn't cold. The host was introducing Delilah Cross, who had a restaurant in Nashville now, a James Beard nomination, a memoir coming out in the spring. The camera panned over a cast-iron skillet, a batter being poured, and I recognized it the way you recognize a face from a long time ago — something in the body before the mind catches up. Brown butter cornbread with cardamom and orange zest. My mother's recipe. My recipe, technically, since she'd given it to me when I moved out, handwritten on a card with a coffee stain in the corner, and I'd shared it at a potluck seven years ago where Delilah had been standing right next to me at the counter asking questions about the orange. I'd told her everything. I'd been flattered that she was interested.

I turned the television off and then turned it back on and then turned it off again for good. The remote control felt strange in my hand. I went to the kitchen and stood at the counter for a while, not doing anything in particular. Delilah and I weren't close — we were potluck friends, peripheral people in each other's lives — and she probably told herself the story differently now, the way you do when something becomes yours through repetition and success and the passage of time. I could almost understand it. What I couldn't figure out was what to do with the fact that my mother had cried a little when she pressed that card into my hands, told me it was the most important thing she had to give me, that food is how we stay connected to the people we've lost. I went back to the couch. I sat down. I thought about calling my mother. I did not call my mother.

Marina found the article by accident, three weeks after moving in. She'd been searching the address for pizza delivery options when the headline surfaced: *Family of Four Found Dead in Riverside Home, 2019*. She read it twice, then set her phone face-down on the kitchen counter. The kitchen counter where she'd been chopping vegetables not twenty minutes ago. She knew, of course she knew, that she should have done a more thorough search before buying. Her realtor had mentioned the price drop in passing — "motivated sellers" — and Marina had nodded along and signed quickly because the neighborhood was good and the mortgage was manageable and she hadn't asked why. She stood now with her back against the counter, arms crossed tight over her ribs, staring at the wall where the article said it had happened.

The neighbors had tried to tell her, in their way. Old Mr. Pratt from across the street had watched her unload boxes in July without waving back, and the woman with the retriever had crossed to the other side every morning since. Marina had assumed she was unfriendly. She'd assumed a lot of things. She picked up her phone again and looked at the faces in the article — a mother, a father, two children in matching striped shirts — and then set it face-down again and stood very still in the room where they had last been alive. Her dinner was still on the cutting board. She didn't go back to it.

I've been avoiding the hallway for four days now. That's where Detective Reyes pointed when she came by, not to question me about anything — just to inform me, she said, standard procedure, the department liked to notify new owners. She stood on my porch in November rain and told me what had happened here, fourteen years ago, before I was even looking at houses. I kept nodding. I kept saying "okay" in a flat voice that didn't sound like mine. After she left I stood in the open doorway for a long time letting the cold come in.

The thing is, my neighbor Constance tried to mention it when I first moved in. She'd started to say something at the mailboxes — "I suppose you know about the history of the place" — and I'd cut her off, cheerfully, the way I do when I don't want bad news to slow me down. I'd said something about how every old house has its stories and then I'd carried my packages inside. I remember her expression. I didn't let myself think about it then. I'm thinking about it now, sitting on the floor of my own living room, back against the couch, unable to look down the hall, running through every moment I decided not to ask a question I should have asked.

The name on the classroom door stopped me cold. Mrs. Alderman. I stood in the hallway of Pinecrest Elementary with my daughter's hand in mine, reading that name tag over and over while Cora tugged at my fingers and asked why we weren't going in. I hadn't thought about Mrs. Alderman in twenty-three years. Not once, not seriously. She'd written me a college recommendation letter—a long one, my mother had said, really went to bat for you—and I'd never sent a thank-you note. I'd meant to. I'd thought about it the way you think about a dentist appointment, always just around the corner, always pushable. The acceptance letter came, and then the summer came, and then I was gone.

Inside, Mrs. Alderman was arranging small chairs with the careful attention of someone who had done this ten thousand times. Her hair was white now and she moved more slowly, but when she looked up and smiled at Cora, I recognized the expression immediately. Warm, unhurried, already curious about this particular child. I felt something curl up and harden behind my sternum.

"And what's your name?" she asked, crouching down to Cora's level.

"Cora." My daughter pressed into my leg, suddenly shy. "What's yours?"

"Mrs. Alderman." She looked up at me then, politely expectant, the way teachers look at parents they haven't met yet. Nothing in her face. No recognition, no stored-up grievance. Just openness.

I told her my name. I watched her eyes. There was a flicker—something small and searching—and then she smiled again and said, "Well, Cora, I have a feeling we're going to have a wonderful year." My hand found the back of my neck without my meaning it to, fingers pressing hard against the skin there. On the drive home, Cora chattered about the classroom hamster, and I answered her in single syllables, my throat tight around words I had no idea how to begin.

Marcus had been holding the school registration paperwork for ten minutes without filling it out. The kitchen table was covered in forms—emergency contacts, medical history, photo permissions—and one sheet near the top listed the third-grade teachers. He'd read the second name and then set the stack down and stood at the window with his arms crossed. Mr. Delaney. The same Mr. Delaney who'd kept Marcus after school for two hours one Tuesday in 1998 to help him practice long division, who had called Marcus's mother not to report a problem but to tell her that her son had real potential, who had written BELIEVE IN YOURSELF in red marker on the top of a test Marcus had failed and then passed and then finally aced.

He'd repaid this by telling the other boys in his class that Mr. Delaney wore the same sweater every Friday because he was poor. It had been a stupid, careless cruelty, the kind that third-graders deploy without understanding the weight of it. Except Marcus had understood, even then. He'd seen something flicker across Mr. Delaney's face in the hallway the following Monday, overheard two other teachers whispering near the copy room, and he'd known with perfect clarity that the words had traveled back to where they'd come from.

He picked up a pen and put it back down. His son Jonah was seven, loud and generous and already the kind of kid who collected strays—broken toys, lonely classmates, injured birds in the backyard. Jonah would love Mr. Delaney. That certainty settled in Marcus's chest like a stone in still water.

He thought about requesting a different classroom. He thought about it seriously for almost a minute, his pen hovering over the preference box at the bottom of the form. Then he set the pen down again and went to refill his coffee, standing over the sink with the mug in both hands, not drinking it, just holding onto something warm.

Marisol kept refreshing the conference room projector, pretending to troubleshoot a connection that wasn't actually broken. Across the table, Dr. Feng was laying out his printed slides in neat rows, completely absorbed, and she could see from six feet away that his diagrams were nearly identical to hers. Same methodology. Same river delta. Same three-year data set. She had known about his preliminary work eighteen months ago—a footnote in a journal, easy to miss, or easy to tell yourself you'd missed. She'd told herself that twice, actually. The refreshing. The fussing with cables. Anything to delay the moment when the room filled up and both presentations began. Her mouth had gone dry in a way that no amount of water from the pitcher in front of her seemed to fix.

I keep glancing at Nina's sketchbook, which is sitting open on the studio table between us. She found mine first, flipped through it the way you do with a friend's work—warmly, expectantly—and then went very quiet. Our canvases aren't just similar. They're the same composition, the same color blocking, the same fractured figure emerging from water. We've been best friends for eleven years and I know she's thinking coincidence, parallel inspiration, the beautiful accident of two minds converging. I'm the one who saw her abandoned rough draft in the recycling bin at the co-op eight months ago, smoothed it out, and kept it under my desk. I haven't touched that draft in weeks, told myself I'd long since moved past it into my own territory. My hands are in my lap now, pressing together hard enough to whiten the knuckles, and I can't quite bring myself to meet her eyes.

Maya refreshed her email for the third time that morning, watching the announcement load again as if the words might change. Professor Aldrich was leaving. Effective end of semester. She set her phone face-down on the desk and stared at the ceiling of her apartment. Last spring, she'd been copied on an email she wasn't supposed to see—a department chair at another university, reaching out to Aldrich about an open position. Maya had mentioned it, offhand, to a friend in the program. Just venting, she'd told herself. By August, she'd heard from two different people that Aldrich had been passed over for the endowed chair she'd been promised, that someone on the committee had raised concerns about her "outside interests." Maya stood up, walked to the kitchen, poured a glass of water, and poured it down the drain without drinking it. She'd been averaging about four hours of sleep a night since September. She wasn't sure when that had started exactly, but she knew the date she'd opened that email.

The thesis itself was nearly done, two chapters from completion after three years of work Aldrich had shaped almost as much as Maya had. She opened the acknowledgments section now, the cursor blinking at the end of a sentence that thanked Aldrich for her "generous guidance." Maya had written that phrase months ago when it felt sufficient. She highlighted it, then unhighlighted it, then highlighted it again. Her shoulders had been knotted since she woke up. Outside her window, the campus was emptying for winter break, students dragging luggage across the wet pavement, and she watched them move without really seeing them, her thumb pressing hard into the muscle below her collarbone the way she'd been doing lately without noticing she was doing it.

I didn't tell him right away. That's the thing I keep coming back to. Professor Reyes had introduced me to Dr. Fontaine at the conference in October—walked me over himself, hand on my shoulder, said "this student is the best thing to come through my program in fifteen years." And Dr. Fontaine had offered me a position on his research team, a fully funded one, contingent on transferring to his institution. I'd said yes by November. I'd accepted the offer, started the paperwork, signed documents with the new university's letterhead—and I said nothing to Reyes. Kept meeting with him every other Thursday. Kept taking his notes on my third chapter. Kept nodding when he talked about plans for the spring.

The announcement came from the department office before I could find the right moment, or whatever I'd been telling myself I was waiting for. Reyes was leaving. He'd accepted a position across the country. I read it standing in the hallway outside the seminar room where I'd spent three years arguing about research methodology, and I had to put my hand flat against the wall because my legs felt genuinely unreliable. Someone said my name and I turned around with what must have been a terrible expression because they immediately asked if I was okay. I said I was fine. I said it twice. Later I sat in my car for forty minutes in the parking structure, engine off, watching the fluorescent light above me flicker, going over every Thursday meeting in reverse, counting all the times he'd told me I had a future here.

Marcus read the rejection email twice, then set his phone face-down on the kitchen table. His roommate Deon was somewhere in the apartment, and Marcus couldn't bring himself to call out to him. Deon had spent eleven hours helping him write the application essay — eleven hours over two weekends, ordering cheap pizza, rereading drafts until the words stopped making sense. Marcus had let him believe every word of it was true. The part about his single mother working double shifts, that was real. The part about the community mentorship program, the one he'd supposedly led for three semesters — that was not. He'd attended twice. He pressed the back of his hand against his mouth and stared at the refrigerator, where Deon had taped a printout that said *future scholar* as a joke that wasn't entirely a joke.

The rejection itself wasn't what held him there. It was the reason listed in the second paragraph — "inconsistencies found during verification of extracurricular involvement." He read that line four times. His throat felt like something was lodged in it. Down the hallway he could hear Deon humming while he cooked, completely unbothered, completely unaware. Marcus turned his phone over and opened Deon's contact, then closed it. He went and stood in the bathroom instead, running cold water over his wrists until his roommate called out that dinner was ready, and he had to answer in a voice he hoped sounded normal.

I got the letter on a Tuesday, which felt wrong somehow — bad news should arrive on a Monday, not halfway through the week when you've already invested in things going okay. I stood at the mailbox for longer than I needed to, reading the same sentence over and over. Application unsuccessful. The woman I'd used as a reference — Professor Aldridge, who'd supervised my volunteer hours — had sent me a text that same morning asking how everything was going. I hadn't replied yet. I still haven't.

She wrote me a recommendation based on thirty hours I told her I'd completed. I did maybe twelve. I kept meaning to finish the rest, kept telling myself next weekend, next weekend, until suddenly the application deadline was there and I just — padded the number. It seemed so small at the time. Professor Aldridge had talked about me in department meetings. I know this because another professor mentioned it once, offhand, saying she'd spoken highly of me. I put the letter in my bag and walked back across campus with my hood up even though it wasn't cold, shoulders drawn in, avoiding the building where her office was on the second floor.

The ring was in a glass case between a trumpet and a collection of pocket watches. Marcus recognized it immediately—the small oval sapphire flanked by two seed pearls, the thin gold band his grandmother had worn for sixty-one years until they'd buried her with it. Except they hadn't buried her with it. He had taken it from the hospital bag of her belongings three weeks after the funeral, told his mother it must have been lost in the chaos of those final days. His mother had cried quietly at the kitchen table and said she supposed these things happened. Marcus stood now with his forehead nearly touching the glass, unable to straighten up. His reflection stared back at him from the dark case, superimposed over the ring.

The pawnbroker was watching him from behind the counter. "That one catch your eye?" he asked.

Marcus's mouth opened and nothing came out for a moment. He had pawned it for four hundred dollars. He had needed the money for a deposit on an apartment he'd ended up not taking. The four hundred dollars was long spent, and his mother still kept a photo of his grandmother on the mantle, the sapphire visible on her hand, and sometimes touched the frame when she thought no one was watching. "How much?" he finally said, his voice coming out strange and thin.

He paid six hundred and twenty dollars. He drove to his mother's house without calling ahead and sat in the driveway for eleven minutes before going inside. When she opened the door and saw what was in his palm, she covered her mouth with both hands. He stood on the porch step, one inch shorter than her for once, and could not think of a single word that was adequate.

I've been in this pawn shop before. I told myself I was just cutting through the strip mall to reach the pharmacy, but I haven't moved toward the door in ten minutes. I'm standing in front of a rotating display of jewelry near the window, and there it is on the third tier, tagged at $180: Grandma Phyllis's ring. The thin braided gold band. The tiny diamond chip she used to let me touch when I was small, pressing my fingertip against it, telling me that was real love in there, compressed.

My cousin Derek pawned it. I know this because I was there. I drove him here, waited in the car, told myself it was his inheritance too, told myself Grandma would have wanted him to keep the lights on in his apartment, told myself a lot of things while the engine ran and the radio played. Derek has been clean for fourteen months now. He calls me on Sundays. Last week he asked me if I thought we should tell Aunt Rosa about the ring someday, and I said I didn't know, and then I changed the subject.

I pick the ring up off the display without asking. It's lighter than I remember, or maybe I'm just older. The woman behind the counter quotes me the price and I hand her my card without negotiating. My hands are completely steady, which surprises me. I thought they would shake. I put the ring in my jacket pocket and stand there another moment, my hand pressed flat against the outside of the pocket, before I walk to my car and sit there trying to decide who I'm buying this back for—Derek, or Aunt Rosa, or the version of myself that stayed in the car.

The manila folder sat open on Margaret's kitchen table, its contents fanned out like an accusation. She'd known for three weeks now—ever since the hospital called to say there'd been an administrative error, that her records had been filed under another woman's name for the better part of a year. A woman named Carol Voss, sixty-two, who had apparently spent four months being treated for the wrong thyroid condition. Margaret had heard the intake coordinator's careful, professional voice explaining it and had said nothing about her own role in the confusion. She'd filled out the intake form carelessly, transposed two digits in her date of birth, then never corrected it when the nurse read it back wrong. She hadn't wanted to slow things down. She'd had somewhere to be.

She picked up her coffee mug and set it down without drinking. The hospital had sent a form letter, very neutral in tone, explaining that both patients would be contacted and records corrected. It did not say what the misdiagnosis had cost Carol Voss. Margaret had Googled the name twice, stopped herself both times before hitting search, then done it anyway at two in the morning, finding a Facebook profile with a cover photo of a woman at a lakeside birthday party, surrounded by grandchildren, a paper crown tilted on her gray hair. She'd closed the laptop and not opened it since.

Her daughter called that evening to chat about nothing in particular, and Margaret answered every question in a voice that came out flatter than she intended. "You okay, Mom?" her daughter asked. Margaret said she was tired. She stood at the kitchen window after they hung up, watching the neighbor's dog wander across the yard, and pressed her thumbnail into the soft skin of her palm until it left a white crescent mark.

I work the night shift at the records department, have for eleven years. I know the filing system better than I know my own apartment. So when Dr. Okafor called me personally last Tuesday—not my supervisor, me—to ask how a patient's chart had ended up cross-linked with another man's account for over eight months, I already knew what had happened before she finished the sentence. I had been in a hurry. Last January, training a new temp, trying to do two things at once, I'd merged an intake file without double-checking the ID numbers. I had meant to go back and verify. I had written myself a note. The note was still in my desk drawer; I found it that same afternoon, a yellow sticky with the patient's last name in my own handwriting and a question mark beside it.

Dr. Okafor was not unkind. She said these things happen, that we'd be reviewing process improvements, that no one was being blamed. I nodded and wrote things down and said "of course" several times. What I did not say was that I'd had the instinct to check and chosen not to. The man whose records were mixed up had apparently been flagged for a cardiac screening he didn't need, while the other patient's abnormal bloodwork sat buried in the wrong account. I don't know yet what that delay meant for him. Nobody's told me, and I haven't asked, because I can't make myself form the question out loud.

I've started taking the long route to the break room so I don't pass the cardiac ward. I eat lunch at my desk now. Yesterday my coworker Rosa left a birthday cake in the break room and came to find me personally, and I followed her down the hall and sang happy birthday with everyone else and ate a piece of cake and the whole time I could feel something pressing against the inside of my ribs like a fist that wouldn't open. That night I sat in my car in the parking garage for twenty minutes before I could make myself drive home.

Maya's phone felt heavier than usual as she scrolled back through her Instagram posts. Mr. Hargrove. His profile picture was small but unmistakable—the same gray cardigan he wore every Thursday. She'd accepted the follow request three weeks ago without checking the name, assuming it was someone from her chemistry class. Her stomach dropped as she kept scrolling. There was the post calling his unit on the French Revolution "the most boring two weeks of my entire academic life." There was the one where she'd photoshopped his face onto a sloth. Forty-seven likes. She put the phone face-down on her desk and stared at the ceiling, pressing her palms flat against the wood as though she needed the surface to stay upright.

I've been raising my hand in every class for a week now. Extra participation, extra effort, showing up ten minutes early to help arrange the desks—things I've never done once in three years of Ms. Chen's English class. She commented on it yesterday, told me she was "pleasantly surprised," and I had to look down at my notebook so she wouldn't see my face. I'd spent the better part of last semester posting about how she assigned too much homework, how her feedback was useless, how she clearly hadn't read a novel published after 1987. When I saw her username in my followers list on Tuesday, I sat in my car for twenty minutes before I could go inside. I've started a new private account. I haven't touched the old one. I just can't bring myself to delete those posts yet—I keep opening the app and closing it again without doing anything.

She found the boxes in the attic while clearing out her mother's house, three weeks after the funeral. Forty-seven years of living compressed into labeled cardboard, and there in the corner, stacked four high, were boxes marked with her own name. Inside: every spelling test, every crayon drawing, every book report she'd ever brought home. A macaroni necklace in a sandwich bag. A Mother's Day card from second grade that read "Your the best mom" in uneven letters. She stood with the card in both hands and couldn't move.

The last real conversation they'd had was two Christmases ago, cut short because she'd needed to get back for a work deadline that, when she checked her files later, turned out to have been moved to January anyway. She remembered her mother standing in the doorway in her slippers, waving, getting smaller in the rearview mirror. She'd told herself there would be other visits.

There was a fifth-grade science project at the bottom of one box, a poster about the water cycle she'd been certain she'd thrown away herself. Her mother must have retrieved it from the trash. Must have smoothed it flat and folded it carefully and decided it was worth keeping. The poster had a small tear along one edge, the kind you'd get pulling something out of a wastebasket.

She sat down on the attic floor among the boxes and pressed the heels of her hands hard into her sternum, as though she could push something back down that had already risen too far.

I called her on a Tuesday, which I almost never do. Usually it's Sundays, when I'm already feeling obligated about a hundred other things, and the call ends up being fifteen minutes of surface talk before I manufacture a reason to hang up. But I'd been going through her spare bedroom, helping her downsize, and I'd found them — binders, actual three-ring binders, one per school year, each assignment slid into a plastic sleeve. My handwriting changing grade by grade. My name at the top of every page, sometimes spelled wrong in the early ones.

"Mom," I said when she picked up. "The binders."

She got quiet in that way she does, a little embarrassed. Said she knew it was silly.

I told her it wasn't silly. I said that with my mouth, the words smooth and reassuring, while I sat on the edge of her bed and thought about every time I'd rolled my eyes at her calls, every birthday where I'd sent a gift card because it was easier, every visit I'd rescheduled and then rescheduled again until the year just ran out.

There's a report I wrote in fourth grade, tucked behind a math test. The assignment was to write about your hero. I'd written about a soccer player. I don't even remember his name now. I turned the laminated page over in my hands for a long time after I got off the phone, sitting in the quiet of her house, listening to her move around in the kitchen below me, still here, still keeping things.

Marina kept the old tackle box on the highest shelf in the garage, behind the camping gear. Her daughter Priya hadn't asked about it in weeks, and Marina told herself that was fine, that was good, actually. The last time Priya had asked to go fishing, Marina had said maybe next weekend, and the weekend before that she'd said the same thing. She watched through the kitchen window now as Priya sat alone on the back porch, carefully arranging a row of plastic lures she'd found in a drawer, studying each one with the focused reverence of someone handling artifacts from an ancient civilization.

Priya had started checking out library books about fly-fishing techniques, stacking them in a careful tower on her nightstand. Marina had noticed them three weeks ago and hadn't said anything. She'd started the hobby when she was nine, her own father standing knee-deep in cold water teaching her patience and stillness—two things Marina hadn't managed to pass on to anyone. She touched the spine of one of Priya's books now, running her thumb across the embossed letters, and then set it back down exactly as she'd found it.

"Mom, can you show me how to tie this knot?" Priya appeared in the doorway holding a length of fishing line, her expression careful and hopeful in equal measure. Marina looked at her daughter's hands, at the tangled line looped around small fingers, and felt something hollow open up in her sternum. She had spent eleven years driving to the reservoir alone every spring, leaving before dawn, never once thinking to wake the child sleeping two rooms away. "Come here," Marina said quietly. She sat down at the kitchen table and held out her hand for the line.

Priya's birthday was in two weeks. Marina had already ordered a telescope—something she'd chosen quickly one night after too much wine, clicking through pages looking for something impressive. She lay awake at 2 a.m. now staring at the ceiling fan, her hands folded over her stomach like she was trying to hold something in. Her daughter had never once mentioned stars.

I started painting watercolors when I was twenty-three, back when I lived alone in a studio apartment and had nothing but time and a secondhand set of brushes. For fifteen years it was mine—early Saturday mornings, the apartment quiet, nobody asking anything of me. I kept my supplies in the spare room and closed the door when I wasn't using it, which my wife always understood without asking. I don't think I ever explained why I needed it to be private. I just did.

Noah found the spare room unlocked on a rainy afternoon last October, when he was seven and bored and I was on a work call in the kitchen. I came in to find him standing at my table with a brush in his hand, staring at the unfinished painting on the board. My first instinct—and I can still feel it, something sharp and reflexive—was to say don't touch. I said it. He put the brush down very carefully and didn't look at me.

He started drawing on printer paper after that. Reams of it. Landscapes, mostly—trees and hills and water. His teacher sent home a note in November saying he had a natural eye for composition, whatever that meant for a second-grader. I taped the note to the refrigerator and stood there looking at it for too long, until my coffee went cold.

Last week I bought him his own set of watercolors. Decent ones, not children's quality. I set them on the kitchen table with a small block of watercolor paper and waited for him to come home from school. When he saw them, he looked at me first before he touched anything, checking my face, and that look—that small, uncertain pause before joy—it sits behind my ribs now like a stone I can't dislodge. I pulled out the chair beside him and we painted together until dinner, and I tried to make my voice light and easy while I showed him how to load a brush.

Marcus stood in the back of the small venue with his drink sweating in his hand. On stage, a young woman was playing "Harbor Light" on an acoustic guitar—his song, the one he'd told everyone he wrote alone during a winter in Vermont. Her voice was doing something careful and true with the bridge, the part that Elena had actually written, sitting cross-legged on his kitchen floor at two in the morning with her hair still wet from the shower. Elena, who had trusted him completely. Elena, who had never pushed for credit because she believed in him that much. Marcus set his glass down on a nearby table with more force than he meant to, and a stranger glanced over. He pretended to check his phone.

The crowd was small but attentive, the kind of audience that leaned forward. A man near the front had his eyes closed. Marcus watched him and felt something shift behind his sternum, like a stone rolling over slowly. The young performer hit the final chorus and the melody filled the low-ceilinged room, and Marcus found himself backing toward the exit. His shoulders were up around his ears. He'd given three interviews about that song. Talked about Vermont, about isolation, about the particular quality of winter light. He'd said the words so many times they'd started to feel real. They did not feel real right now. He pushed through the door into the cold air and stood on the sidewalk staring at his own reflection in a darkened shop window across the street.

I heard it from the hallway before I even saw the stage. Someone had tuned a piano to the same slightly flat E I always used, and for a disorienting second I thought I was hearing a recording of myself. Then I rounded the corner into the auditorium and saw her—a teenager, maybe sixteen, playing "What the River Knows" at the school recital with her whole body leaning into it. I'd lifted that chord progression from Danny's demo tape. Danny, who'd died before anything ever came of his music. I'd changed the melody, added lyrics, told myself it was homage, transformation, evolution. I'd told myself this so many times and with such practiced confidence that I'd almost stopped hearing the lie in it.

I sat down in an empty seat near the back row. The girl's mother was filming on a phone two rows ahead of me, and the father was nodding along with his arms folded, proud. My hands were folded in my lap and I became very aware of them, these hands that had accepted awards, signed contracts, smiled in photographs. The girl finished and the room applauded and she stood and bowed with a huge, uncomplicated grin. I clapped too, mechanically, my palms coming together while something cold and specific moved through my chest. On the drive home I kept both hands on the wheel and the radio off and I thought about Danny's sister, who I still had her number for, somewhere, and who I had never once called.

The coffee shop was nearly empty when Priya sat down across from the woman named Claire. They'd agreed to meet after an uncomfortable string of texts, both of them piecing together what Marcus had been doing for the past seven months. Claire was kind about it, which made everything worse. She kept saying things like "it's not your fault" and "he fooled us both," and Priya nodded along while pressing her thumbnail hard into the pad of her index finger, over and over, a small private pain she couldn't stop returning to.

Priya had known. That was the thing she couldn't say out loud. Not everything, but enough. There had been a voicemail she'd heard by accident in February, a woman's voice asking Marcus to call back, something soft and familiar in the tone of it. She'd asked him about it once, lightly, and accepted his explanation the way you accept something you've already decided to believe. Claire was sitting here now, turning a coffee cup in her hands, and she had no idea that Priya had chosen not to look.

"He told me you were his cousin," Claire said finally. "I feel so stupid."

"Don't," Priya said, too quickly. She looked out the window at the street. A man was walking a dog in the rain, unhurried, unbothered. She watched him until he was gone.

On the train home, Priya typed and deleted four different messages to Marcus. She kept thinking about Claire's face when she'd shown Priya a photo of the two of them at a vineyard—the same vineyard where Marcus had proposed a weekend trip to Priya just last month. She put her phone in her bag and held very still as the train moved underground, watching her own reflection in the dark window, not quite meeting her own eyes.

I recognized his jacket first—the olive canvas one with the broken snap on the left pocket that he always meant to fix. It was hanging on the back of a chair at a table where a man I didn't know was sitting, staring at me with the same frozen expression I must have been wearing. His name was Derek, and he introduced himself the way people do when they've already figured out the situation and are mostly just filling silence.

We ended up talking for almost two hours, which I hadn't planned on. Derek was easy to talk to, and that made me feel worse, not better. He had a good laugh. He showed me texts from Jordan—my Jordan, apparently his Jordan too—and I noticed that Jordan had used the same phrases with both of us. Same nicknames. Same jokes about hating mornings. I'd felt so singled out by those small things. I'd repeated some of them to friends as examples of how well Jordan knew me.

Derek kept saying I must have had no idea, and I let him believe that. But I'd seen a second toothbrush once, pushed to the back of the bathroom cabinet, and Jordan had laughed and said it was an old one. I'd wanted to believe it. I wanted to believe everything was what it appeared to be, and I worked hard at it, and I never asked any of the questions I probably should have asked.

When Derek left, he shook my hand and said he hoped I was okay. I said the same back to him and meant it. Then I sat alone with the olive jacket still hanging on the chair behind me, and I ordered another coffee I didn't want, and I tried to figure out which part of this was something that happened to me and which part was something I'd let happen.

The ceremony had been beautiful, and now the reception was in full swing, but Marcus kept finding reasons to hover near the bar. He'd known Claire would be here — of course he'd known — and he'd told himself he was ready. He was not ready. She was standing near the floral centerpiece talking to someone he didn't recognize, laughing at something, and she looked the way she always looked when she didn't know she was being watched: completely unguarded. He ordered another whiskey he didn't need.

He'd ended things over a text message. Fourteen months together, and he'd sent eleven words. He hadn't been able to face her, hadn't been able to say the real reason, which was that he'd already started seeing Priya. He stared at the amber liquid in his glass and rotated it slowly on the bar top, round and round, the ice clicking against the sides.

When Claire finally saw him, she smiled — a careful, measured smile — and crossed the room. "Marcus," she said. He hugged her too quickly and stepped back too fast, knocking his elbow against a chair. "You look great," he said, and his voice came out thin and high, like something stretched. She tilted her head slightly, reading him the way she always could.

They talked about the wedding, about the bride's dress, about how neither of them had tried the salmon. Marcus laughed at the right moments and refilled her champagne when a waiter passed, and the whole time he kept swallowing against a pressure in his throat that wouldn't resolve. She was gracious. She was so gracious. She asked about his work and listened to the answer, and he couldn't stand how easy she was making it.

Later, when the dancing started, he slipped outside and sat on the venue's stone steps alone. The music filtered through the walls. He unlaced one shoe and laced it again, slowly, for no reason at all, just to do something with his hands. A couple laughed somewhere in the parking lot. He pressed the heel of his palm flat against his sternum and held it there, like he was trying to quiet something.

I saw her before she saw me, which I'm not proud of, because my first instinct was to turn around and walk back through the double doors I'd just come through. I didn't, but I thought about it for a full four seconds, standing there in my rented suit with a glass of sparkling water sweating in my hand. Nadia was standing with her sister near the gift table, wearing something dark green, and she was exactly as I remembered her and also somehow more.

The thing is, I know what I did. I borrowed money from her — three thousand dollars, when we were together — and I told her it was for a certification course that would lead to a promotion. It was partially true. The course was real. I just never mentioned the other reason I needed it, which was a debt to a friend I'd been avoiding for two years, a debt that had started to feel dangerous. I paid him. I took the course. I got the promotion. I paid Nadia back every cent, on time, in three installments. But I never told her the real reason, and when she asked, I smiled and said it had all worked out.

We broke up over something unrelated, six months after that. She never knew. She still doesn't know. That's the part that sits wrong — not the borrowing, not even the lie exactly, but the fact that she was so trusting, so completely certain I was telling her the truth, and I let her stay certain.

She spotted me halfway through dinner, waved from across the table arrangement, and I waved back and smiled, and the smile felt like a mask that didn't quite fit. When she came over later to say hello, I stood up too fast and bumped the table. "It's so good to see you," she said, and meant it — I could tell she meant it — and something in my chest went tight and stayed tight. I asked about her work, her family, the drive down. I listened very carefully to every answer. I don't know what I was hoping to find there, some kind of permission maybe, or maybe just the opposite. I walked to the bathroom twice during the reception and both times stood at the sink running cold water over my wrists, staring at my own reflection like it owed me something.

Coach Marren folded the roster sheet in half, then in half again, until it was a small square she couldn't read anymore. Danny Kowalski had been at every single practice since August. Every single one, including the Thursday it rained so hard the field turned to soup and half the other kids went home early. He'd stayed. She'd watched him from under her umbrella, drilling the same footwork pattern over and over in the mud, his cleats making sucking sounds with each step. His form was improving. That was the thing she kept circling back to. His form was genuinely improving.

She'd built her argument in her head a dozen times on the drive over. The team needed speed at the position. Danny worked hard but the gap was too wide to close in one season. These were facts. She repeated them to herself now in the parking lot, engine still running, watching the lights inside the gymnasium. Her hand rested on the door handle and stayed there. She'd coached for eleven years and had never once sat in a parking lot for twenty minutes before a meeting. She cracked the window even though it was cold outside.

Danny's father had called her last week, which was unusual—parents rarely called her directly. He hadn't lobbied for his son or complained. He'd just mentioned, almost carefully, that Danny had started keeping a journal of his progress and read it every night before bed. He'd laughed a little when he said it, the way parents laugh when something touches them more than they expected. She had laughed too, lightly, and then changed the subject.

She got out of the car. The folded roster sheet crinkled in her jacket pocket as she walked and she pressed her hand flat against it to stop the sound.

I've coached youth basketball for six years, and I've made cuts before. I know how to do it. You keep it short, you keep it kind, you don't let it drag. I wrote those principles down on an actual index card my first year and I still have it somewhere in my desk drawer. So when the athletic director told me the roster cap was dropping from fifteen to twelve this season, I told him it would be fine.

Marcus came to tryouts with his older brother's worn-out Nikes, two sizes too big, stuffed with extra socks in the toes. I noticed because I have a habit of checking footwear—you can tell a lot about a kid's foundation from how he plants. He was slow. He had a hitch in his shot that I don't think anyone had ever bothered to correct, and it was going to take real time to unlearn. But when I ran the scramble drill, the one that usually breaks newcomers down, he just kept getting up. Other kids bent over, hands on knees. Marcus stood straight and asked if we were going again.

My coffee has been sitting on the counter for an hour. I poured it and then walked away and now it's cold and I keep almost picking it up and putting it down without drinking it. His name is at the bottom of the cut list. I wrote it there myself, in pen, which now seems like an absurd choice. There's a pen on the counter next to the coffee. I keep looking at it and looking away, as though it did something wrong.

Marisol's hands went still over the kitchen sink when her sister called her into the living room. Spread across the coffee table were a dozen of little Camila's drawings, the ones their mother had been collecting since Camila started talking about her "special friend." The friend had a name — Pip — and wore a striped hat and had one eye that was a different color than the other. Marisol stared at the drawings for a long time without saying anything. She had invented Pip when she was six years old, in the year after their father left, and she had never told a single soul. Not her sister, not their mother, not any therapist she had seen as an adult. She had kept Pip entirely to herself like something precious and then, when she was nine, had decided Pip wasn't real and had been furious at herself for ever believing otherwise. She had driven that fury like a nail into the ground.

She picked up one of Camila's drawings and held it at arm's length. In the picture, Pip sat beside a small girl who was crying. The detail that made Marisol's throat close was the hand — Pip's hand, four fingers and a little oval thumb, drawn in green crayon resting on the girl's shoulder. Marisol had drawn that same hand hundreds of times as a child, in notebooks she had eventually ripped up and thrown away so no one would see. She set the drawing back down with exaggerated care, the way you put something fragile on a high shelf.

Her sister was saying something about talking to Camila's teacher, about whether this kind of imaginative play was developmentally normal at five. Marisol nodded at intervals. She had one hand pressed flat against her sternum without realizing it, holding pressure there against something that felt like a bruise. She thought about the notebooks she'd destroyed. She had destroyed them because she was ashamed, and she had stayed ashamed of that whole soft, lonely period of her life for twenty-five years. And now Pip was sitting in her niece's drawings, patient and striped-hatted, one eye blue and one eye brown, still keeping company with a child who was crying alone.

"Mari, are you even listening?" her sister said.

"Sorry." She picked up her coffee cup though she didn't want any. "I think it's fine," she said, her voice coming out slightly too controlled. "I think Camila is fine. She's probably just — I think it's fine." She took a sip she didn't need and looked out the window at the yard where Camila was playing, and she stood there holding the warm cup in both hands like something she had no business holding.

---

I haven't been back to my brother's house in almost two years. I told him it was the distance, the cost of flights, the demands of my job. He believed me, or pretended to. When he sent me the photos of Nadia's artwork for her school portfolio, I was sitting in bed on a Sunday morning with nowhere I needed to be.

I scrolled through them quickly at first — bright, chaotic kindergarten paintings, the kind where a horse looks like an oval and the sun has a face. I was smiling. Then I stopped scrolling.

The figure in the corner of the painting was tall and thin with very long arms, and it was the color I used to call "almost purple" as a child, a muddy violet I made by mixing crayons together. I used to draw this figure over and over in the margins of my school notebooks. I called it Lendon. It had no mouth, only eyes, and I told myself that was because Lendon communicated without words, the way I wished I could. I had invented Lendon the year my parents moved us across the country and I started at a new school where I understood immediately that I would not fit in. I kept Lendon secret because even at seven I knew it was the kind of thing that could make things worse.

My phone screen went dark from inactivity. I tapped it back on. Nadia was in the painting too, I realized — a small figure standing near Lendon with one arm raised. Her arm reached toward Lendon's long one. I put the phone face-down on the mattress and sat there with my knees pulled up.

The thing is, I know exactly why I stopped visiting. It wasn't the distance or the money. It was because of a fight my brother and I had two Christmases ago, something I said about his parenting that I cannot walk back no matter how many ways I have rehearsed it. He forgave me, he said so, and somehow that made it worse. And then I started finding reasons not to show up, and then the reasons became a habit, and then two years went by, and somewhere in that gap my niece, who I have met exactly four times in her life, started drawing a figure the almost-purple color of loneliness.

I picked the phone back up and looked at the painting again for a long time. Lendon's eyes, painted in careful dark blue, looked back.

I opened a new message to my brother. I typed: *I want to come visit. Soon. If that's okay.* I deleted "if that's okay." I put it back. I stared at the message until the screen went dark again.

The pocket watch sat on the kitchen table between them, catching the afternoon light. My uncle kept turning it over in his hands, the same way he'd done since I was a child watching him wind it every Sunday. "Your grandfather wore this the day he came through Ellis Island," he said quietly. I nodded and looked out the window. I hadn't told him yet that I'd already spoken to the antique dealer on Marsh Street, already gotten an appraisal, already mentally spent the money on the down payment. Three weeks ago it had seemed so simple. Now I couldn't quite meet his eyes, and I kept finding reasons to refill my coffee cup, to straighten things on the counter, to be anywhere but in that chair across from him.

Margaret had been rehearsing the conversation for days, but standing in her mother's living room with the quilt folded over her arm, every prepared sentence dissolved.
